title.zh: 卷五十二 崔駰列傳
title.raw: 卷五十二 崔駰列傳 Volume 52: Biography of Cui Yin

sentences.zh: 崔駰字亭伯，涿郡安平人也。

translations.literal: Cui Yin, courtesy name Tingbo, was a man of Anping in Zhuo Commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Yin, style Tingbo, came from Anping in Zhuo Commandery.

sentences.zh: 高祖父朝，昭帝時為幽州從事，諫刺史無與燕剌王通。

translations.literal: His great-great-grandfather Chao, in the time of Emperor Zhao, served as an aide in You Province and remonstrated with the inspector, telling him not to associate with the Prince of Yan, Prince Li.
translations.idiomatic: His great-great-grandfather Cui Chao, under Emperor Zhao, held a staff post in You Province; he urged the provincial inspector not to deal with Prince Dan of Yan, the so-called 'pricked' prince.

sentences.zh: 及剌王敗，擢為侍御史。

translations.literal: When Prince Li was defeated, he was promoted to palace attendant censor.
translations.idiomatic: After Prince Dan's fall, he was raised to imperial clerk censor.

sentences.zh: [一]生子舒，歷四郡太守，所在有能名。

translations.literal: [Note one] He begat Shu, who served as grand administrator of four commanderies in succession and wherever he went had a reputation for competence.
translations.idiomatic: He fathered Cui Shu, who successively governed four commanderies as grand administrator and everywhere earned a name for ability.

sentences.zh: 注[一]燕剌王旦，武帝子，坐與上官桀等謀亂，自殺。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Prince Li Dan of Yan was a son of Emperor Wu; for plotting rebellion with Shangguan Jie and others he was implicated and killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Prince Dan of Yan was Emperor Wu's son; he joined Shangguan Jie and others in a revolt and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 剌，力割反。

translations.literal: Li 'pricked,' read with initial l and final ge cut.
translations.idiomatic: The character read li (as in 'cut').

sentences.zh: 舒小子篆，王莽時為郡文學，以明經征詣公車。

translations.literal: Shu's youngest son Zhuan, in Wang Mang's time, was a commandery erudite in the classics and, because of his mastery of the canon, was summoned to the public carriage office.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Shu’s youngest son, Cui Zhuan, served as commandery professor of letters under Wang Mang; his command of the classics earned him a summons to the imperial coach office.

sentences.zh: 太保甄豐舉為步兵校尉，篆辭曰：「吾聞伐國不問仁人，[一]戰陳不訪儒士。

translations.literal: Grand Guardian Zhen Feng recommended him for colonel of foot soldiers; Zhuan declined, saying: 'I have heard that one does not ask a humane man about attacking a state, [note one] and one does not consult a scholar about battle formations.'
translations.idiomatic: The grand protector Zhen Feng nominated him for colonel of foot soldiers. Zhuan refused: 'They say you do not ask a humane man how to wage war on another state, and you do not ask a classicist how to draw up a battle line.'

sentences.zh: [二]此舉奚為至哉？

translations.literal: [Note two] Why has this recommendation come to me?
translations.idiomatic: Why should this appointment fall to me?

sentences.zh: 」遂投劾歸。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he submitted a self-accusation and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: With that he filed a resignation on his own initiative and went home.

translations.literal: [Note three]
translations.idiomatic: See note three below.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書董仲舒曰：「昔*

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The Former Documents records Dong Zhongshu as saying: 'In antiquity *
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Han shu* quotes Dong Zhongshu: 'Long ago…

translations.literal: (at)
translations.idiomatic: (the character supplied in the text reads 'at').

sentences.zh: **[者]*魯君問柳下惠曰：『吾欲伐齊，如何？

translations.literal: **[one who]* the lord of Lu asked Liu Xiahui, saying: "I wish to attack Qi; how would that be?
translations.idiomatic: …someone*—the Lord of Lu asked Liu Xiahui: "I mean to strike Qi; what do you think?"

sentences.zh: 』柳下惠曰：『不可。

translations.literal: "' Liu Xiahui said: "It may not be done.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xiahui answered: "You must not."

sentences.zh: 』歸而有憂色，曰：『吾聞伐國不問仁人，此言何為至於我哉？』」

translations.literal: "' Returning, he had a troubled look and said: "I have heard that one does not ask a humane man about attacking a state; why should these words reach me?"'
translations.idiomatic: Going home, he looked troubled and said: "I was taught that humane men are not consulted about invading another state—so why do these words come to me?"'

sentences.zh: 注[二]論語曰：「□靈公問陳於孔子。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: The *Analects* says: "Duke Ling of Wei asked Confucius about battle formations.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Analects* records Duke Ling of Wei asking Confucius about military arrays.

sentences.zh: 孔子對曰：『俎豆之事則嘗聞之，軍旅之事未之學也。』」

translations.literal: Confucius replied: 'I have heard about the matter of sacrificial stands and vessels; I have not studied the matter of armies and hosts.'"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius replied: "I have learned something about ritual vessels; as for leading troops, I have had no training."'

sentences.zh: 注[三]投辭自劾有過，不合應舉。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: He cast aside the summons and impeached himself for fault, deeming himself unfit to answer the recommendation.
translations.idiomatic: Note: He declined the nomination and filed a self-indictment, holding that he was not a proper candidate.

sentences.zh: 莽嫌諸不附己者，多以法中傷之。

translations.literal: Wang Mang resented all who would not attach themselves to him and often used the law to wound them.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang bore a grudge toward anyone who would not side with him and repeatedly used the statutes to harm them.

sentences.zh: 時篆兄發以佞巧幸於莽，位至大司空。

translations.literal: At that time Zhuan's elder brother Fa, by flattery and cleverness, won Mang's favor and rose to grand minister of works.
translations.idiomatic: Meanwhile Zhuan’s older brother Fa curried favor with Wang Mang through slick ingratiation and climbed to grand minister of works.

sentences.zh: 母師氏能通經學﹑百家之言，莽寵以殊禮，賜號義成夫人，金印紫綬，文軒丹轂，顯於新世。

translations.literal: His mother, of the Shi clan, could penetrate the learning of the classics and the words of the hundred schools; Mang favored her with extraordinary rites, bestowing the title Lady Yicheng, a gold seal and purple ribbon, a patterned carriage and vermillion hubs, so that she stood out in the Xin era.
translations.idiomatic: Their mother, Lady Shi, was versed in the canon and the hundred schools of thought; Mang showered her with exceptional honors—she was styled Lady Yicheng, given a gold seal on purple ribbon, a lacquered carriage with crimson wheel hubs, and shone conspicuously in the Xin court.

sentences.zh: 後以篆為建新大尹，[一]篆不得已，乃歎曰：「吾生無妄之世，值澆﹑羿之君，[二]上有老母，下有兄弟，安得獨潔己而危所生哉？

translations.literal: Later he appointed Zhuan grand administrator of Jianxin, [note one] Zhuan had no choice and sighed: "I was born into an age without falsehood, yet I meet a ruler like Jiao and Yi; above I have an aged mother, below I have brothers—how can I alone keep myself pure and endanger those who gave me life?
translations.idiomatic: Later Mang named Zhuan grand administrator of Jianxin commandery. Zhuan had no way out and lamented: "I live in a time that is anything but 'no error,' and I serve a master as cruel as Jiao or Yi of old; I have a mother above and brothers below—how could I save my own spotless name and put my kin in mortal danger?

sentences.zh: 」乃遂單車到官，稱疾不視事，三年不行縣。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he went alone in a single cart to his post, pleaded illness, and did not conduct affairs; for three years he did not tour the counties.
translations.idiomatic: He took a lone carriage to his seat, pleaded sickness, refused to handle business, and for three years never made the rounds of his districts.

sentences.zh: [三]門下掾倪敞諫，篆乃強起班春。

translations.literal: [Note three] A clerk of his household, Ni Chang, remonstrated, and only then did Zhuan force himself to rise and circulate the spring edict.
translations.idiomatic: His aide Ni Chang urged him on, and Zhuan roused himself at last to go out and proclaim the spring agricultural orders.

sentences.zh: [四]所至之縣，獄犴填滿。

translations.literal: [Note four] In every county he reached, the prisons were packed full.
translations.idiomatic: Everywhere he went the jail cells were crammed.

sentences.zh: [五]篆垂涕曰：「嗟乎！

translations.literal: [Note five] Zhuan, tears streaming, said: "Alas!
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan wept aloud: "Alas!

sentences.zh: 刑罰不中，乃陷人於稨。

translations.literal: The punishments are not balanced, and so they trap people in the net.
translations.idiomatic: When punishments miss the mean, innocent people are caught in the snare.

sentences.zh: 此皆何罪，而至於是！」

translations.literal: What crimes are these, that they have come to this!"
translations.idiomatic: What wrong have these people done, that it should come to this?"

sentences.zh: 遂平理，所出二千餘人。

translations.literal: Thereupon he settled and rectified cases and released more than two thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: He reviewed the dockets, set matters right, and freed over two thousand people.

sentences.zh: 掾吏叩頭諫曰：「朝廷初政，州牧峻刻。

translations.literal: The clerks knocked their heads in remonstrance, saying: "The court has just begun its government, and the provincial shepherds are harsh.
translations.idiomatic: His subordinates kowtowed and warned him: "The new regime is still tightening its grip, and regional governors are severe.

sentences.zh: [六]宥過申枉，誠仁者之心；

translations.literal: [Note six] To pardon faults and stretch out the wronged is truly the mind of a humane man;
translations.idiomatic: To forgive offenses and redress injustice is the way of a humane man—

sentences.zh: 然獨為君子，將有悔乎！

translations.literal: yet to act alone as a gentleman—will there not be regret!
translations.idiomatic: —but to play the lone gentleman may yet bring regret!"

sentences.zh: 」篆曰：「邾文公不以一人易其身，君子謂之知命。

translations.literal: " Zhuan said: "Lord Wen of Zhu did not trade his person for one other man; the gentleman calls that knowing Heaven's decree.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan replied: "Lord Wen of Zhu would not exchange his own life for anyone else's; the noble man says that is knowing fate."

sentences.zh: [七]如殺一大尹贖二千人，蓋所願也。

translations.literal: [Note seven] If by killing one grand administrator I redeem two thousand men, that is what I would wish.
translations.idiomatic: If executing one grand administrator could ransom two thousand souls, I would count it a bargain."

sentences.zh: 」遂稱疾去。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he pleaded illness and departed.
translations.idiomatic: On that plea he resigned and left.

sentences.zh: 注[一]莽改千乘郡曰建新，守曰大尹。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Mang changed the commandery of Qiancheng to Jianxin, and its administrator was titled grand administrator.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Wang Mang renamed Qiancheng commandery *Jianxin* and called its governor *grand administrator*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]易曰：「無妄之行，窮之災也。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: The *Changes* says: "Action without error that goes to extremes meets disaster in exhaustion.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zhou yi* says: "The movement of 'no error' pushed to the limit meets calamity at the road's end."

sentences.zh: 」左傳曰：「昔有夏之方衰也，后羿自鉏遷於窮石，因夏人以代夏政，而淫於原獸。

translations.literal: " The *Zuo Tradition* says: "When the house of Xia was declining, Hou Yi moved from Chu to Qiongshi, took advantage of the Xia people to replace Xia government, and gave himself to beasts in the field.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zuo zhuan* adds: "When the Xia house waned, Hou Yi shifted from Chu to Qiongshi, used the Xia populace to seize their government, and abandoned himself to hunting."

sentences.zh: 用寒浞，伯明氏之讒子弟也。

translations.literal: He employed Han Zhuo, a slanderous scion of the Bright-of-Bo clan.
translations.idiomatic: He took Han Zhuo into service—a treacherous younger kinsman of the lord of Boming.

sentences.zh: 而虞羿於田，以取其國家。

translations.literal: And fooled Yi in the hunt in order to seize his state and family.
translations.idiomatic: He trapped Yi in the chase and so stole his state and household.

sentences.zh: 浞因羿室，生澆及□，恃其讒慝詐偽，而不德於人。

translations.literal: Zhuo took Yi's household, begat Jiao and [character missing in text], relied on slanderous wickedness, deceit, and pretense, and did not show virtue toward people.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuo occupied Yi's home, fathered Jiao and a second son (the name is lost in the received text), and trusted in malice, fraud, and sham, winning no one's goodwill.

sentences.zh: 」澆音五吊反。

translations.literal: " Jiao is read with initial ng and final diao in the fanqie spelling.
translations.idiomatic: The note gives the fanqie reading for *Jiao*.

sentences.zh: □音許既反。

translations.literal: [Character missing] is read with initial x and final ji in the fanqie spelling.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss gives the fanqie for the damaged name (traditionally reconstructed).

sentences.zh: 注[三]續漢志曰：「郡國常以春行*

translations.literal: Comment on note three: The *Continued Han Treatise* says: "Commanderies and kingdoms regularly in spring proceed *
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Xu Han zhi* says that each spring the commanderies and kingdoms would travel *

translations.literal: (to)
translations.idiomatic: (to)

sentences.zh: **[主]*縣，勸人農桑，振救乏絕。」

translations.literal: **[preside over]* the counties, encourage people in farming and sericulture, and relieve the exhausted and cut off."
translations.idiomatic: …to* each county, urge farming and silk raising, and relieve the destitute."

sentences.zh: 注[四]班布春令。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: To promulgate the spring ordinance.
translations.idiomatic: Note: That is, to publish the spring agricultural edict.

sentences.zh: 注[五]犴音岸。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: An 'prison' is read with initial ng and final an.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *An* (the jail name) is glossed with the *an* sound.

sentences.zh: 前書音義曰：「鄉亭之獄曰犴。」

translations.literal: The Former Documents sounds and meanings says: "The prison of village and canton is called an."
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shu* yinyi explains: "Local jails were called *an*."

sentences.zh: 注[六]初政謂莽即位。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: 'Beginning government' refers to Mang's accession.
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'The new government' means Wang Mang's enthronement.

sentences.zh: 注[七]左傳曰「邾文公卜遷於繹。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: The *Zuo Tradition* says: "Lord Wen of Zhu divined about moving to Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zuo zhuan* says Lord Wen of Zhu divined on removing the capital to Yi.

sentences.zh: 史曰：『利於人，不利於君。

translations.literal: The scribe said: 'It profits the people but not the lord.
translations.idiomatic: The scribe reported: "It benefits the people but not the ruler."

sentences.zh: 』邾子曰：『苟利於人，孤之利也。

translations.literal: " The lord of Zhu said: 'If it profits the people, it is my profit.
translations.idiomatic: The lord of Zhu said: "If the people gain, I gain with them."

sentences.zh: 人既利矣，孤必與焉。

translations.literal: The people have already profited; I am surely with them.
translations.idiomatic: If they prosper, I share in that prosperity."

sentences.zh: 』遂遷於繹。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he moved to Yi.
translations.idiomatic: So he moved the seat to Yi."

sentences.zh: 五月，邾文公卒。

translations.literal: In the fifth month, Lord Wen of Zhu died.
translations.idiomatic: The fifth month after the move, Lord Wen of Zhu died.

sentences.zh: 君子曰知命」也。

translations.literal: The gentleman says he knew fate" also.
translations.idiomatic: The noble man said he had known his allotted fate."

sentences.zh: 建武初，朝廷多薦言之者，幽州刺史又舉篆賢良。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jianwu, the court had many who recommended and spoke for him, and the inspector of You Province again nominated Zhuan as worthy and good.
translations.idiomatic: In the first years of Emperor Guangwu's Jianwu era, many at court spoke up for him, and the You provincial inspector again nominated Zhuan as *xianliang*.

sentences.zh: 篆自以宗門受莽偽寵，籩愧漢朝，遂辭歸不仕。

translations.literal: Zhuan, because his clan had received Wang Mang's false favor, was ashamed before the Han house, and therefore declined and returned, not taking office.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan believed his family had accepted unworthy honors from Wang Mang and therefore felt ashamed before the restored Han, so he declined all posts and lived in retirement.

sentences.zh: 客居滎陽，閉門潛思，著周易林六十四篇，用決吉凶，多所佔驗。

translations.literal: He lodged as a guest in Yingyang, closed his gate and pondered in secret, and composed sixty-four chapters of *Forest of the Zhou Changes*, using them to decide auspiciousness and inauspiciousness; in many cases they proved correct in divination.
translations.idiomatic: He settled in Yingyang, shut his doors to think, and wrote sixty-four chapters of the *Zhou yi lin*, using them to read good and ill fortune; diviners found them uncannily accurate.

sentences.zh: 臨終作賦以自悼，名曰慰志。

translations.literal: Facing death he composed a fu to mourn himself, entitled *Consoling the Will*.
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed he composed a *fu* of self-lament titled *Weizhi* ('Consoling My Purpose').

sentences.zh: 其辭曰：

translations.literal: The words thereof say:
translations.idiomatic: It begins:

sentences.zh: 嘉昔人之遘辰兮，[一]美伊﹑傅之□時。

translations.literal: I praise the men of old who met their hour, [note one] and admire how Yi Yin and Fu Yue met their season.
translations.idiomatic: I praise the ancients who met their destined moment and marvel how Yi Yin and Fu Yue arrived in the nick of time.

sentences.zh: [二]應規矩之淑質兮，過班﹑倕而裁之。

translations.literal: [Note two] I match the fine substance of compass and square, surpassing Ban and Chui and trimming it.
translations.idiomatic: My nature answers to compass and square; I trim it finer than even Gongshu Ban or the craftsman Chui could carve.

sentences.zh: [三]協准矱之貞度兮，同斷金之玄策。

translations.literal: [Note three] I harmonize with the straight measure of level and marking cord, sharing the subtle strategy that severs metal.
translations.idiomatic: I keep to the true line of plumb and rule, one in spirit with the subtle counsel that 'cuts metal when two hearts agree.'

sentences.zh: [四]何天衢於盛世兮，超千載而垂績。

translations.literal: [Note four] I tread Heaven's highway in a flourishing age, leaping a thousand years to leave lasting deeds.
translations.idiomatic: I walk Heaven's great road in a brilliant age and leap beyond a millennium to leave enduring merit.

sentences.zh: [五]豈修德之極致兮，將天祚之攸適？

translations.literal: [Note five] Is it the utmost of cultivating virtue, or is it that Heaven's blessing was directed there?
translations.idiomatic: Is this the crown of cultivated virtue, or simply Heaven's favor settling where it will?

sentences.zh: 注[一]遘，遇也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Gou means to meet.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Gou* means 'to encounter.'

sentences.zh: 辰，時也。

translations.literal: Chen means time.
translations.idiomatic: *Chen* means 'the right moment.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]伊尹干湯，傅說遇高宗。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Yi Yin presented himself to Tang; Fu Yue met Gaozong.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yi Yin sought out Tang; Fu Yue was found by Gaozong.

sentences.zh: 爾雅曰：「□，遇也。

translations.literal: The *Erya* says: "[Missing graph], means to meet.
translations.idiomatic: The *Erya* glosses the word (graph damaged) as 'meet.'

sentences.zh: 」音五故反。

translations.literal: " It is read with initial ng and final gu in the fanqie spelling.
translations.idiomatic: The fanqie spelling is given for its pronunciation.

sentences.zh: 注[三]公輸班，魯人也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Gongshu Ban was a man of Lu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gongshu Ban came from Lu.

sentences.zh: 倕，舜時為共工之官。

translations.literal: Chui, in Shun's time, held the office of Minister of Works.
translations.idiomatic: Chui served Shun as minister of public works.

sentences.zh: 皆巧人也。

translations.literal: Both were clever men.
translations.idiomatic: Both were master artisans.

sentences.zh: 以喻湯及高宗也。

translations.literal: By them Tang and Gaozong are likened.
translations.idiomatic: They stand for Tang and Gaozong in the metaphor.

sentences.zh: 注[四]准，繩也。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: Zhun means cord.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhun* is the plumb line.

sentences.zh: 矱，尺也。

translations.literal: Yue means foot-rule.
translations.idiomatic: *Yue* is the carpenter's marking cord.

sentences.zh: 貞，正也。

translations.literal: Zhen means correct.
translations.idiomatic: *Zhen* means upright, correct.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「二人同心，其利斷金。

translations.literal: The *Changes* says: "When two men share one mind, their keenness can cut metal.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou yi* says: "Two men of one mind can slice through metal."

sentences.zh: 」玄策猶妙策也。

translations.literal: " Mysterious strategy means a subtle strategy.
translations.idiomatic: *Xuance* means a subtle, penetrating plan."

sentences.zh: 注[五]易大畜卦，干下艮上，其上九曰：「何天之衢，亨。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: The *Changes* great accumulating hexagram, Qian below and Gen above; its top sixth line says: "What of Heaven's highway—success.
translations.idiomatic: Note: On the *Da chu* hexagram (Qian over Gen), the top line reads: "What of Heaven's highway? Success."

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄云：「艮為手，手上肩也。

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan says: "Gen is hand; hand is shoulder.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan explains: "Gen is the hand; the hand here is the shoulder."

sentences.zh: 干為首。

translations.literal: Qian is head.
translations.idiomatic: Qian is the head."

sentences.zh: 首肩之閒荷物處。

translations.literal: The place between head and shoulder is where one bears a load.
translations.idiomatic: The space between head and shoulder is where a burden is carried."

sentences.zh: 干為天，艮為徑路，天衢象也。」

translations.literal: Qian is Heaven; Gen is path; it is an image of Heaven's highway."
translations.idiomatic: Qian stands for Heaven, Gen for a path—thus the image of Heaven's highway."

sentences.zh: 愍餘生之不造兮，[一]丁漢氏之中微。

translations.literal: I pity that my life was not well made, [note one] encountering the mid-decline of the Han house.
translations.idiomatic: I mourn that my life was ill-starred, born into the Han house as it slid into eclipse.

sentences.zh: [二]氛霓郁以橫厲兮，羲和忽以潛暉。

translations.literal: [Note two] Vapor and rainbow were dense and swept fiercely; Xihe suddenly hid his radiance.
translations.idiomatic: Miasma and false rainbows choked the sky; the charioteer of the sun suddenly veiled his light.

sentences.zh: [三] 六柄制於家門兮，王綱漼以陵□。

translations.literal: [Note three] The six handles were controlled within the family gate; the king's net was shattered and hung in ruins.
translations.idiomatic: The six reins of state fell to private hands; the royal net frayed and collapsed (the last word is damaged in the text).

sentences.zh: [四]黎﹑共奮以跋扈兮，羿﹑浞狂以恣睢。

translations.literal: [Note four] Li and Gong raged in arrogance; Yi and Zhuo ran wild in wanton license.
translations.idiomatic: Li Si and Wang Gong flaunted their power; Hou Yi and Han Zhuo raged unchecked.

sentences.zh: [五] 睹嫚臧而乘釁兮，竊神器之萬機。

translations.literal: [Note five] Seeing wanton ministers seize the opening, they stole the myriad engines of the sacred regalia.
translations.idiomatic: They watched smooth courtiers exploit every crack and usurp the myriad levers of the throne.

sentences.zh: [六]思輔弼以偷存兮，亦號咷以詶咨。

translations.literal: [Note six] I thought to rely on assistants to eke out survival, yet also wailed and cried in counsel.
translations.idiomatic: I hoped good ministers might let me cling to life, yet I could only howl and plead in council.

sentences.zh: [七]嗟三事之我負兮，乃迫余以天威。

translations.literal: [Note seven] Alas that the three high ministers betrayed me; then they pressed me with Heaven's majesty.
translations.idiomatic: Woe that the three dukes failed me—then Heaven's awful mandate closed on me.

sentences.zh: [八]豈無熊僚之微介兮？

translations.literal: [Note eight] Was there not the slight integrity of Xiong Liao?
translations.idiomatic: Had I not even the small steadfastness of Xiong Liao?

sentences.zh: 悼我生之殲夷。

translations.literal: I mourn that my life was cut off and destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: I grieve that my life was broken and brought low.

sentences.zh: [九]庶明哲之末風兮，懼大雅之所譏。

translations.literal: [Note nine] I hope in the last breeze of the wise and bright, fearing what the *Greater Odes* would ridicule.
translations.idiomatic: I look to the fading breath of the wise, dreading the rebuke the *Da ya* would pronounce.

sentences.zh: [一０]遂翕翼以委命兮，受符守乎艮維。

translations.literal: [Note ten] Thereupon I folded wings and entrusted my fate, receiving the tally and guarding the Gen quarter.
translations.idiomatic: So I folded my wings and resigned myself to fate, took the tallies and held the northeast march (Gen).

sentences.zh: [一一] 恨遭閉而不隱兮，違石門之高蹤。

translations.literal: [Note eleven] I hate having met obstruction and not hiding; I depart from the lofty tracks of Stone Gate.
translations.idiomatic: I resent that, blocked at every turn, I did not withdraw; I forsake the high path of the Stone Gate recluses.

sentences.zh: [一二]揚蛾眉於復關兮，犯孔戒之冶容。

translations.literal: [Note twelve] I raised moth eyebrows at the double gate; I violated Confucius's warning against alluring looks.
translations.idiomatic: I painted moth eyebrows at the inner gate and broke the Master's warning against seductive beauty.

sentences.zh: [一三]懿氓蚩之悟悔兮，慕白駒之所從。

translations.literal: [Note thirteen] I praise the rustic's foolish awakening and regret, and yearn for the white colt's path to follow.
translations.idiomatic: [Note thirteen] I scorn the bumpkin's belated regret and long to follow the noble guest of the *White Colt* ode.

sentences.zh: [一四]乃稱疾而屢復兮，歷三祀而見許。

translations.literal: [Note fourteen] Then I pleaded illness and repeatedly sought release; after three years of offerings I was granted leave.
translations.idiomatic: [Note fourteen] Time and again I feigned sickness until, three calendar rounds later, the court let me go.

sentences.zh: [一五]悠輕舉以遠遁兮，托峻峗以幽處。

translations.literal: [Note fifteen] Leisurely I rose lightly and fled far away, entrusting myself to steep crags for a hidden dwelling.
translations.idiomatic: [Note fifteen] I drifted aloft and withdrew to the distance, lodging my person among towering cliffs in seclusion.

sentences.zh: [一六]竫潛思於至賾兮，騁六經之奧府。

translations.literal: [Note sixteen] Quietly I pondered the utmost profundities and galloped through the treasure house of the Six Classics.
translations.idiomatic: [Note sixteen] I plumbed the deepest truths in silent thought and ranged the inner chambers of the six canonical texts.

sentences.zh: [一七]皇再命而紹恤兮，乃雲眷乎建武。

translations.literal: [Note seventeen] August Heaven twice commanded and renewed its pity; then its clouds turned kindly toward Jianwu.
translations.idiomatic: [Note seventeen] High Heaven issued a second charge of mercy; its favor gathered on the Jianwu restoration.

sentences.zh: [一八]運欃槍以電埽兮，清六合之土宇。

translations.literal: [Note eighteen] It wielded the broom-comet like a lightning sweep and cleansed the soil and roofbeams within the six directions.
translations.idiomatic: [Note eighteen] The comet-tail scourge flashed like lightning and swept the realm clean from corner to corner.

sentences.zh: [一九]聖德滂以橫被兮，黎庶愷以鼓舞。

translations.literal: [Note nineteen] Sage virtue poured abroad in a horizontal flood; the black-haired people rejoiced and beat their drums.
translations.idiomatic: [Note nineteen] Sacred power spread like pouring rain; the common folk danced and drummed for joy.

sentences.zh: 辟四門以博延兮，彼幽牧之我舉。

translations.literal: He opened the four gates to summon widely, and that shepherd of You raised me up.
translations.idiomatic: The throne threw open every gate to seek talent, and the You provincial governor nominated me.

sentences.zh: [二０]分畫定而計決兮，豈雲賁乎鄙耇，[二一]遂懸車以縶馬兮，絕時俗之進取。

translations.literal: [Note twenty] Once divisions were drawn and the plan was fixed, how could one speak of mere adornment for rustic elders? [Note twenty-one] Thereupon I hung up my cart and hobbled my horses, cutting off the vulgar pursuit of advancement.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty] My mind was made up and my course set—this was no quest for empty honors from country greybeards. [Note twenty-one] I hung up the carriage, tethered the team, and turned my back on the scramble for place.

sentences.zh: 歎暮春之成服兮，闔衡門以埽軌。

translations.literal: I sighed that late spring's garments were complete, closed the crossbeam gate, and swept away the wheel tracks.
translations.idiomatic: I mourned the season when spring robes are donned, barred the humble wicket gate, and erased every trace of the road out.

sentences.zh: [二二]聊優遊以永日兮，守性命以盡齒。

translations.literal: [Note twenty-two] For a while I wandered at ease through the long day, guarding my nature and allotted life to exhaust my years.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-two] I passed the endless daylight in quiet ease, keeping body and destiny whole to the end of my span.

sentences.zh: [二三]貴啟體之歸全兮，庶不忝乎先子。

translations.literal: [Note twenty-three] I honor returning whole in body as the fulfillment of duty, hoping thereby not to shame my late father.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-three] I prize coming home with limb and virtue intact, lest I disgrace the father who went before me.

sentences.zh: [二四]

translations.literal: [Note twenty-four]
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-four] End of this stanza's notes.

sentences.zh: 注[一]造，成也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Zao means 'completed.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zao* here means 'formed' or 'brought to pass.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]丁，當也。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Ding means 'to meet' or 'to face.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Ding* means 'to strike upon' or 'to encounter.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]氛，祲也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Fen means an inauspicious vapour.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Fen* is the murky aura of ill omen.

sentences.zh: 霓，日傍之氣。

translations.literal: Ni is the vapour beside the sun.
translations.idiomatic: *Ni* is the secondary halo or mock-sun beside the disk.

sentences.zh: 橫厲謂氣盛而陵於天也。

translations.literal: Heng li means the vapour flourished and overrode Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: *Heng li* describes malignant qi mounting until it veils the sky.

sentences.zh: 羲和，日也。

translations.literal: Xihe is the sun.
translations.idiomatic: Xihe stands for the charioteer of the sun—here, the sunlight itself.

sentences.zh: 氣盛而日光微，諭王莽篡漢。

translations.literal: The vapour flourished and sunlight was slight; it illustrates Wang Mang's usurpation of Han.
translations.idiomatic: The image means the omens swelled while Han's light failed—an allegory of Wang Mang's seizure of the throne.

sentences.zh: 注[四]國語管仲對齊桓公曰：「昔者聖人之理天下也，而慎用其六柄焉。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: The *Discourses of the States* records Guan Zhong answering Duke Huan of Qi: 'In antiquity the sage, in ordering All under Heaven, was careful in wielding his six handles.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Guoyu* quotes Guan Zhong telling Duke Huan how the sage-kings governed with six instruments of power.

sentences.zh: 」韋昭注云：「六柄，生﹑殺﹑貧﹑賤﹑富﹑貴也。

translations.literal: " Wei Zhao's commentary says: 'The six handles are life, death, poverty, low rank, wealth, and high rank.'
translations.idiomatic: Wei Zhao glosses them as the powers of life and death, ruin and exaltation, want and plenty.

sentences.zh: 」漼猶摧落也，音千隗反。

translations.literal: " Cui 'shattered' means broken and fallen; it is read with initial ts'ien and final ngwei in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: *Cui* means 'broken, cast down'; the gloss gives its fanqie spelling.

sentences.zh: 注[五]國語曰：「昔少□之衰，九黎亂德，人神雜揉，不可方物。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: The *Discourses of the States* says: 'In antiquity, when Shao [graph missing] declined, the Nine Li threw virtue into confusion; men and spirits were mixed and could not be distinguished.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Guoyu* tells how in Shao Hao's decline the Nine Li corrupted morals until men and gods mingled indistinguishably.

sentences.zh: 」淮南子曰：「昔者共工與顓頊爭為帝，怒而觸不周之山，天柱折，地維絕。

translations.literal: " The *Huainanzi* says: 'Formerly Gonggong strove with Zhuanxu to become emperor; in rage he struck Mount Buzhou; the pillar of Heaven broke and the cords of Earth snapped.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Huainanzi* adds Gonggong's rage against Zhuanxu, the broken sky-pillar, and the snapped earth-anchors.

sentences.zh: 」跋扈，強梁也。

translations.literal: " Bahu means violent and overbearing.
translations.idiomatic: *Bahu* means domineering and brutal.

sentences.zh: 恣睢，自用之貌也。

translations.literal: Zisui is the appearance of self-willed action.
translations.idiomatic: *Zisui* describes the look of arbitrary, wilful conduct.

sentences.zh: 恣音訾。

translations.literal: Zi is read like zi.
translations.idiomatic: The first syllable is read *zi*.

sentences.zh: 睢音許維反。

translations.literal: Sui is read with initial x and final wei in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie is given for *sui*.

sentences.zh: 羿﹑浞已見上。

translations.literal: Yi and Zhuo have already been seen above.
translations.idiomatic: Hou Yi and Han Zhuo were glossed earlier.

sentences.zh: 注[六]易曰：「嫚藏誨盜。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: The *Changes* says: 'To slight one's store is to teach thieves.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zhou yi* warns that careless hoarding invites theft.

sentences.zh: 」釁，隙也。

translations.literal: " Xin means a crack.
translations.idiomatic: *Xin* means a chink or opening.

sentences.zh: 神器，帝王之位。

translations.literal: The divine vessel is the position of emperor.
translations.idiomatic: The 'divine vessel' is the imperial throne.

sentences.zh: 老子曰：「天下神器，不可為也。

translations.literal: Laozi says: 'All under Heaven is a divine vessel; it cannot be acted upon.'
translations.idiomatic: Laozi says the realm is a sacred trust not to be seized by force.

sentences.zh: 」書云：「兢兢業業，一日二日萬機。」

translations.literal: " The *Documents* says: 'Diligent, diligent—from one day to the next, myriad are the engines of rule.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Shang shu* adds the king's myriad daily cares.

sentences.zh: 注[七]輔弼謂王莽輔政也。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: Fu bi refers to Wang Mang assisting government.
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'Assistants' means Wang Mang's regency before usurpation.

sentences.zh: 偷，苟且也。

translations.literal: Tou means making shift in a base way.
translations.idiomatic: *Tou* means getting by meanly or precariously.

sentences.zh: 號咷，哀呼也。

translations.literal: Haotao means wailing and crying out.
translations.idiomatic: *Haotao* is the sound of bitter lament.

sentences.zh: 前書王莽策孺子嬰為定安公，莽親執孺子手，流涕歔欷也。

translations.literal: The Former Documents records that Wang Mang, in investing the child Ying as duke of Ding'an, personally grasped the child's hand, weeping and sobbing.
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shu* tells how Mang, enthroning the infant Ying as Duke of Ding'an, clutched his hand and wept aloud.

sentences.zh: 注[八]三事謂三公也。

translations.literal: Comment on note eight: The three affairs are the three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The 'three high ministers' are the three dukes.

sentences.zh: 負謂太保甄豐舉也。

translations.literal: Betrayed refers to Grand Guardian Zhen Feng's recommendation.
translations.idiomatic: 'Betrayed me' alludes to Zhen Feng's nomination that drew Zhuan into service.

sentences.zh: 注[九]左傳曰：「楚白公勝為亂。

translations.literal: Comment on note nine: The *Zuo Tradition* says: 'Bai Gong of Chu raised a revolt.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zuo zhuan* tells of Duke Sheng of Bai's coup in Chu.

sentences.zh: 石乞曰：『市南有熊相宜僚者，若得之，可以當五百人矣。

translations.literal: Shi Qi said: 'South of the market is Xiong Yi, styled Liao; if you get him, he can match five hundred men.'
translations.idiomatic: Shi Qi said a fighter named Xiong Yi Liao south of the market was worth five hundred soldiers.

sentences.zh: 』從白公而見之。

translations.literal: "' He followed Bai Gong to see him.
translations.idiomatic: They went with Bai Gong to visit him.

sentences.zh: 與之言，說；

translations.literal: They spoke with him, and he was pleased;
translations.idiomatic: They talked; he was won over;

sentences.zh: 告之故，辭；

translations.literal: they told him the reason, and he declined;
translations.idiomatic: they explained the plot, and he refused;

sentences.zh: 承之以□，不動。

translations.literal: they threatened him with a [weapon], yet he did not stir.
translations.idiomatic: they laid a blade to him, yet he never flinched.

sentences.zh: 勝曰：『不為利*

translations.literal: Sheng said: 'He will not for profit *
translations.idiomatic: Bai Gong said: 'This is not a man who serves profit *

translations.literal: (nor)
translations.idiomatic: (nor)

sentences.zh: **[諂]*，不為威惕，不洩人言以求媚者。

translations.literal: **[nor]* for flattery, will not tremble at threats, and will not leak others' words to seek favor.'
translations.idiomatic: **nor]* flattery, quails at no threat, and will not betray another's counsel to curry favor.'

sentences.zh: 』去之。

translations.literal: "' He departed.
translations.idiomatic: With that he walked away.

sentences.zh: 」介，耿介也。

translations.literal: " Jie means upright integrity.
translations.idiomatic: *Jie* means principled steadfastness.

sentences.zh: 我生謂母也。

translations.literal: My life refers to his mother.
translations.idiomatic: 'My life' means his mother, whose safety he feared for.

sentences.zh: 殲，滅也。

translations.literal: Jian means to extinguish.
translations.idiomatic: *Jian* means to wipe out.

sentences.zh: 夷，傷也。

translations.literal: Yi means to injure.
translations.idiomatic: *Yi* means to wound or harm.

sentences.zh: 言其母老，恐禍及也。

translations.literal: It says his mother was old and he feared calamity would reach her.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains he could not keep clean hands without endangering his aged mother.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]詩大雅曰：「既明且哲，以保其身。」

translations.literal: Comment on note ten: The *Greater Odes* says: 'Clear-sighted and wise, thereby to preserve one's person.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Da ya* praises the wise who know how to save their own skins.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]艮，東北之位。

translations.literal: Comment on note eleven: Gen is the northeast quarter.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Gen trigram marks the northeast—the direction of Zhuan's post.

sentences.zh: 謂篆為千乘太守也。

translations.literal: It refers to Zhuan as grand administrator of Qiancheng.
translations.idiomatic: That is, his appointment as governor of Qiancheng commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]易曰：「天地閉而賢人隱。

translations.literal: Comment on note twelve: The *Changes* says: 'When Heaven and Earth close, the worthy hide.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zhou yi* says when heaven and earth shut, worthies withdraw from sight.

sentences.zh: 」論語曰：「子路宿於石門。

translations.literal: " The *Analects* says: 'Zilu lodged at Stone Gate.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Analects* tells of Zilu spending the night at Stone Gate.

sentences.zh: 晨門曰：『奚自？

translations.literal: The morning gatekeeper said: 'Whence have you come?'
translations.idiomatic: The gatekeeper in the morning asked where he had come from.

sentences.zh: 』子路曰：『自孔氏。

translations.literal: "' Zilu said: 'From the Kong clan.'
translations.idiomatic: Zilu answered: 'From the house of Kong.'

sentences.zh: 』曰：『是知其不可而為之者歟？

translations.literal: "' He said: 'Is that the one who knows it cannot be done yet still does it?'
translations.idiomatic: The man replied: 'The fellow who knows the task is hopeless yet keeps trying?'

sentences.zh: 』」注[一三]楚詞曰：「觿女皆妒余之蛾眉。

translations.literal: "'" Comment on note thirteen: The *Songs of Chu* says: 'The pettifogging women all envy my moth eyebrows.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Chuci* speaks of jealous rivals envying the poet's painted brows.

sentences.zh: 」詩國風序曰：「氓，刺時也。

translations.literal: " The preface to the *Airs of the States* says: 'The "Foolish Lad" criticizes the times.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Guo feng* preface to *Meng* rebukes the morals of the age.

sentences.zh: 淫風大行，男女無別，故序其事以風焉。

translations.literal: Lewd custom ran great; men and women were undifferentiated; therefore it ordered the matter to instruct by indirection.
translations.idiomatic: Licentious ways ran riot and the sexes blurred, so the poem was arranged as a corrective lesson.

sentences.zh: 」其詩曰：「乘彼垝垣，以望復關。

translations.literal: " Its poem says: 'I climbed that ruined wall to gaze toward Fu Pass.'
translations.idiomatic: The ode runs: 'I climbed the broken wall and looked toward Fu Pass.'

sentences.zh: 」毛萇注云：「垝，毀也。

translations.literal: " Mao Chang's commentary says: 'Gui means ruined.'
translations.idiomatic: Mao Heng glosses *gui* as 'collapsed.'

sentences.zh: 復關，君子所近之處也。

translations.literal: Fu Pass is the place where the gentleman drew near.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Pass is where the lady's true lover would appear.

sentences.zh: 」易系辭曰：「冶容誨淫。

translations.literal: " The *Appended Remarks* of the *Changes* says: 'Alluring looks teach lewdness.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Xi ci* warns that seductive dress teaches wantonness.

sentences.zh: 」鄭玄云：「謂飾其容而見於外曰冶。」

translations.literal: " Zheng Xuan says: 'To adorn one's appearance and show it outward is called ye.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan defines *ye* as painting one's face for the world to see.

sentences.zh: 注[一四]詩曰「氓之蚩蚩，抱布貿絲。

translations.literal: Comment on note fourteen: The *Odes* says: 'That fool looked simple, bringing cloth to trade for silk.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shijing* line: 'The lad looked artless, hugging cloth to barter for silk.'

sentences.zh: 匪來貿絲，來即我謀」。

translations.literal: He did not come to trade silk; he came to press his suit with me.'"
translations.idiomatic: 'He came not for silk but to court me.'

sentences.zh: 注云：「氓，人也。

translations.literal: The commentary says: 'Meng means a man.'
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: *meng* is a common fellow.

sentences.zh: 蚩蚩，殷厚之貌。

translations.literal: Chichi is the appearance of earnest simplicity.
translations.idiomatic: *Chichi* describes an honest, simple mien.

sentences.zh: 布，幣也。

translations.literal: Bu means cloth currency.
translations.idiomatic: *Bu* here is silk used as money.

sentences.zh: 即，就也。

translations.literal: Ji means to draw near.
translations.idiomatic: *Ji* means 'to approach.'

sentences.zh: 言此之人，非買絲來，就我為室家也。

translations.literal: It means this person did not come to buy silk but came to seek me for a household.
translations.idiomatic: The sense is: he sought a marriage, not a market deal.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「及爾偕老，老使我怨。

translations.literal: " It also says: 'I meant to grow old with you; growing old, you embitter me.'
translations.idiomatic: The poem adds: 'I swore to age with you; now you age only to spite me.'

sentences.zh: 」注云：「我欲與汝俱至老，汝反薄我使怨也。

translations.literal: " The commentary says: 'I wished to reach old age with you, yet you turned harsh and made me resentful.'"
translations.idiomatic: The note: she wanted lifelong union; he grew cold and bred her resentment.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「皎皎白駒。

translations.literal: " It also says: 'Bright, bright the white colt.'
translations.idiomatic: It also sings: 'Gleaming the white colt.'

sentences.zh: 」諭賢人也。

translations.literal: " It illustrates a worthy man.
translations.idiomatic: The image stands for the elusive worthy.

sentences.zh: 注[一五]復猶白也。

translations.literal: Comment on note fifteen: Fu is read the same as bai, 'to make plain.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The gloss equates *fu* with *bai* as a phonetic loan, meaning to disclose or explain oneself clearly.

sentences.zh: 注[一六]峻峗謂山也。

translations.literal: Comment on note sixteen: Jun wei means mountains.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Jun wei* names steep mountain heights.

sentences.zh: 峗音魚委反。

translations.literal: Wei is read with initial ng and final wei in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie spelling is given for *wei*.

sentences.zh: 注[一七]賾，深也。

translations.literal: Comment on note seventeen: Ze means depth.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Ze* means the deepest layer of meaning.

sentences.zh: 注[一八]皇，天也。

translations.literal: Comment on note eighteen: Huang means Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Huang* is Heaven.

sentences.zh: 紹，繼也。

translations.literal: Shao means to continue.
translations.idiomatic: *Shao* means to carry on, to succeed.

sentences.zh: 恤，憂也。

translations.literal: Xu means to pity.
translations.idiomatic: *Xu* means compassionate concern.

sentences.zh: 言天憂恤眷顧漢家，所以再命光武也。

translations.literal: It says Heaven pitied and cared for the house of Han, and therefore twice commanded Guangwu.
translations.idiomatic: The line means Heaven pitied the Liu house and twice charged Guangwu to restore it.

sentences.zh: 注[一九]欃槍，彗也。

translations.literal: Comment on note nineteen: Chanqiang means a comet.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Chanqiang* is the broom-star, a comet.

sentences.zh: 注[二０]開闢四方之門，廣求賢也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty: To open wide the gates of the four quarters is broadly to seek the worthy.
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'Opening the four gates' is imperial summons of talent from every side.

sentences.zh: 幽牧謂為幽州刺史所舉也。

translations.literal: You mu refers to being raised up by the inspector of You Province.
translations.idiomatic: *You mu* means the nomination came from the You provincial governor.

sentences.zh: 注[二一]賁，飾也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-one: Ben means adornment.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Ben* means ornament or show.

sentences.zh: 易曰「束帛戔戔，賁於丘園」也。

translations.literal: The *Changes* says: 'Silks in bundles, richly adorning the hill garden.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou yi* line about silks offered to adorn a rustic retreat.

sentences.zh: 注[二二]論語曾點曰：「暮春*[者]*，春服既成。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-two: Zeng Dian of the *Analects* said: 'In late spring *
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zeng Dian's spring outing in the *Analects*: 'In late spring *

sentences.zh: 」衡，橫也，謂橫木為門。

translations.literal: " Heng means crosspiece; it refers to a crosspiece of wood for a gate.
translations.idiomatic: *Heng* is the crossbar; here, a simple wicket gate.

sentences.zh: 軌，跡也。

translations.literal: Gui means tracks.
translations.idiomatic: *Gui* are wheel ruts or footprints.

sentences.zh: 注[二三]齒，年也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-three: Chi means years.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Chi* means the teeth of age—one's years.

sentences.zh: 注[二四]論語曰：「曾子有疾，召門弟子曰：『啟余足。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-four: The *Analects* says: 'When Master Zeng was ill, he called his disciples to him and said: "Uncover my feet and hands for me.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Analects* records Zengzi summoning his disciples at the end to prove his limbs had escaped harm.

sentences.zh: 』」注云：「父母全己生之，亦當全而歸之。

translations.literal: "'" The commentary says: "Parents gave birth to one's person whole; one should also return it whole.'
translations.idiomatic: The gloss explains: one's parents bore a complete body, so one must return it unharmed.

sentences.zh: 」忝，辱也。

translations.literal: " Tian means disgrace.
translations.idiomatic: *Tian* means to bring shame.

sentences.zh: 先子謂先人也。

translations.literal: Xian zi means one's forebear.
translations.idiomatic: *Xian zi* here means a departed father or ancestor.

sentences.zh: 孟子曾西曰：「吾先子之所畏。」

translations.literal: Mencius records Zeng Xi as saying: 'He whom my forebear feared.'"
translations.idiomatic: Mencius quotes Zeng Xi: "The man my father stood in awe of."'

sentences.zh: 篆生毅，以疾隱身不仕。

translations.literal: Zhuan begat Yi, who by illness hid his person and did not take office.
translations.idiomatic: Zhuan's son was Yi, who pleaded sickness and lived in seclusion, never entering government.

sentences.zh: 毅生駰，年十三能通詩﹑易﹑春秋，博學有偉才，盡通古今訓詁百家之言，善屬文。

translations.literal: Yi begat Yin; at age thirteen he could penetrate the *Odes*, *Changes*, and *Spring and Autumn*; he was broadly learned and had great talent, thoroughly mastering the glosses of antiquity and modernity and the words of the hundred schools, and was skilled at composing literary pieces.
translations.idiomatic: Yi fathered Yin, who at thirteen had mastered the *Shijing*, *Zhou yi*, and *Chunqiu*; he was encyclopedic and brilliantly gifted, commandeering every commentary school old and new, and he wrote superbly.

sentences.zh: 少游太學，與班固﹑傅毅同時齊名。

translations.literal: In youth he wandered the Imperial Academy and at the same time shared fame with Ban Gu and Fu Yi.
translations.idiomatic: As a young man he studied at the imperial university alongside Ban Gu and Fu Yi, his name ranked with theirs.

sentences.zh: 常以典籍為業，未遑仕進之事。

translations.literal: He always took the canon and documents as his occupation and had not yet attended to the matter of advancing in office.
translations.idiomatic: He devoted himself to the classics and had no time yet for the ladder of official promotion.

sentences.zh: 時人或譏其太玄靜，將以後名失實。

translations.literal: People of the time some ridiculed him for being too profoundly still, thinking he would lose substance while chasing a later reputation.
translations.idiomatic: Some contemporaries mocked his extreme quietism, warning he would sacrifice real achievement for a posthumous name.

sentences.zh: 駰擬楊雄解嘲，作達旨以荅焉。

translations.literal: Yin, modeling himself on Yang Xiong's *Disrobing the Mockers*, composed *Penetrating the Intent* to answer them.
translations.idiomatic: Yin answered them with a piece modeled on Yang Xiong's *Jie chao*, titled *Dazhi* ('Penetrating the Intent').

sentences.zh: [一]其辭曰：

translations.literal: [Note one] The words thereof say:
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] It opens thus:

sentences.zh: 注[一]華嶠書曰：「駰譏楊雄，以為范﹑蔡﹑鄒衍之徒，乘釁相傾，誑曜諸侯者也，而云『彼我異時』。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Hua Qiao's book says: 'Yin ridiculed Yang Xiong for holding that men like Fan Ju, Cai Ze, and Zou Yan, who seized openings to overthrow one another and dazzled the feudal lords, could yet say "they and I lived in different times."'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Hua Qiao records Yin faulting Yang Xiong for excusing the sophists Fan and Cai and Zou Yan—men who preyed on rulers' weaknesses—by saying they belonged to another age.

sentences.zh: 又曰，竊貲卓氏，割炙細君，斯蓋士之贅行，而云『不能與此數公者同』。

translations.literal: It also says he secretly borrowed from the Zhuo clan's wealth, carved roast meat for his darling wife—conduct that is mere excrescence on a gentleman—yet claimed he "could not be classed with such gentlemen."
translations.idiomatic: Yin also cites Xiong's petty borrowing from the Zhuos and slicing meat for his wife—hardly noble conduct—while insisting he was not of their kind.

sentences.zh: 以為失類而改之也。」

translations.literal: He thought Xiong had missed the category and revised it.'"
translations.idiomatic: Yin judged that self-portrait inconsistent and rewrote the argument.'"

sentences.zh: 或說己曰：「易稱『備物致用』，『可觀而有所合』，故能扶陽以出，順陰而入。

translations.literal: Someone urged him, saying: 'The *Changes* says "prepare things to extend their use" and "may be contemplated and has what fits together"; therefore it can support yang in going out and follow yin in entering.
translations.idiomatic: A visitor pressed him: "The *Zhou yi* speaks of readying tools for use and of what can be viewed and brought into harmony—so yang may rise outward and yin sink inward in due turn."

sentences.zh: [一] 春發其華，秋收其實，有始有極，爰登其質。

translations.literal: [Note one] In spring it sends forth its blossoms; in autumn it gathers its fruit; having a beginning and an end, thereby it mounts its substance.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Spring opens the flower, autumn stores the fruit; from start to finish the inner substance matures.

sentences.zh: 今子韞櫝六經，服膺道術，[二] 歷世而游，高談有日，俯鉤深於重淵，仰探遠乎九干，[三]窮至賾於幽微，測潛隱之無源。

translations.literal: Now you have wrapped the Six Classics in a casket and clasped the Way and its arts to your breast, [note two] passed through generations while roaming, and for many days held lofty discourse; below you hook the deep in layered abysses, above you probe the far in the nine heavens, [note three] exhaust the utmost profundities in what is hidden and subtle, and measure what lurks unseen without a source.
translations.idiomatic: Yet you keep the six classics locked in a casket and the Way pressed to your heart, [note two] wander age after age, and hold court in lofty talk; you fish the deepest pools and reach toward the ninefold sky, [note three] sound the subtlest recesses and chart the sourceless depths of the hidden.

sentences.zh: 然下不步卿相之廷，上不登王公之門，進不黨以贊己，退不黷於庸人。

translations.literal: Yet below you do not tread the courts of ministers and chancellors, above you do not ascend the gates of kings and dukes; in advancing you do not factionalize to praise yourself, in withdrawing you do not defile yourself among common men.
translations.idiomatic: Still you never cross a chief minister's threshold or climb a prince's stair; you neither intrigue on the way up nor wallow with the vulgar on the way down.

sentences.zh: [四]獨師友道德，合符曩真，抱景特立，與士不腢。

translations.literal: [Note four] Alone you take teachers and friends in virtue and the Way, match tallies with truth of old, embrace the light and stand apart, and do not haggle with gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: [Note four] You make morality your only company, keep faith with ancient truth, stand in the radiance alone, and refuse to haggle with the herd.

sentences.zh: 蓋高樹靡陰，獨木不林，隨時之宜，道貴從凡。

translations.literal: A high tree casts no shade; a single tree does not make a forest; the right action for the time—on the Way, the precious thing is to follow the common run.
translations.idiomatic: Lofty timber spreads no shade; one trunk is not a grove; the Way esteems moving with the season and not scorning the common path.

sentences.zh: [五]於時太上運天德以君世，憲王僚而布官；

translations.literal: [Note five] At this time the Supreme One applied Heaven's virtue to rule the age, modeled the kings' offices and spread out the posts;
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] Meanwhile the sovereign wields Heaven's de to govern, patterns his bureaucracy on the ancient kings,

sentences.zh: [六] 臨雍泮以恢儒，疏軒冕以崇賢；

translations.literal: [note six] presides at the Yong and Pan pools to enlarge Confucian learning, and loosens chariot canopies and caps to honor the worthy;
translations.idiomatic: [note six] opens the imperial academy and regional schools to exalt the ru, and strips rank from the idle to exalt the able;

sentences.zh: [七]率惇德以厲忠孝，揚茂化以砥仁義；

translations.literal: [note seven] leads men of honest virtue to sharpen loyalty and filial piety, raises a luxuriant transformation to whet humanity and righteousness;
translations.idiomatic: [note seven] summons the steadfast to hone loyalty and filial piety, and spreads a rich moral teaching to sharpen humanity and right;

sentences.zh: [八] 選利器於良材，求鏌□於明智。

translations.literal: [note eight] selects sharp tools from fine timber, seeks the Mo-ye swords from the clear-sighted and wise.
translations.idiomatic: [note eight] chooses keen blades from sound wood and seeks *Mo ye* steel in the discerning mind.

sentences.zh: [九]不以此時攀台階，窺紫闥，[一０]據高軒，望朱闕，夫欲千里而咫尺未發，[一一]蒙竊惑焉。

translations.literal: [Note nine] If not at this time to climb the terrace steps and peer into the purple portals, [Note ten] to occupy the high carriage and gaze on the vermillion tower—though you desire a thousand leagues, an inch of the foot has not stirred, [Note eleven] I privately am perplexed.
translations.idiomatic: [Note nine] Yet you will not mount the triple steps or peer through the purple doors, [Note ten] take the high seat or look upon the scarlet gate—you would ride a thousand *li* but never spur the horse; [Note eleven] frankly, I am baffled.

sentences.zh: 故英人乘斯時也，[一二]猶逸禽之赴深林，□蚋之趣大沛。

translations.literal: Therefore the outstanding man, riding this moment, [note twelve] is like a swift bird darting to deep forest, like gnats racing to the great marsh.
translations.idiomatic: So the exceptional man seizes the hour, [note twelve] like game bolting for thick woods or midges swarming to a broad mere.

sentences.zh: [一三]胡為嘿嘿而久沉滯也？」

translations.literal: [Note thirteen] Why do you stay silent and long sunk in stagnation?'"
translations.idiomatic: [Note thirteen] Why sit mute and mired while the tide runs?'"

sentences.zh: 注[一]「備物致用」，易系辭之文也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: 'Prepare things to extend their use' is wording from the *Appended Remarks* of the *Changes*.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The phrase comes from the *Xi ci* of the *Zhou yi*.

sentences.zh: 「可觀而有所合」，序卦之文也。

translations.literal: 'May be contemplated and has what fits together' is wording from the *Ordering the Hexagrams*.
translations.idiomatic: The second phrase is from the *Xu gua* sequence.

sentences.zh: 鄭玄注易干鑿度曰：「陽起於子，陰起於午，天數大分。

translations.literal: Zheng Xuan's commentary on the *Changes* *Qian zao du* says: 'Yang arises at zi, yin arises at wu; Heaven's numbers divide greatly.
translations.idiomatic: Zheng Xuan on the *Qian zao du* says yang is born at the zi hour and yin at the wu—the great cleavage of celestial numbers.

sentences.zh: 以陽出離，以陰入坎，坎為中男，離為中女。

translations.literal: By yang one goes out through Li, by yin one enters through Kan; Kan is the middle son, Li is the middle daughter.
translations.idiomatic: Yang exits through *Li*, yin enters through *Kan*; *Kan* is the middle male trigram, *Li* the middle female.

sentences.zh: 太一之行，出從中男，入從中女。

translations.literal: The march of the Grand Unity: going out follows the middle son, entering follows the middle daughter.
translations.idiomatic: The Grand Unity's circuit: departure follows the middle male, return the middle female.

sentences.zh: 因陰陽男女之偶為終始也。」

translations.literal: Thus by the pairing of yin and yang, male and female, it makes end and beginning.'"
translations.idiomatic: So the coupling of yin and yang, man and woman, frames every cycle from end to start.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]韞，匣也。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Yun means a case.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Yun* is a jewel box.

sentences.zh: 櫝，匱也。

translations.literal: Du means a chest.
translations.idiomatic: *Du* is a chest or coffer.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「有美玉，韞櫝而臧諸。」

translations.literal: The *Analects* says: 'There is a fine jade here—wrapped in a case and stored—should I seek a good price and sell it?'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Analects* asks whether the good jade should stay boxed or go to market.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]易曰：「探賾索隱，鉤深致遠。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: The *Changes* says: 'Probe the profundities and search the hidden, hook the deep and reach the far.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zhou yi* line on plumbing depth and distance.

sentences.zh: 」九干謂天有九重也。

translations.literal: " The nine *gan* means Heaven has nine layers.
translations.idiomatic: The 'nine *gan*' means the ninefold vault of heaven.

sentences.zh: 離騷天問曰：「圓則九重，孰營度之？」

translations.literal: The *Encountering Sorrow* and *Heavenly Questions* say: 'Round Heaven has ninefold—who measured and laid it out?'"
translations.idiomatic: *Li sao* and *Tian wen* ask who laid out the nine spheres of the round sky.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]贊猶稱也。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: Zan means to praise or declare.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zan* means to tout or recommend.

sentences.zh: 注[五]華嶠書作「高樹不庇」。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Hua Qiao's book writes 'a high tree gives no shelter.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Hua Qiao's text reads 'tall trees cast no shade.'

sentences.zh: 易曰：「隨時之義大矣哉！

translations.literal: The *Changes* says: 'How great is the meaning of suiting the time!'
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou yi* praises the meaning of moving with the hour.

sentences.zh: 」老子曰：「和其光而同其塵。

translations.literal: " Laozi says: 'Blunt its sharpness and align its light; merge with the dust.'
translations.idiomatic: Laozi says to temper brilliance and mingle with the dust of the world.

sentences.zh: 」故言道貴從凡。

translations.literal: " Therefore it says on the Way the precious thing is to follow the common run.
translations.idiomatic: Hence the gloss: the Way values joining the multitude.

sentences.zh: 注[六]太上，明帝也。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: Taishang is Emperor Ming.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Taishang* denotes Emperor Ming (Liu Zhuang).

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「太上立德。

translations.literal: A tradition says: 'The Supreme One establishes virtue.'
translations.idiomatic: Classic phrase: the highest ruler plants virtue.

sentences.zh: 」天德，含弘光大也。

translations.literal: " Heaven's virtue means embracing, enlarging, and making bright.
translations.idiomatic: Heaven's virtue is broad, bright, and all-encompassing.

sentences.zh: 易曰：「乃位乎天德。

translations.literal: The *Changes* says: 'Then he occupies the position of Heaven's virtue.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou yi* says he then occupies the station of Heaven's virtue.

sentences.zh: 」尚書曰：「唐虞稽古，建官惟百，夏商官倍，亦克用乂。

translations.literal: " The *Documents* says: 'Tang and Yu examined antiquity; they established offices, only a hundred; Xia and Shang doubled the offices, yet were able to bring order.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Shang shu* recalls how Tang and Yu modeled antiquity with a hundred posts, while Xia and Shang doubled them yet kept good rule.'"

sentences.zh: 」憲，法也。

translations.literal: " Xian means model or law.
translations.idiomatic: *Xian* means to take as pattern.

sentences.zh: 僚，官也。

translations.literal: Liao means office.
translations.idiomatic: *Liao* means an official post.

sentences.zh: 言法三王而建官也。

translations.literal: It means he modeled the Three Kings in establishing offices.
translations.idiomatic: The point is that the court patterned its bureaucracy on the three sage dynasties.

sentences.zh: 注[七]天子辟雍，諸侯頖宮。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: The Son of Heaven has the Yong, the feudal lords have the Pan palace.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The Son of Heaven's round moat school is *biyong*; feudal lords had the half-scale *pan* school.

sentences.zh: 璧雍者，環之以水，圓而如璧也。

translations.literal: Biyong: it is encircled with water, round like a jade disk.
translations.idiomatic: *Biyong* is ringed with water, round as a ritual jade *bi*.

sentences.zh: 頖，半也。

translations.literal: Pan means half.
translations.idiomatic: *Pan* means 'half.'

sentences.zh: 諸侯半天子之宮。

translations.literal: The feudal lords had half the Son of Heaven's palace.
translations.idiomatic: Their schools were half the imperial model.

sentences.zh: 皆所以立學垂教也。

translations.literal: Both are the means whereby schools were founded and teaching handed down.
translations.idiomatic: Both institutions founded learning and transmitted doctrine.

sentences.zh: 注[八]砥，礪也。

translations.literal: Comment on note eight: Di means whetstone.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Di* is the grindstone.

sentences.zh: 注[九]吳越春秋曰：「干將，吳人也，造二□，一曰干將，二曰莫邪。

translations.literal: Comment on note nine: The *Annals of Wu and Yue* says: 'Gan Jiang was a man of Wu who forged two swords, one called Gan Jiang, the second called Mo Ye.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Wu Yue chunqiu* names Gan Jiang of Wu, who forged two blades—one Gan Jiang, one Mo Ye.

sentences.zh: 莫邪者，干將之妻名也。

translations.literal: Mo Ye was the name of Gan Jiang's wife.
translations.idiomatic: Mo Ye was his wife's name.

sentences.zh: 干將作□，采五山之精，合六金之英，百神臨觀，遂以成□。

translations.literal: Gan Jiang forged swords, gathered the essence of the five mountains, combined the splendor of the six metals; the hundred spirits looked on, and thereby the swords were completed.
translations.idiomatic: He smelted the quintessence of five peaks and six metals while the gods watched the blades take shape.

sentences.zh: 」說苑曰：「所以尚干將、莫邪者，貴其立斷。

translations.literal: " The *Garden of Persuasions* says: 'The reason Gan Jiang and Mo Ye are prized is that they cut the instant they are set down.
translations.idiomatic: The *Shuo yuan* says Gan Jiang and Mo Ye are prized for cutting clean at a stroke.

sentences.zh: 所以尚騏驎者，貴其立至。

translations.literal: The reason the unicorn steeds are prized is that they arrive the instant they are sent.
translations.idiomatic: The *qilin* mounts are prized for arriving the moment they are spurred.

sentences.zh: 必且歷日曠久，絲犛猶能栔石，駑馬亦能致遠。

translations.literal: If it must take days and stretch long, even silken cord can saw stone, and a nag horse can also reach a far place.
translations.idiomatic: Given enough time, silk can saw rock and a plug horse will still reach the distance.

sentences.zh: 是以聰明敏捷，人之美材也。」

translations.literal: Therefore quickness and keen intelligence are the fine timber of man.'"
translations.idiomatic: So wit and agility are a man's true edge.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一０]三台謂之三階，三公之象也。

translations.literal: Comment on note ten: The three terraces are called the three steps; they are the image of the three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The three platforms symbolize the three high ministers.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]八寸為咫。

translations.literal: Comment on note eleven: Eight inches make a *zhi*.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Eight inches equal one *zhi* (foot span).

sentences.zh: 注[一二]文子曰：「智過萬人謂之英，千人謂之俊。」

translations.literal: Comment on note twelve: Wenzi says: 'Surpassing ten thousand men is called *ying*; a thousand, *jun*.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Wen zi* ranks the outstanding: above ten thousand is *ying*, above a thousand *jun*.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一三]蚋，小蟲，蚊之類。

translations.literal: Comment on note thirteen: Rui is a small insect, a kind of mosquito.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Rui* is a gnat-like midge.

sentences.zh: 蚋音芮。

translations.literal: Rui is read like rui.
translations.idiomatic: Pronounced *rui*.

sentences.zh: 說文曰：「秦謂之蚋，楚謂之蚊。

translations.literal: The *Shuowen* says: 'Qin calls it rui; Chu calls it wen.
translations.idiomatic: The *Shuowen* says Qin dialect called it *rui*, Chu called it *wen*.

sentences.zh: 」孟子曰：「污池沛澤。

translations.literal: " Mencius says: 'Muddy pools and teeming marshes.'
translations.idiomatic: Mencius speaks of 'stagnant pools and lush marshes.'

sentences.zh: 」劉熙曰：「沛，水草相半。」

translations.literal: " Liu Xi says: 'Pei means water and plants half and half.'"
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xi glosses *pei* as marsh where water and reeds mingle.'"

sentences.zh: 荅曰：「有是言乎？

translations.literal: He answered: 'Is there such talk?
translations.idiomatic: Yin replied: "Do you really say so?

sentences.zh: 子苟欲勉我以世路，不知其跌而失吾之度也。

translations.literal: If you wish to urge me along the world's road, you do not know that in stumbling you would make me miss my measure.
translations.idiomatic: If you mean to hustle me onto the world's highway, you ignore how easily a false step would cost me my footing.

sentences.zh: 古者陰陽始分，天地初制，[一]皇綱雲緒，帝紀乃設，傳序歷數，三代興滅。

translations.literal: In antiquity when yin and yang first divided and Heaven and Earth were first established, [note one] the royal net was like clouds and threads, the imperial record was then set up, tradition handed down the calendar's reckoning, and the three dynasties rose and perished.
translations.idiomatic: When yin and yang first split and the cosmos was framed, [note one] Heaven's net was woven, the imperial chronicle began, regnal lines succeeded one another, and the three dynasties rose and fell in turn.

sentences.zh: 昔大庭尚矣，赫胥罔識。

translations.literal: Formerly the Great Court age lies too far off; the Herxu age cannot be known.
translations.idiomatic: The age of Great Court lies beyond memory; the Herxu age cannot be known.

sentences.zh: [二] 淳□散離，人物錯乖。

translations.literal: [Note two] Simple harmony scattered and parted; things and persons went awry.
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] Primal simplicity dissolved; creatures and customs fell out of joint.

sentences.zh: 高辛攸降，厥趣各違。

translations.literal: Gaoxin descended; each inclination went its own way.
translations.idiomatic: Under Gaoxin each nature took its divergent course.

sentences.zh: [三]道無常稽，與時張□。

translations.literal: [Note three] The Way has no constant track; with the times it stretches wide.
translations.idiomatic: [Note three] The Way keeps no fixed path; it widens or narrows with the age.

sentences.zh: [四] 失仁為非，得義為是。

translations.literal: [Note four] To lose humanity is wrong; to gain righteousness is right.
translations.idiomatic: [Note four] To abandon humanity is evil; to grasp righteousness is good.

sentences.zh: [五]君子通變，各審所履。

translations.literal: [Note five] The gentleman adapts to change and each examines what he treads.
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] The noble man shifts with circumstance and tests every step he takes.

sentences.zh: 故士或掩目而淵潛，[六]或盥耳而山棲；

translations.literal: Therefore some gentlemen cover their eyes and hide in the depths, [note six] or wash their ears and dwell on the mountains;
translations.idiomatic: So some veil their sight and dive into the deeps, [note six] or rinse their ears and live on peaks;

sentences.zh: [七]或草耕而僅飽，[八]或木茹而長饑；

translations.literal: [note seven] or plough with grass and barely eat their fill, [note eight] or eat tree bark and long hunger;
translations.idiomatic: [note seven] some weed a plot and barely fill the belly, [note eight] some gnaw bark and hunger for years;

sentences.zh: [九]或重聘而不來，[一０]或屢黜而不去；

translations.literal: [note nine] or receive heavy summons yet will not come, [note ten] or are repeatedly dismissed yet will not leave;
translations.idiomatic: [note nine] some spurn rich appointments, [note ten] some stay on after repeated demotion;

sentences.zh: [一一]或冒紘以干進，或望色而斯舉；

translations.literal: [note eleven] or don the grand cap to importune for promotion, or watch the ruler's mood and then rise;
translations.idiomatic: [note eleven] some beg office in full regalia, some leap forward at a sovereign's glance;

sentences.zh: [一二]或以役夫發夢於王公，[一三]或以漁父見兆於元龜。

translations.literal: [note twelve] or a laborer sends a dream to a king or duke, [note thirteen] or a fisherman reads omens from the great tortoise.
translations.idiomatic: [note twelve] a commoner may dream his way to a prince's ear, [note thirteen] an angler may read omens on the sacred tortoise.

sentences.zh: [一四]若夫紛□塞路，凶虐播流，[一五] 人有昏墊之□，主有疇咨之憂，[一六]條垂藟蔓，上下相求。

translations.literal: [Note fourteen] When disorder chokes the roads and cruel havoc spreads abroad, [note fifteen] the people meet drowning and darkness, the lord has the chouzi lament, [note sixteen] branches hang down and vines creep—above and below seek one another.
translations.idiomatic: [Note fourteen] When chaos blocks every road and cruelty floods the land, [note fifteen] the people sink in flood and gloom while the ruler groans his chouzi lament, [note sixteen] then branch and vine intertwine and high and low reach for each other.

sentences.zh: [一七]於是乎賢人授手，援世之災，[一八]跋涉赴俗，急斯時也。

translations.literal: [Note seventeen] Then worthies stretch out a hand and rescue the age's calamity, [note eighteen] trudge through mud to join the vulgar—urgent is that hour.
translations.idiomatic: [Note seventeen] Then the worthy lend a hand to save the world from disaster, [note eighteen] wade through the mire of common life, for that is the pressing moment.

sentences.zh: [一九]昔堯含戚而戲陶謨，高祖歎而子房慮；

translations.literal: [Note nineteen] Formerly Yao bore sorrow for the flood while shaping the *Tao mo* counsel; Gaozu sighed and Zhang Liang laid his plans;
translations.idiomatic: [Note nineteen] Yao bore care yet rehearsed the *Tao mo*; Gaozu groaned and Zhang Liang weighed the moment;

sentences.zh: [二０]禍不散而曹、絳奮，[二一]結不解而陳平權。

translations.literal: [Note twenty] calamity not yet dispersed yet Cao Can and Zhou Bo roused themselves; [note twenty-one] knots not yet undone yet Chen Ping wielded stratagem.
translations.idiomatic: [note twenty] before the peril lifted, Cao Can and Zhou Bo stirred; [note twenty-one] before the tangle cleared, Chen Ping took the scales.

sentences.zh: [二二] 及其策合道從，克亂弭沖，乃將鏤玄珪，冊顯功，[二三]銘昆吾之冶，[二四] 勒景、襄之鐘。

translations.literal: [Note twenty-two] When their plans matched the Way and they could quell chaos and still the flood, then they would carve dark jade tablets and inscribe manifest merit, [note twenty-three] inscribe the smelting furnace of Kunwu, [note twenty-four] cut the bells of Jing and Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-two] When counsel ran with the Way and they could crush revolt and calm the surge, they engraved black jade patents and wrote up glorious deeds, [note twenty-three] cast legends on the Kunwu forge, [note twenty-four] and cut inscriptions on the bells of Jing and Xiang.

sentences.zh: [二五]與其有事，則褰裳濡足，冠掛不顧。

translations.literal: [Note twenty-five] When there was business for them, they tucked up skirts and soaked their feet, caps hung and they did not look back.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-five] In crisis they hitched robes, waded deep, caps snagged—they never looked back.

sentences.zh: [二六]人溺不拯，則非仁也。

translations.literal: [Note twenty-six] If a man is drowning and one does not save him, that is not humanity.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-six] To let a neighbor drown is to fail humanity.

sentences.zh: 當其無事，則躐纓整襟，規矩其步。

translations.literal: When there was no business for them, they straightened tassel and lapel and measured their paces by compass and square.
translations.idiomatic: In quiet times they smoothed sash and collar and walked to the measure of ritual.

sentences.zh: [二七]德讓不修，則非忠也。

translations.literal: [Note twenty-seven] If yielding virtue is not cultivated, that is not loyalty.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-seven] Without courteous deference there is no loyalty.

sentences.zh: 是以險則救俗，平則守禮，舉以公心，不私其體。

translations.literal: Therefore in danger they rescue custom; in peace they guard ritual; they act from a public heart and do not privatize their persons.
translations.idiomatic: So in peril they save the manners of the age; in calm they keep the rites; they act from an impartial mind and never trade the self for private gain.

sentences.zh: 注[一]制，協韻音之設反。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Zhi, for rhyme, is read with initial ts and final shet in the fanqie spelling.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhi* is given a fanqie reading for euphony in the verse.

sentences.zh: 注[二]大庭、赫胥並古帝王號也。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Great Court and Hecun are both designations of ancient emperors.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Great Court* and *Hecu* are legendary royal epithets.

sentences.zh: 尚，遠也。

translations.literal: Shang means remote.
translations.idiomatic: Here *shang* means 'distant in time.'

sentences.zh: 罔，無也。

translations.literal: Wang means there is not.
translations.idiomatic: *Wang* means 'there is none' or 'un-.'

sentences.zh: 識，記也。

translations.literal: Shi means to record.
translations.idiomatic: *Shi* means 'to remember' or 'record.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]高辛氏，帝嚳也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: The Gaoxin clan is Emperor Ku.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gaoxin is the clan name of Emperor Ku (Di Ku).

sentences.zh: 注[四]隨時□張，不考之於常道也。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: 'Suit the times' stretches wide—this is not to be tested by a constant Way.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Moving with the hour expands or contracts; it cannot be judged by a single fixed rule.

sentences.zh: 注[五]老子曰：「失道後德，失德後仁，失仁後義，失義後禮。」

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Laozi says: 'When the Way was lost, then came virtue; when virtue was lost, then came humanity; when humanity was lost, then came righteousness; when righteousness was lost, then came ritual.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: Laozi's sequence of decline from Dao down to ritual.'"

sentences.zh: 注[六]莊子曰「北人無澤與舜為友，舜以天下讓之，無澤乃自投清泠之淵，終身不反」也。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: Zhuangzi says: 'The man of the north, Wuze, was friend to Shun; Shun wished to yield All under Heaven to him; Wuze then threw himself into the clear cold abyss and never returned.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhuangzi* tells how Wuze of the north drowned himself rather than accept Shun's abdication.'"

sentences.zh: 注[七]盥，洗也。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: Guan means to wash.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Guan* is to rinse or wash.

sentences.zh: 許由字武仲，隱於沛澤之中。

translations.literal: Xu You, courtesy name Wuzhong, hid among the marshes of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Xu You, styled Wuzhong, lived hidden in the Pei marshlands.

sentences.zh: 堯聞之，乃致天下而讓焉。

translations.literal: When Yao heard of him, he sent him All under Heaven and wished to yield it.
translations.idiomatic: Yao heard of him and tried to cede the empire to him.

sentences.zh: 由以為污，乃臨池洗耳。

translations.literal: You thought it filthy and went to the pool to wash his ears.
translations.idiomatic: Xu You thought the offer defiled him and went to the stream to rinse his ears.

sentences.zh: 其友巢父飲犢，聞由為堯所讓，曰：「何以污吾犢口！

translations.literal: His friend Chao Fu was watering a calf; hearing that Yao had tried to yield to You, he said: 'How can you defile the mouth of my calf!'
translations.idiomatic: His friend Chao Fu was watering a calf; learning that Yao had offered the throne to Xu You, he cried, 'You would foul my calf's muzzle!'

sentences.zh: 」牽於上流而飲之。

translations.literal: "' He led it upstream to drink.
translations.idiomatic: He led the beast upstream to drink clear water.

sentences.zh: 見莊子及高士傳。

translations.literal: See Zhuangzi and the *Biographies of High Gentlemen*.
translations.idiomatic: The tale appears in *Zhuangzi* and the *Gaoshi zhuan*.

sentences.zh: 注[八]伯成子高，唐虞時為諸侯。

translations.literal: Comment on note eight: Bo Cheng Zigao was a feudal lord in the time of Tang and Yu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Bo Cheng Zigao held a fief under Yao and Shun.

sentences.zh: 至禹，去而耕。

translations.literal: When it came to Yu, he left and ploughed.
translations.idiomatic: When Yu took power, he resigned and took up the plough.

sentences.zh: 禹往見之，則耕在野。

translations.literal: Yu went to see him, and he was ploughing in the wilds.
translations.idiomatic: Yu sought him out and found him hoeing in the fields.

sentences.zh: 見呂氏春秋。

translations.literal: See the *Lüshi chunqiu*.
translations.idiomatic: Recorded in the *Lüshi chunqiu*.

sentences.zh: 注[九]說苑曰：「鮑焦衣木皮，食木實。

translations.literal: Comment on note nine: The *Garden of Persuasions* says: 'Bao Jiao wore tree bark and ate tree fruit.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Shuo yuan* describes Bao Jiao clothed in bark, feeding on wild fruit.

sentences.zh: 」韓詩外傳曰「焦□其蔬，而立槁死於洛濱」也。

translations.literal: " The *Han shi waizhuan* says: 'Jiao [acted] on his vegetables and stood withered to death on the bank of the Luo.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shi waizhuan* adds that he tended his greens and starved himself dead on the Luo.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一０]狂接輿者，楚人也。

translations.literal: Comment on note ten: Mad Jieyu was a man of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'Mad Jieyu' was a recluse of Chu.

sentences.zh: 耕而食。

translations.literal: He ploughed and fed himself.
translations.idiomatic: He lived by the plough and his own labor.

sentences.zh: 楚王聞其賢，使使者持金百溢、車二駟聘之，曰：「願煩先生理江南。

translations.literal: The king of Chu heard of his worth and sent envoys with a hundred yi of gold and two teams of four horses to summon him, saying: 'I trouble you, sir, to put in order the lands south of the river.'"
