translations.idiomatic: The king of Chu, hearing of his virtue, sent messengers with a hundred *yi* of gold and two four-horse chariots, begging him to administer the south of the Yangzi.'"

sentences.zh: 」接輿笑而不應。

translations.literal: " Jieyu laughed and did not answer.
translations.idiomatic: Jieyu only laughed and gave no reply.

sentences.zh: 使者去而遠徙，莫知所之。

translations.literal: When the envoys departed, he moved far away; none knew where he went.
translations.idiomatic: When the envoys left, he vanished to parts unknown.

sentences.zh: 見莊子。

translations.literal: See Zhuangzi.
translations.idiomatic: See *Zhuangzi*.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]論語曰「柳下惠為士師，三黜。

translations.literal: Comment on note eleven: The *Analects* says: 'Liuxia Hui was criminal judge and was dismissed three times.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Analects* says Liuxia Hui served as judge yet was cashiered thrice.

sentences.zh: 人曰：『可以去矣。

translations.literal: People said: 'You may leave.'
translations.idiomatic: People told him, 'You could go elsewhere.'

sentences.zh: 』曰：『直道而事人，何往而不三黜』」也。

translations.literal: "' He said: 'If with the straight Way I serve men, whither could I go and not be dismissed three times?'"'
translations.idiomatic: He answered: 'If I serve others with a straight path, where could I go without three dismissals?'"'

sentences.zh: 注[一二]紘，辱也，音火豆反。

translations.literal: Comment on note twelve: Hong means disgrace; it is read with initial x and final du in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Hong* here means 'disgrace'; the commentary gives fanqie.

sentences.zh: 新序曰：「伊尹蒙恥辱，負鼎俎以干湯。

translations.literal: The *Xin xu* says: 'Yi Yin bore disgrace, carrying the cauldron and stand to seek audience with Tang.'
translations.idiomatic: *Xin xu* records Yi Yin enduring shame, bearing pots to importune Tang.

sentences.zh: 」論語曰：「色斯舉矣，翔而後集。

translations.literal: " The *Analects* says: 'At a change in the ruler's mood, they rise; they wheel and then settle.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Analects* line: when the sovereign's color shifts, the birds rise, circle, and settle.

sentences.zh: 」舉，協韻音據。

translations.literal: " Ju, for rhyme, is read like ju.
translations.idiomatic: The word *ju* is given a rhyming reading.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]高宗夢得說，乃使百工營求諸野，得諸傅巖。

translations.literal: Comment on note thirteen: Gaozong dreamed he had obtained Yue and ordered the hundred crafts to search the wilds and found him at Fu Rock.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gaozong's dream of Yue and the search that found him at Fu Yan.

sentences.zh: 孔安國曰：「傅氏之巖，在虞、虢之界，信道所經，有澗水壞道，常使胥靡刑人築護此道。

translations.literal: Kong Anguo says: 'The Fu clan's cliff lay on the border of Yu and Guo, on a road travelers used; a stream had ruined the road, so they always made convict laborers wall and maintain it.'
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo explains the cliff between Yu and Guo where convicts were forced to repair the road.

sentences.zh: 說賢而隱，代胥靡築之以供食。

translations.literal: Yue was worthy but hid; he substituted for the convict laborers in building it to earn his food.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Yue hid his worth and worked as a conscript builder for his meals.

sentences.zh: 」事見尚書。

translations.literal: " The affair is seen in the *Documents*.'"
translations.idiomatic: The full story is in the *Shang shu*.'"

sentences.zh: 王公，總而言也。

translations.literal: Kings and dukes—speaking in general terms.
translations.idiomatic: 'Kings and dukes' is a blanket term for rulers.

sentences.zh: 爾雅：「皇、王、後、辟、公、侯，君也。」

translations.literal: The *Erya* says: 'Huang, wang, hou, pi, gong, and hou all mean lord.'"
translations.idiomatic: *Erya* lists royal titles as synonyms for 'ruler.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一四]戰國策曰：「呂尚之遇文王也，身為漁父。

translations.literal: Comment on note fourteen: The *Strategies of the Warring States* says: 'When Lü Shang met King Wen, his person was that of a fisherman.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhanguo ce* says Lü Shang first met King Wen as a simple angler.

sentences.zh: 」史記曰：「太公以釣干周西伯。

translations.literal: " The *Records of the Grand Historian* says: 'The Grand Duke angled to importune the Western Earl of Zhou.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Shiji* says the Grand Duke fished to catch the Western Earl's notice.'"

sentences.zh: 西伯將出獵，卜之，曰：『所獲非龍非螭，非熊非羆，所獲霸王之輔。

translations.literal: The Western Earl was about to go hunting and divined, saying: 'What is caught will be neither dragon nor chi, neither bear nor brown bear, but the aid of a hegemon king.'"
translations.idiomatic: The earl hunted only after a divination promising no beast but the helper of a hegemon-king.'"

sentences.zh: 』於是西伯獵，果遇太公渭水之陽，與語大說。

translations.literal: " Thereupon the Western Earl hunted and indeed met the Grand Duke on the north bank of the Wei; speaking with him, he was greatly pleased.'"
translations.idiomatic: He hunted north of the Wei, met the Grand Duke, and delighted in their talk.'"

sentences.zh: 」元，大也。

translations.literal: " Yuan means great.
translations.idiomatic: *Yuan* means 'great'—hence the tortoise omen.

sentences.zh: 注[一五]方言云：「□，盛多也。

translations.literal: Comment on note fifteen: The *Fangyan* says: '[Graph missing] means abundant and many.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Fangyan* glosses the word as 'thickly crowded.'

sentences.zh: 」音奴董反。

translations.literal: " It is read with initial n and final dong in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie spelling follows.

sentences.zh: 注[一六]尚書曰：「下人昏墊。

translations.literal: Comment on note sixteen: The *Documents* says: 'Below, the people were benighted and drowned.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shang shu* speaks of the people drowning in flood and gloom.

sentences.zh: 」孔安國曰「昏瞀墊溺，皆困水災也。

translations.literal: " Kong Anguo says: 'Benighted, submerged, drowned—all mean trapped by the water disaster.'"
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo glosses the terms as water-borne distress.'"

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「帝曰：咨洪水滔天，浩浩懷山襄陵，有能俾乂。」

translations.literal: " It also says: 'The Emperor said: Alas, the flood water swells to Heaven, vast and boundless, embracing mountains and overrunning hills—is there anyone who can make it ordered?'"
translations.idiomatic: The text adds Yao's cry over the flood and his search for a minister to tame it.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一七]藟，籐也。

translations.literal: Comment on note seventeen: Lei means vine.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Lei* is the creeping vine.

sentences.zh: 音壘。

translations.literal: It is read like lei.
translations.idiomatic: Read *lei* as in 'pile.'

sentences.zh: 詩曰：「南有樛木，葛藟累之。」

translations.literal: The *Odes* says: 'Southward the bending tree has ge-lei twining it.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Shijing* image of vine on the crooked tree.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一八]孟子曰「天下溺則援之以道，嫂溺則援之以手」也。

translations.literal: Comment on note eighteen: Mencius says: 'When All under Heaven is drowning, rescue it with the Way; when a sister-in-law is drowning, rescue her with the hand.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: Mencius contrasts public rescue by principle with the human duty to save a drowning in-law.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一九]草行為跋。

translations.literal: Comment on note nineteen: Walking through plants is called bo.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Bo* means to trudge through undergrowth.

sentences.zh: 注[二０]謨，謀也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty: Mo means planning.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Mo* means counsel or planning.

sentences.zh: 堯遭洪水，咨嗟憂愁，訪下人有能理者，戲陶、大禹陳其謀。

translations.literal: Yao suffered the flood, sighed and grieved, and asked among his subordinates who could set it in order; at Tao Tang and the Great Yu they laid out their plans.
translations.idiomatic: Yao met the flood with lament, sought talent among his people, and drew up plans with the house of Tao Tang and Great Yu.

sentences.zh: 見尚書。

translations.literal: See the *Documents*.
translations.idiomatic: See the *Shang shu*.

sentences.zh: 史記曰，高祖為項羽所敗，下馬踞鞍而問子房曰：「吾欲捐關以東，誰可與共功者？

translations.literal: The *Records* says: when the Exalted Ancestor was defeated by Xiang Yu, he dismounted, sat on his saddle bow, and asked Zifang: 'I mean to abandon the lands east of the passes—who can share the achievement with me?'
translations.idiomatic: The *Shiji* tells how Liu Bang, beaten by Xiang Yu, sat his saddle and asked Zhang Liang whom to enlist east of Hangu.

sentences.zh: 」子房曰：「九江王布、彭越、韓信。

translations.literal: " Zifang said: 'The king of Jiujiang, Bu; Peng Yue; Han Xin.'"
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Liang named Ying Bu of Jiujiang, Peng Yue, and Han Xin.'"

sentences.zh: 即欲捐之此三人，楚可破*

translations.literal: If we wish to entrust it to these three, Chu can be broken *
translations.idiomatic: Give the eastern lands to those three and Chu can be shattered *

translations.literal: (to)
translations.idiomatic: (to)

sentences.zh: 注[二一]曹參及絳侯周勃，皆從高祖征伐，以定天下也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-one: Cao Can and Marquis Jiang Zhou Bo both followed the Exalted Ancestor on campaign and thereby settled All under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cao Can and Zhou Bo of Jiang followed Liu Bang in the wars that founded the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 注[二二]高祖擊匈奴，至白登，被圍七日，用陳平計得出。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-two: The Exalted Ancestor struck the Xiongnu, reached Baideng, was surrounded seven days, and by Chen Ping's stratagem got out.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Liu Bang's siege at Baideng and Chen Ping's ruse that freed him.

sentences.zh: 注[二三]珪，玉也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-three: Gui means jade.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Gui* is the jade tablet of investiture.

sentences.zh: 詩含神霧曰：「刻之玉版，臧之金匱。」

translations.literal: The *Poetry containing Spiritual Mists* says: 'Carve it on jade tablets, store it in metal coffer.'"
translations.idiomatic: A weft text says deeds were cut in jade and locked in golden chests.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二四]墨子曰：「昔夏後開*

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-four: Mozi says: 'In antiquity the lord of Xia, Kai *
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Mozi* cites how the Xia ruler Kai *

translations.literal: (smelting)
translations.idiomatic: (smelting)

sentences.zh: *使飛廉析金於山，以鑄鼎於昆吾。

translations.literal: *ordered Feilian to split metal on the mountains in order to cast cauldrons at Kunwu.'"
translations.idiomatic: *sent Feilian to split ore on the mountains and cast tripods at Kunwu.'"

sentences.zh: 」蔡邕銘論曰「呂尚作周太師，其功銘於昆吾之鼎」也。

translations.literal: " Cai Yong's *Discourse on Inscriptions* says: 'Lü Shang became grand preceptor of Zhou; his merit was inscribed on the Kunwu cauldron.'"
translations.idiomatic: Cai Yong says Lü Shang's deeds were cast on the Kunwu tripod.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二五]國語曰：「晉魏顆以其身退秦師於輔氏，其勳銘於景鐘。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-five: The *Discourses of the States* says: 'Wei Ke of Jin, with his person, repulsed the Qin army at Fushi; his merit was inscribed on the Jing bell.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Guoyu* praises Wei Ke's victory at Fushi, cut on the Jing bell.

sentences.zh: 」此兼言襄也。

translations.literal: " This also speaks of Xiang.
translations.idiomatic: The line alludes to Xiang as well as Jing.

sentences.zh: 注[二六]褰裳，涉水也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-six: Qian chang means wading through water.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Tucking the robe means fording deep water.

sentences.zh: 新序曰：「今為濡足之故，不救人溺，可乎？

translations.literal: The *Xin xu* says: 'Now, for fear of wetting your feet, not to save a drowning man—is that permissible?'
translations.idiomatic: *Xin xu* asks whether one may refuse to save a drowner for fear of soaked feet.

sentences.zh: 」淮南子曰「禹之趨時，冠掛而不顧，履遺而不取」也。

translations.literal: " The *Huainanzi* says: 'In Yu's haste for the hour, his cap caught and he did not look back; his shoe dropped and he did not pick it up.'"
translations.idiomatic: *Huainanzi* praises Yu's urgency: cap snagged, shoe lost, he never paused.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二七]躐音呂涉反。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-seven: Lie is read with initial l and final shep in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for *lie* (to tread).

sentences.zh: 躐，踐也。

translations.literal: Lie means to tread.
translations.idiomatic: *Lie* means to trample or tread.

sentences.zh: 此字宜從「手」。

translations.literal: This character ought to follow the 'hand' radical.
translations.idiomatic: The received graph should be the hand radical form.

sentences.zh: 廣雅云：「擸，持也。

translations.literal: The *Guangya* says: 'Lie (hand form) means to hold.'
translations.idiomatic: *Guangya* glosses the variant as 'to grasp.'

sentences.zh: 」言持纓整襟，修其容止。

translations.literal: " It means holding the tassel and straightening the lapel, cultivating one's bearing.
translations.idiomatic: That is, adjusting sash and collar to perfect deportment.

sentences.zh: 史記曰：「攝纓整襟。

translations.literal: The *Records* says: 'He adjusted his tassel and straightened his lapel.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Shiji* uses the phrase for composing one's dress.'"

sentences.zh: 」華嶠書「躐」作「攝」也。

translations.literal: " Hua Qiao's book writes *lie* as *she* (to gather).'
translations.idiomatic: Hua Qiao's text reads *she* instead of *lie*.'

sentences.zh: 「今聖上之育斯人也，樸以皇質，雕以唐文。

translations.literal: 'Now the sage sovereign, in nurturing these people, uses plainness for the substance of High Antiquity and carving for the pattern of Tang.'
translations.idiomatic: The present sage, in rearing his subjects, grounds them in the plain stuff of primordial ages and refines them with the culture of Tang.

sentences.zh: [一]六合怡怡，比屋為仁。

translations.literal: [Note one] The six directions are harmonious; every roof shares in kindness.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Within the six regions all is harmony; kindness runs from house to house.

sentences.zh: 壹天下之觿異，齊品類之萬殊。

translations.literal: He unites the myriad differences under Heaven and aligns the ten thousand kinds of things.
translations.idiomatic: He brings the world's diversity under one measure and ranks every category of being.

sentences.zh: 參差同量，壞冶一陶。

translations.literal: Uneven things share one measure; ruin and smelting are one potter's wheel.
translations.idiomatic: Unequal gifts are weighed on one scale; what breaks and what refines share one wheel of fate.

sentences.zh: [二]腢生得理，庶績其凝。

translations.literal: [Note two] When yin and yang attain their proper pattern, the myriad tasks stand firm.
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] When yin and yang keep their due order, every office holds steady.

sentences.zh: [三] 家家有以樂和，人人有以自優。

translations.literal: [Note three] In every home there is harmony to enjoy; in every person there is ease for himself.
translations.idiomatic: [Note three] Each household knows the music of peace; each man may cultivate his own ease.

sentences.zh: 威械臧而俎豆布，六典陳而九刑厝。

translations.literal: Weapons are stored away and the stands of meat and beans are spread; the six canons are displayed and the nine punishments are set aside.
translations.idiomatic: Arms go to the arsenal while ritual vessels fill the hall; the six statutes shine forth and the nine penalties rest unused.

sentences.zh: [四]濟茲兆庶，出於平易之路。

translations.literal: [Note four] He ferries these millions out onto the level road of ease.
translations.idiomatic: [Note four] He leads the myriad commoners onto the broad, easy path.

sentences.zh: 雖有力牧之略，尚父之厲，[五]伊、戲不論，奚事范、蔡？

translations.literal: Though one had the schemes of Li Mu or the fierceness of the Grand Duke, [note five] with Yi Yin and Fu Yue not at issue, why trouble about Fan Ju and Cai Ze?
translations.idiomatic: What need of Li Mu's designs or the Grand Duke's sternness if Yi Yin and Fu Yue go unmentioned—why then stoop to the likes of Fan Ju and Cai Ze?

sentences.zh: [六]夫廣廈成而茂木暢，遠求存而良馬縶，[七]陰事終而水宿臧，[八]場功畢而大火入。

translations.literal: [Note six] When the great hall is built, lush trees flourish; when the distant hunt is kept, good horses are tethered; [note seven] when yin affairs end, water-roosting birds hide away; [note eight] when the threshing floor is cleared, the Great Fire asterism enters.
translations.idiomatic: [Note six] The hall raised, timber thrives; the hunt staged, steeds stand ready; [note seven] yin work finished, marsh birds roost; [note eight] harvest done, the Great Fire star sinks.

sentences.zh: [九]方斯之際，處士山積，學者川流，衣裳被宇，冠蓋雲浮。

translations.literal: [Note nine] At such a season recluses pile up like mountains, scholars flow like rivers, robes cover the realm, caps and canopies float like clouds.
translations.idiomatic: [Note nine] Then hermits crowd like peaks, students stream like currents, robes blanket the land, and official umbrellas mass like clouds.

sentences.zh: 譬猶衡陽之林，岱陰之麓，[一０]伐尋抱不為之稀，蓺拱把不為之數。

translations.literal: [Note ten] It is like the forests south of Heng or the foot of Mount Tai's north slope—[note ten] felling trees an arm-span round does not thin them; gathering handfuls does not count their number.
translations.idiomatic: [Note ten] Like the woods south of Heng or north of Dai—you could hew arm-thick trunks without thinning the stand, or bundle saplings without numbering them.

sentences.zh: [一一]悠悠罔極，亦各有得。

translations.literal: [Note eleven] Vast and boundless without limit, yet each has his gain.
translations.idiomatic: [Note eleven] Endless variety, yet everyone finds his place.

sentences.zh: [一二]彼采其華，我收其實。

translations.literal: [Note twelve] They pluck the blossom; I gather the fruit.
translations.idiomatic: [Note twelve] Others take the showy flower; I take the solid fruit.

sentences.zh: 捨之則臧，己所學也。

translations.literal: To leave it is good—that is what I have studied.
translations.idiomatic: To renounce rank is the good I have learned to pursue.

sentences.zh: [一三]故進動以道，則不辭執珪而秉柱國；

translations.literal: [Note thirteen] Therefore if advancing accords with the Way, one does not refuse to grasp the jade tablet and wield the pillar of state;
translations.idiomatic: [Note thirteen] If moving forward matches the Way, one accepts the jade baton and the pillar ministership without demur;

sentences.zh: [一四]復靜以理，則甘糟糠而安藜藿。

translations.literal: [Note fourteen] if withdrawing accords with principle, one is content with lees and husks and at ease with coarse greens.
translations.idiomatic: [Note fourteen] if retiring accords with right, one gladly eats chaff and wild greens.

sentences.zh: 注[一]孔子曰：「大哉堯之為君也，煥乎其有文章。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Confucius said: 'How great was Yao as a lord—splendid how he had pattern and culture.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: Confucius praises Yao's radiant civilization.'"

sentences.zh: 」故言唐文。

translations.literal: " Therefore it speaks of Tang's pattern.'"
translations.idiomatic: Hence the text invokes the cultural model of Tang.

sentences.zh: 注[二]壞，土器之未燒者。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Pi means earthenware not yet fired.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Pi* is unfired clay on the wheel.

sentences.zh: 郭璞注爾雅曰：「壞胎，物之始也。

translations.literal: Guo Pu's commentary on the *Erya* says: 'Pi tai is the beginning of things.'
translations.idiomatic: Guo Pu glosses *pi tai* as the raw start of matter.

sentences.zh: 」壞音普才反。

translations.literal: " Pi is read with initial p and final tsai in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for *pi*.

sentences.zh: 注[三]凝，成也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Ning means to complete.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Ning* means 'to set' or 'complete.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]械謂器械甲兵之屬也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Xie means implements—arms and armor and the like.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Xie* covers weapons and tools of war.

sentences.zh: 厝謂置之不用也。

translations.literal: Cuo means to set them aside and not use them.
translations.idiomatic: *Cuo* means to lay aside, unused.

sentences.zh: 周禮：「太宰之職，掌建邦之六典，以佐王理邦國；

translations.literal: The *Rites of Zhou* says: 'The grand steward's office grasps the six canons of the state to assist the king in ordering the kingdoms:'
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou li* lists the grand steward's six statutes for governing the realm:

sentences.zh: 一曰理典，二曰教典，三曰禮典，四曰政典，五曰刑典，六曰事典。

translations.literal: 'First, the ordering canon; second, the teaching canon; third, the ritual canon; fourth, the administrative canon; fifth, the penal canon; sixth, the works canon.'"
translations.idiomatic: …ordering, teaching, ritual, government, punishments, and public works.'"

sentences.zh: 」左傳曰：「周有亂政而作九刑。

translations.literal: " The *Zuo Tradition* says: 'Zhou had disorder in government and therefore made the nine punishments.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Zuo zhuan* says Zhou's decay produced the nine-penalty code.

sentences.zh: 」杜預注云：「周之衰，為刑書，謂之九刑。」

translations.literal: " Du Yu's commentary says: 'In Zhou's decline they made a penal text called the nine punishments.'"
translations.idiomatic: Du Yu explains it as a written criminal code of nine sections.'"

sentences.zh: 注[五]力牧，黃帝臣也。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Li Mu was a minister of the Yellow Emperor.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Li Mu served the Yellow Emperor.

sentences.zh: 史記，尚父呂望相武王以伐紂。

translations.literal: The *Records* and *Documents* show the Grand Duke Lü Wang assisting King Wu to attack Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The histories show the Grand Duke aiding King Wu's campaign against Shang.

sentences.zh: 厲謂威容嚴厲。

translations.literal: Li means awesome bearing and stern dignity.
translations.idiomatic: *Li* here means stern martial presence.

sentences.zh: 注[六]伊尹、戲繇、范睢、蔡澤。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: Yi Yin, Gao Yao, Fan Ju, and Cai Ze.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yi Yin, minister Gao Yao, Fan Ju, and Cai Ze—the note glosses the persuaders named in the verse.

sentences.zh: 注[七]廣廈既成，不求材，故林木條暢也。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: When the great hall is finished, timber is not sought, so forest trees grow straight.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Once the hall stands, no more beams are felled, so the woods grow tall and free.

sentences.zh: 遠求謂方珍異之物也。

translations.literal: Yuan qiu means rare treasures from distant regions.
translations.idiomatic: *Yuan qiu* are exotic tribute goods from afar.

sentences.zh: 存猶止息也。

translations.literal: Cun means to stop and rest.
translations.idiomatic: *Cun* means the quest has ceased.

sentences.zh: 言所求之物既止，不資良馬之力也。

translations.literal: It means the things sought have already stopped, so one does not rely on good horses' strength.
translations.idiomatic: When the hunt for curios ends, fine steeds are no longer needed.

sentences.zh: 注[八]立冬之後，盛德在水，陰氣用事，故曰陰事。

translations.literal: Comment on note eight: After the Beginning of Winter, flourishing virtue lies in water; yin qi is in charge—therefore it is called yin business.
translations.idiomatic: Note: After winter's start, cosmic virtue rests in water and yin holds sway—hence 'yin affairs.'

sentences.zh: 水宿謂遠北方七宿，斗、牛、女、虛、危、室、壁也。

translations.literal: Shui xiu means the seven northern lodges—Dipper, Ox, Girl, Emptiness, Rooftop, Chamber, Wall.
translations.idiomatic: The 'water lodges' are the northern seven: Dou, Niu, Nu, Xu, Wei, Shi, Bi.

sentences.zh: 月令曰，孟冬之月昏危中，仲冬昏東壁中，季冬昏婁中，孟春昏參中，水星伏臧不見也。

translations.literal: The *Monthly Ordinances* says: in the first month of winter at dusk Rooftop is centered; mid-winter at dusk eastern Wall is centered; last winter at dusk Bond is centered; first spring at dusk Triaster is centered—the water star hides and is not seen.
translations.idiomatic: The *Yue ling* tracks dusk midheavens through the seasons until the water star sets and vanishes.

sentences.zh: 注[九]爾雅曰：「心為大火。

translations.literal: Comment on note nine: The *Erya* says: 'The heart is the Great Fire.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Erya* identifies the Heart asterism as Great Fire.

sentences.zh: 」詩豳風曰：「七月流火。

translations.literal: " The *Odes* of Bin say: 'In the seventh month Great Fire flows down.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Bin feng* line on the Heart setting in the seventh month.

sentences.zh: 」又曰「九月築場圃」也。

translations.literal: " It also says 'In the ninth month we bank the threshing floor.'"
translations.idiomatic: The same air adds banking the threshing floor in the ninth month.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一０]山南曰陽，山北曰陰。

translations.literal: Comment on note ten: South of a mountain is called yang; north is called yin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Sunny south slope is *yang*, shaded north *yin*.

sentences.zh: 谷梁傳曰：「林屬於山曰麓。」

translations.literal: The *Guliang Commentary* says: 'When forest belongs to a mountain, it is called a foot.'"
translations.idiomatic: *Guliang zhuan* defines the wooded foot of a peak as *lu*.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一一]八尺曰尋。

translations.literal: Comment on note eleven: Eight feet make a xun.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Eight feet equal one *xun*.

sentences.zh: 蓺，殖也。

translations.literal: Yi means to plant.
translations.idiomatic: *Yi* means to cultivate or plant.

sentences.zh: 兩手曰拱。

translations.literal: Two hands' grasp is called gong.
translations.idiomatic: A *gong* is the span of two cupped hands.

sentences.zh: 數猶穊也。

translations.literal: Shu means dense.
translations.idiomatic: *Shu* here means thick growth.

sentences.zh: 數音疏角反。

translations.literal: Shu is read with initial s and final kok in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for the reading of *shu*.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]悠悠，觿多也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twelve: Youyou means very many.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Youyou* means teeming multitudes.

sentences.zh: 罔極猶無窮也。

translations.literal: Wang ji means without limit.
translations.idiomatic: *Wang ji* is 'boundless.'

sentences.zh: 亦各有得，言皆自以為得也。

translations.literal: Also 'each has his gain' means all think they have gained.
translations.idiomatic: Each party imagines itself the winner.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]彼，彼觿人也。

translations.literal: Comment on note thirteen: 'They' means those many people.
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'They' are the crowd just described.

sentences.zh: 論語曰：「用之則行，捨之則臧。」

translations.literal: The *Analects* says: 'When employed, they go forth; set aside, they hide.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Analects* praises those who serve when called and withdraw when not.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一四]呂氏春秋曰：「得伍員者位執珪。

translations.literal: Comment on note fourteen: The *Lüshi chunqiu* says: 'Whoever obtains Wu Yuan receives the rank of jade tablet.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Lüshi chunqiu* promises a jade-tablet noble rank to whoever secures Wu Zixu.

sentences.zh: 」前書音義曰：「古爵名也。

translations.literal: " The Former Documents sounds and meanings says: 'It is an ancient title of nobility.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shu* yinyi says it was an old ducal title.

sentences.zh: 」又曰：「柱國，楚官，猶秦之相國也。」

translations.literal: " It also says: 'Pillar of state was a Chu office, like Qin's chancellor.'"
translations.idiomatic: It adds that *zhuguo* in Chu matched Qin's chief minister.'"

sentences.zh: 「夫君子非不欲仕也。

translations.literal: 'The gentleman does not lack the wish to serve in office.'
translations.idiomatic: The noble man is not unwilling to hold office.

sentences.zh: 恥夸毗以求舉；

translations.literal: He is ashamed to toady and cringe in order to win recommendation;
translations.idiomatic: He shames himself if he fawns and crawls for a nomination;

sentences.zh: [一]非不欲室也，惡登牆而摟處。

translations.literal: [note one] it is not that he lacks the wish for a chamber, but he hates to climb a neighbor's wall and seize a maiden there.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Not that he scorns marriage, but that he will not scale a wall to snatch another's daughter.

sentences.zh: [二] 叫呼衒鬻，縣旌自表，非隨和之寶也。

translations.literal: [Note two] Shouting and hawking, hanging a banner to praise oneself—that is no treasure of Sui and He.
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] Bawling one's wares and flaunting a sign is no jewel of Sui or He.

sentences.zh: 暴智耀世，因以干祿，非仲尼之道也。

translations.literal: To flaunt wit and dazzle the age, thereby to seek salary, is not the way of Zhongni.
translations.idiomatic: Parading cleverness for pay is not the path of Confucius.

sentences.zh: [三] 游不倫黨，苟以徇己，[四]汗血競時，利合而友。

translations.literal: [Note three] To wander without matching one's class, merely to pursue private ends, [Note four] to sweat blood racing the hour, to befriend where profit joins.
translations.idiomatic: [Note three] To drift outside one's proper circle for selfish ends, [Note four] to strain like a blood-sweating steed for fashion, and to call someone friend only when gain aligns.

sentences.zh: [五]子笑我之沉滯，吾亦病子□□而不已也。

translations.literal: [Note five] You laugh at my long stagnation; I also fault you for [incessant striving] without end.
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] You mock my standing still; I find your restless scurrying just as hard to bear.

sentences.zh: [六]先人有則而我弗虧，行有枉徑而我弗隨。

translations.literal: [Note six] My forebears left norms I will not impair; though there be crooked bypaths, I will not follow them.
translations.idiomatic: [Note six] Ancestors set the rule I dare not break; crooked shortcuts I refuse to take.

sentences.zh: [七]臧否在予，唯世所議。

translations.literal: [Note seven] Praise and blame rest with others—only let the age judge.
translations.idiomatic: [Note seven] Let the world speak good or ill as it will.

sentences.zh: 固將因天質之自然，誦上哲之高訓；

translations.literal: I shall rely on Heaven's inborn substance in me and chant the lofty lessons of the highest sages;
translations.idiomatic: I will trust the nature Heaven gave me and recite the teaching of the greatest wise men;

sentences.zh: 詠太平之清風，行天下之至順。

translations.literal: I shall sing the clear wind of great peace and walk the utmost compliance under Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: I will breathe the calm air of an ordered age and walk the straightest path on earth.

sentences.zh: 懼吾躬之穢德，勤百畝之不耘。

translations.literal: I fear foul virtue in my person and toil that the hundred mu go unweeded.
translations.idiomatic: I dread a tainted character within and the hundred acres of the heart left unhoed.

sentences.zh: [八]縶余馬以安行，俟性命之所存。

translations.literal: [Note eight] I hobble my horse to go at ease and await what life and destiny hold in store.
translations.idiomatic: [Note eight] I check my horse to an easy pace and wait what fate and nature allow.

sentences.zh: [九]昔孔子起威於夾谷，[一０]晏嬰發勇於崔杼；

translations.literal: [Note nine] Formerly Confucius raised awe at Jiagu, [Note ten] Yan Ying showed courage before Cui Zhu;
translations.idiomatic: [Note nine] Confucius awed the assembly at Jiagu, [Note ten] Yan Ying faced down Cui Zhu with nerve;

sentences.zh: [一一]曹劌舉節於柯盟，[一二]卞嚴克捷於強禦；

translations.literal: [note eleven] Cao Gui lifted his token at the Ke covenant, [note twelve] Bian Zhuang won victory over the strong oppressor;
translations.idiomatic: [note eleven] Cao Gui brandished his tally at Ke, [note twelve] Bian Zhuang broke a brutal foe;

sentences.zh: [一三]范蠡錯埶於會稽，[一四]五員樹功於柏舉；

translations.literal: [note thirteen] Fan Li shifted strategy at Kuaiji, [note fourteen] Wu Yuan set up merit at Baiju;
translations.idiomatic: [note thirteen] Fan Li read the tide at Kuaiji, [note fourteen] Wu Zixu earned his fame at Baiju;

sentences.zh: [一五]魯連辯言以退燕，[一六]包胥單辭而存楚；

translations.literal: [note fifteen] Lu Zhonglian argued Yan back, [note sixteen] Bao Xu with a single plea preserved Chu;
translations.idiomatic: [note fifteen] Lu Zhonglian talked Yan into retreat, [note sixteen] Shen Baoxu saved Chu with one cry;

sentences.zh: [一七]唐且華顛以悟秦，[一八]甘羅童牙而報趙；

translations.literal: [note seventeen] Tang Ju with hoary temples awed Qin, [note eighteen] Gan Luo in milk teeth answered Zhao;
translations.idiomatic: [note seventeen] Tang Ju in old age awed the king of Qin, [note eighteen] Gan Luo, still a child, dealt with Zhao;

sentences.zh: [一九]原衰見廉於壺飧，[二０]宣孟收德於束脯；

translations.literal: [note nineteen] Yuan Xian showed integrity over a basket of food, [note twenty] Zhao Dun gathered virtue from a bundle of dried meat;
translations.idiomatic: [note nineteen] Yuan Xian proved honest over a simple meal, [note twenty] Zhao Dun earned gratitude for a gift of meat;

sentences.zh: [二一]吳札結信於丘木，[二二]展季□貞於門女；

translations.literal: [note twenty-one] Wu Ji swore faith by the tree on the mound, [note twenty-two] Zhan Ji [proved] constancy toward the girl at the gate;
translations.idiomatic: [note twenty-one] Prince Ji of Wu pledged faith beneath a tomb tree, [note twenty-two] Zhan Ji (Liuxia Hui) kept faith with a woman at his door;

sentences.zh: [二三]顏回明仁於度轂，程嬰顯義於趙武。

translations.literal: [note twenty-three] Yan Hui clarified humanity by a worn axle cap, Cheng Ying displayed righteousness for Zhao Wu.
translations.idiomatic: [note twenty-three] Yan Hui showed benevolence in a worn wheel hub, Cheng Ying revealed duty in saving Zhao Wu.

sentences.zh: [二四]僕誠不能編德於數者，竊慕古人之所序。」

translations.literal: [Note twenty-four] I truly cannot braid virtue with those several; I secretly admire the ordering the ancients gave them.'"
translations.idiomatic: [Note twenty-four] I cannot rank with such paragons, yet I quietly honor the sequence the ancients made of them.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一]夸毗謂佞人足恭，善為進退。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Kua pi means a flatterer's excessive deference, good at advancing and retreating.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Kua pi* is the mincing obsequiousness of the sycophant.

sentences.zh: 注[二]孟子曰：「踰東家牆摟其處子則得妻，不摟則不得，將摟之乎？

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Mencius says: 'If you climb the east neighbor's wall and drag off his maiden, you get a wife; if you do not drag her off, you do not get one—will you drag her off?'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Mencius' ugly parable of stealing a bride over the wall.

sentences.zh: 」趙岐注云：「摟，牽也。

translations.literal: " Zhao Qi's commentary says: 'Lou means to pull.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Qiu glosses *lou* as 'to seize or pull.'

sentences.zh: 」其字從「手」。

translations.literal: " The character follows the 'hand' radical.
translations.idiomatic: The graph uses the hand radical.

sentences.zh: 「處子，處女也。」

translations.literal: "'Chu zi means a maiden.'"
translations.idiomatic: 'Chu zi' is an unmarried girl.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]華嶠書*

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Hua Qiao's book *
translations.idiomatic: Note: Hua Qiao's text *

translations.literal: (says)
translations.idiomatic: (says)

sentences.zh: *「因」字作「回」。

translations.literal: *writes the character for "bend" instead of "accord."'
translations.idiomatic: *substitutes the graph for 'devious' for 'follow.'"

sentences.zh: 回，邪也。

translations.literal: Hui means deviant.
translations.idiomatic: *Hui* means crooked or deviant.

sentences.zh: 注[四]倫謂等倫，黨謂朋黨。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: Lun means rank; dang means faction.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Lun* is proper class; *dang* is clique.

sentences.zh: 徇，營也。

translations.literal: Xun means to scheme.
translations.idiomatic: *Xun* means to angle for gain.

sentences.zh: 言交非其類，苟以營己而已。

translations.literal: It means associating with those not of one's kind, merely to scheme for oneself.
translations.idiomatic: It describes befriending the unworthy only to advance oneself.

sentences.zh: 注[五]汗血謂勞力也。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Han xue means laboring to the utmost.
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'Blood-sweating' images extreme exertion.

sentences.zh: 競時謂趨時也。

translations.literal: Jing shi means rushing with the times.
translations.idiomatic: *Jing shi* is racing the fashion of the hour.

sentences.zh: 利合而友，不以道義。

translations.literal: Befriending where profit joins—not by moral right.
translations.idiomatic: Friendship founded on profit, not on principle.

sentences.zh: 注[六]□□猶區區也。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: [Graphs missing] means fussy, petty.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the damaged phrase means petty fussing.

sentences.zh: 注[七]枉，曲也。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: Wang means crooked.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Wang* means 'bent.'

sentences.zh: 徑，道也。

translations.literal: Jing means path.
translations.idiomatic: *Jing* is a road or course.

sentences.zh: 注[八]尚書曰：「穢德彰聞。

translations.literal: Comment on note eight: The *Documents* says: 'Foul virtue was openly heard.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shang shu* speaks of shameful conduct broadcast abroad.

sentences.zh: 」禮記曰：「夫人情者，聖王之田也。

translations.literal: " The *Record of Rites* says: 'Human dispositions are the field of the sage-kings.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Li ji* compares human nature to a field the sages till.

sentences.zh: 修禮以耕之，陳義以種之，講學以耨之。

translations.literal: 'They cultivate ritual to plough it, set forth righteousness to plant it, lecture learning to weed it.'"
translations.idiomatic: They plough with ritual, plant with righteousness, weed with teaching.'"

sentences.zh: 」古者夫田百畝。

translations.literal: " In antiquity a husband's field was one hundred mu.
translations.idiomatic: Anciently each husbandman had a hundred *mu*.

sentences.zh: 耘，除草也。

translations.literal: Yun means to weed.
translations.idiomatic: *Yun* means to hoe out weeds.

sentences.zh: 注[九]安行，不奔馳也。

translations.literal: Comment on note nine: An xing means not galloping.
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'Easy pace' means no reckless rush.

sentences.zh: 天命之謂性。

translations.literal: What Heaven mandates is called nature.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhong yong* line: what Heaven commands is our nature.

sentences.zh: 言隱居以體命。

translations.literal: It means dwelling in reclusion to embody one's mandate.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss: retirement is how one embodies that mandate.

sentences.zh: 注[一０]解見陳禪傳。

translations.literal: Comment on note ten: Explanation seen in the biography of Chen Chan.
translations.idiomatic: Note: See the commentary in Chen Chan's biography.

sentences.zh: 注[一一]解見馮衍傳。

translations.literal: Comment on note eleven: Explanation seen in the biography of Feng Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Note: See Feng Yan's biography for the gloss.

sentences.zh: 注[一二]曹劌，曹沬也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twelve: Cao Gui is Cao Mo.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cao Gui is the same as Cao Mo.

sentences.zh: 史記曰，曹沬以勇事魯莊公，為魯將，與齊戰，三敗，莊公懼，乃獻遂邑地以和，猶以為將。

translations.literal: The *Records* says: Cao Mo by courage served Duke Zhuang of Lu as Lu's general; fighting Qi he was thrice defeated; Duke Zhuang feared and then ceded the territory of Sui to make peace, yet still kept him as general.
translations.idiomatic: The *Shiji* says Cao Mo served Duke Zhuang of Lu as general, lost thrice to Qi, and when Lu ceded Sui for peace Zhuang kept him in command.

sentences.zh: 齊桓公與莊公會於柯而盟。

translations.literal: Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Zhuang met at Ke to covenant.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Zhuang of Lu met at Ke to swear a pact.

sentences.zh: 桓公與莊公既盟於壇上，曹沬執匕首劫齊桓公，左右莫敢動，乃還魯之侵地。

translations.literal: When Duke Huan and Duke Zhuang had covenanted on the altar, Cao Mo grasped a dagger and seized Duke Huan of Qi; none of his attendants dared move, and he restored Lu's invaded lands.
translations.idiomatic: On the altar Cao Mo drew a short blade on Huan; no guard stirred, and Lu's lost soil was returned.

sentences.zh: 注[一三]新序曰「卞莊子養母，戰而三北，交遊非之，國君辱之。

translations.literal: Comment on note thirteen: The *Xin xu* says: 'Bian Zhuangzi cared for his mother; in battle he thrice fled north; his companions reviled him and his lord shamed him.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Xin xu* tells how Bian Zhuangzi fled thrice to support his mother and was scorned for it.

sentences.zh: 及母死三年，齊與魯戰，莊子請從，遂赴敵而□，三獲甲首。

translations.literal: When his mother died, after three years Qi fought Lu; Zhuangzi asked to follow and then rushed the enemy and [fought], thrice taking armored heads.
translations.idiomatic: After her death, when Qi attacked Lu, he begged to join the fight, charged, and took three armored heads.

sentences.zh: 曰：『夫三北，以養母也。

translations.literal: He said: 'My thrice fleeing north was to nurture my mother.'
translations.idiomatic: He said, 'I ran three times to keep my mother fed.'

sentences.zh: 今志節小具，而責塞矣。

translations.literal: 'Now my resolve is somewhat ready, and the debt of blame is closed.'
translations.idiomatic: 'Now my purpose is ready and the old reproach is paid.'

sentences.zh: 吾聞之，節士不以辱生。

translations.literal: 'I have heard that a man of integrity does not live on in disgrace.'
translations.idiomatic: 'I have heard that a steadfast man will not drag out a shameful life.'

sentences.zh: 』遂反敵，殺十人而死。

translations.literal: "' Thereupon he turned on the enemy, killed ten men, and died.'"
translations.idiomatic: He wheeled on the foe, slew ten, and fell.'"

sentences.zh: 君子曰：三北已塞，滅世斷宗，於孝未終」也。

translations.literal: The gentleman said: 'The three retreats are already made good; to destroy one's age and cut off the ancestral line—toward filial piety it is not yet complete.'"
translations.idiomatic: The verdict runs: 'He redeemed the three routs, yet to wipe out his line would leave filial duty unfinished.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一四]錯，置也，音七故反。

translations.literal: Comment on note fourteen: Cuo means to set in place; it is read with initial ts and final ku in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Cuo* means to deploy or arrange; fanqie follows.

sentences.zh: 埶謂謀略也。

translations.literal: Shi means stratagem.
translations.idiomatic: *Shi* here means strategy or design.

sentences.zh: 史記曰，吳王敗越於夫椒，越王乃以余兵五千人保於會稽。

translations.literal: The *Records* says: the king of Wu defeated Yue at Fujiao; the king of Yue then with remnant troops of five thousand men held out at Kuaiji.
translations.idiomatic: The *Shiji* records Wu's victory at Fujiao and Yue's last stand at Kuaiji with five thousand men.

sentences.zh: 吳師追而圍之。

translations.literal: Wu troops pursued and surrounded them.
translations.idiomatic: The army of Wu pressed the siege.

sentences.zh: 越王謂范蠡曰：「柰何？

translations.literal: The king of Yue said to Fan Li: 'What is to be done?'
translations.idiomatic: Goujian asked Fan Li, 'What now?'

sentences.zh: 」范蠡對曰：「卑辭厚禮以遺之。

translations.literal: " Fan Li answered: 'With humble words and rich gifts send to them.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Li replied, 'Humble speech and lavish gifts to the victor.'

sentences.zh: 」句踐乃命大夫種行成於吳。

translations.literal: " Thereupon Goujian ordered Grand Officer Zhong to go seek peace with Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Goujian sent Minister Zhong to sue for peace at Wu's camp.

sentences.zh: 膝行頓首曰：「句踐請為臣，妻為妾。

translations.literal: Crawling on his knees he knocked his head and said: 'Goujian begs to be your servant, his wife your concubine.'
translations.idiomatic: He crept forward on his knees and said Goujian would be Wu's vassal and his wife a handmaid.

sentences.zh: 」吳王乃赦越王。

translations.literal: " Thereupon the king of Wu pardoned the king of Yue.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Wu accepted and spared Yue.

sentences.zh: 越王反國，拊循其士。

translations.literal: The king of Yue returned to his state and comforted his warriors.
translations.idiomatic: Goujian went home and rallied his troops.

sentences.zh: 范蠡曰：「可矣。

translations.literal: Fan Li said: 'It is enough.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Li said, 'The moment has come.'

sentences.zh: 」乃伐吳。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he attacked Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Yue then struck Wu.

sentences.zh: 吳師敗，越復棲吳王姑蘇之山也。

translations.literal: Wu troops were defeated; Yue again drove the king of Wu to perch on Mount Gusu.
translations.idiomatic: Wu broke; Yue drove the king of Wu up Mount Gusu again.

sentences.zh: 注[一五]伍子胥名員，楚人也。

translations.literal: Comment on note fifteen: Wu Zixu's name was Yuan; he was a man of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Wu Zixu, personal name Yuan, came from Chu.

sentences.zh: 子胥父誅於楚，子胥挾弓矢而干吳王闔閭，闔閭甚勇之，為興師伐楚，戰於柏舉，楚師敗績。

translations.literal: His father was executed in Chu; Zixu, bearing bow and arrows, importuned King Helü of Wu; Helü greatly admired his courage, raised troops to attack Chu, fought at Baiju, and Chu's army was routed.
translations.idiomatic: His father died at Chu's hands; Zixu fled to Wu with bow in hand, won Helü's trust, and led Wu to crush Chu at Baiju.

sentences.zh: 事見谷梁傳。

translations.literal: The affair is seen in the *Guliang Commentary*.
translations.idiomatic: See the *Guliang zhuan*.

sentences.zh: 注[一六]史記曰，魯仲連，齊人也。

translations.literal: Comment on note sixteen: The *Records* says: Lu Zhonglian was a man of Qi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shiji* introduces Lu Zhonglian of Qi.

sentences.zh: 燕將攻下齊聊城，固保守之，田單攻之不下。

translations.literal: A Yan general had taken Liaocheng in Qi and held it fast on defense; Tian Dan attacked but could not take it.
translations.idiomatic: A Yan general held Liaocheng; Tian Dan could not break the defense.

sentences.zh: 魯仲連乃為書遺燕將。

translations.literal: Lu Zhonglian then composed a letter and sent it to the Yan general.
translations.idiomatic: Lu Zhonglian wrote a letter to the Yan commander.

sentences.zh: 燕將見書，泣三日，乃自殺。

translations.literal: When the Yan general saw the letter, he wept three days, then killed himself.
translations.idiomatic: The general read it, wept three days, and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 遂平聊城。

translations.literal: Thereupon Liaocheng was pacified.
translations.idiomatic: Liaocheng then fell without a fight.

sentences.zh: 注[一七]左傳曰，楚昭王為吳所敗，奔隨，申包胥如秦乞師，曰：「吳為封豕長憨，以薦食上國，寡君越在草莽，使下臣告急。

translations.literal: Comment on note seventeen: The *Zuo Tradition* says: King Zhao of Chu was defeated by Wu and fled to Sui; Shen Baoxu went to Qin to beg troops, saying: 'Wu is a great boar and long snake, devouring the upper states; our lord is stranded in the brush and sends me to cry alarm.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zuo zhuan* tells how Shen Baoxu begged Qin for rescue when Wu shattered Chu.

sentences.zh: 」立依於庭牆而哭，日夜不絕聲，勺飲不入口，七日，秦師乃出，軍敗吳而復楚國。

translations.literal: " He stood leaning on the courtyard wall and wailed; day and night his voice did not cease; he took not a spoonful of drink; after seven days Qin troops marched forth, defeated Wu in battle, and restored the state of Chu.
translations.idiomatic: He leaned on the palace wall and wailed seven days without food until Qin marched and drove Wu from Chu.

sentences.zh: 注[一八]唐且即唐睢也。

translations.literal: Comment on note eighteen: Tang Ju is Tang Sui.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Tang Ju is the same man as Tang Sui in other texts.

sentences.zh: 戰國策曰：「齊、楚伐魏，魏使人請救*[於秦]*，不至。

translations.literal: The *Strategies of the Warring States* says: 'Qi and Chu attacked Wei; Wei sent a man to beg rescue from Qin, but help did not come.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhanguo ce* tells how Qi and Chu besieged Wei while Qin held back.

sentences.zh: 魏人有唐睢者，年九十餘矣，西見秦王。

translations.literal: Among the Wei people was one Tang Sui, more than ninety years old, who went west to see the king of Qin.
translations.idiomatic: A Wei man named Tang Sui, past ninety, traveled west to the king of Qin.

sentences.zh: 秦王曰：『丈人忙然乃遠至*

translations.literal: The king of Qin said: 'Venerable sir, you have come so far in your haste *
translations.idiomatic: The king of Qin said, 'Old sir, you have hurried all this way *

translations.literal: (to Wei)
translations.idiomatic: (for Wei)

sentences.zh: *此，*[魏]*來者數矣，寡人知魏之急矣。

translations.literal: *to this place;* envoys from Wei have come many times—I know Wei's distress.'
translations.idiomatic: *—Wei* has sent envoys again and again; I know the danger Wei faces.'

sentences.zh: 』唐且曰：『夫魏，萬乘之國也。

translations.literal: "' Tang Sui said: 'Wei is a state of ten thousand chariots.'
translations.idiomatic: Tang Sui answered, 'Wei fields ten thousand chariots.'

sentences.zh: 稱東藩者，以秦之強也。

translations.literal: It calls itself eastern vassal because of Qin's strength.
translations.idiomatic: It bows east only because Qin is strong.

sentences.zh: 今齊、楚之兵已在魏郊矣，大王之救不至，魏急，且割地而約從。

translations.literal: Now Qi and Chu are already at Wei's borders; if the great king's rescue does not arrive, Wei in extremity will cede land and join a vertical alliance.
translations.idiomatic: Yet Qi and Chu stand on Wei's border; if your army tarries, Wei will cede territory and league with them against you.

sentences.zh: 是王亡一萬乘之魏，而強二敵之齊、楚。

translations.literal: Thus the king would lose a ten-thousand-chariot Wei and strengthen two foes in Qi and Chu.'
translations.idiomatic: You would lose Wei and strengthen both rivals at once.'

sentences.zh: 』秦王悟，遽發兵救魏。

translations.literal: "' The king of Qin understood and quickly sent troops to save Wei.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Qin saw the point and marched at once.

sentences.zh: 」爾雅曰：「顛，頂也。

translations.literal: " The *Erya* says: 'Dian means crown of the head.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Erya* glosses *dian* as the crown of the head.

sentences.zh: 」華顛謂白首也。

translations.literal: " Hua dian means white-haired head.
translations.idiomatic: So 'flowered crown' means hoary hair.

sentences.zh: 注[一九]甘羅，下蔡人，甘茂孫也。

translations.literal: Comment on note nineteen: Gan Luo was a man of Xiacai, grandson of Gan Mao.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gan Luo of Xiacai was Gan Mao's grandson.

sentences.zh: 年十二，事秦相呂不韋。

translations.literal: At age twelve he served Qin chancellor Lü Buwei.
translations.idiomatic: At twelve he entered Lü Buwei's service.

sentences.zh: 秦使張唐往相燕。

translations.literal: Qin sent Zhang Tang to go be minister to Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Qin was sending Zhang Tang to Yan as chief minister.

sentences.zh: 羅曰：「借臣車五乘，請為張唐先報趙。

translations.literal: Luo said: 'Lend me five teams of chariots; let me report to Zhao ahead of Zhang Tang.'
translations.idiomatic: Gan Luo asked for five chariots to clear the road with Zhao first.

sentences.zh: 」不韋乃言之於始皇，召見，使甘羅於趙，趙襄王郊迎。

translations.literal: " Buwei then spoke of it to the First Emperor, who summoned and saw him, sent Gan Luo to Zhao, and King Xiang of Zhao met him beyond the walls.
translations.idiomatic: Lü Buwei told the First Emperor, who received Gan Luo and sent him to Zhao; King Xiangwen met him outside the capital.

sentences.zh: 事見史記。

translations.literal: The affair is seen in the *Records*.
translations.idiomatic: The *Shiji* gives the full account.

sentences.zh: 童牙謂幼小也。

translations.literal: Tong ya means young and small.
translations.idiomatic: Tong ya means still in childhood.

sentences.zh: 注[二０]昔趙衰為原大夫，故曰原衰。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty: Formerly Zhao Cui was grand officer of Yuan—therefore he is called Yuan Cui.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zhao Cui held the Yuan fief, hence 'Yuan Cui.'

sentences.zh: 左傳曰，晉侯問原守於寺人勃鞮，對曰：「昔趙衰以壺飧從徑，餒而不食，故使處原。

translations.literal: The *Zuo Tradition* says: the marquis of Jin asked the guardian at the temple about the warden of Yuan, who answered: 'Formerly Zhao Cui carried a basket of food along a path, hungry yet would not eat—therefore he was made warden of Yuan.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Zuo zhuan* tells how Jin chose Zhao Cui for Yuan after he refused food on the road.

sentences.zh: 」見音胡殿反。

translations.literal: Xian is read with initial h and final tien in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for the reading of *xian* (to show).

sentences.zh: 注[二一]呂覽曰，昔趙宣孟將之絳，見桑下有餓人，宣孟止車下食而餔之，再咽而能視。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-one: The *Lü's Spring and Autumn Annals* says: Formerly Zhao Dun was going to Jiang and saw a starving man beneath a mulberry; Dun stopped his chariot, gave him food from below the seat, and fed him; twice he swallowed before he could see.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Lüshi chunqiu* tells how Zhao Dun fed a starving man by the mulberry road.

sentences.zh: 宣孟問之曰：「汝何為而餓若是？

translations.literal: Dun asked him, saying: 'Why are you starving like this?'
translations.idiomatic: Dun asked why he was famished.

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「臣官於絳，歸而糧絕，羞行乞，故至行此。

translations.literal: "' He answered: 'I was an official at Jiang; returning, my provisions ran out; I was ashamed to beg—therefore I came to this pass.'
translations.idiomatic: The man said he had served at Jiang, ran out of food on the way home, and was too proud to beg.

sentences.zh: 」宣子與脯三朐，拜受而弗敢食。

translations.literal: " The Viscount gave him three pieces of dried meat in bundles; he bowed and received but dared not eat.
translations.idiomatic: Dun gave him three bundles of dried meat; he bowed but would not eat.

sentences.zh: 問其故。

translations.literal: Dun asked the reason.
translations.idiomatic: Dun asked why.

sentences.zh: 曰：「臣有老母，將以遺之。

translations.literal: He said: 'I have an aged mother; I meant to leave it for her.'
translations.idiomatic: He said he was saving it for his old mother.

sentences.zh: 」宣孟曰：「食之，吾更與汝。

translations.literal: " Dun said: 'Eat this; I will give you more.'
translations.idiomatic: Dun told him to eat and promised more.

sentences.zh: 」乃復與脯二束。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he again gave two bundles of dried meat.
translations.idiomatic: He added two more bundles of meat.

sentences.zh: 注[二二]史記曰：「吳公子季札使過徐，徐君好季札□，口不敢言。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-two: The *Records* says: 'Prince Jizha of Wu was on a mission and passed through Xu; the lord of Xu loved Jizha's [sword] but did not dare speak.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shiji* tells how the lord of Xu coveted Jizha's sword but held his tongue.

sentences.zh: 季札知之，為使上國，未獻。

translations.literal: Jizha knew it, but because he was on mission to the upper states had not yet presented it.
translations.idiomatic: Jizha understood but was bound by embassy rites and could not give it yet.

sentences.zh: 洎還至徐，徐君已死，於是乃解其寶□，系之徐君頤樹而去。」

translations.literal: When he returned as far as Xu, the lord of Xu had died; thereupon he loosed his precious [sword], hung it on the tree before the lord of Xu's tomb mound, and departed.'"
translations.idiomatic: On his return Xu was dead; Jizha hung the sword on a tree at the tomb and rode away.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二三]展季，柳下惠也。

translations.literal: Comment on note twenty-three: Zhan Ji is Liuxia Hui.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Zhan Ji is Liuxia Hui.

sentences.zh: 韓詩外傳曰：「魯有男子獨處，夜暴風雨至，婦人趨而托之，男子閉戶不納，曰：『吾聞男子不六十不閒居。

translations.literal: The *Han shi waizhuan* says: 'Lu had a man who lived alone; in the night a violent storm came; a woman rushed to take shelter with him; the man closed his door and would not admit her, saying: "I have heard that a man not sixty does not dwell alone with others."'
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shi waizhuan* tells of a Lu recluse who refused a woman shelter in a storm, citing the rule against mixed lodging.

sentences.zh: 』婦人曰：『子何不學柳下惠然？

translations.literal: "' The woman said: "Why do you not learn from Liuxia Hui?'
translations.idiomatic: She asked why he did not imitate Liuxia Hui.

sentences.zh: 嫗不逮門之女，國人不稱其亂焉。

translations.literal: He sheltered a girl who had not reached the door; the people of the state did not call it impropriety.'
translations.idiomatic: Liuxia Hui warmed a stranded girl without scandal in Lu.'

sentences.zh: 』」注[二四]程嬰解見馮衍傳。

translations.literal: "'" Comment on note twenty-four: Cheng Ying—explanation seen in the biography of Feng Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Cheng Ying is glossed in Feng Yan's biography.'

sentences.zh: 度轂，未詳。

translations.literal: Du gu—not detailed.
translations.idiomatic: The phrase *du gu* is not explained in the commentary.

sentences.zh: 元和中，肅宗始修古禮，巡狩方岳。

translations.literal: In the Yuanhe era, Emperor Zhang first restored ancient rites and toured the sacred peaks.
translations.idiomatic: During Emperor Zhang's Yuanhe reign the court revived classical rites and the imperial tour of the sacred peaks.

sentences.zh: 駰上四巡頌以稱漢德，辭甚典美，文多故不載。

translations.literal: Yin presented odes on the four tours praising Han virtue; the wording was very canonical and beautiful, but the text is long and therefore is not carried here.
translations.idiomatic: Yin submitted four *xun* odes in praise of Han; the pieces were too long to quote in full.

sentences.zh: [一]帝雅好文章，自見駰頌後，*

translations.literal: [Note one] The emperor by nature loved literature; after he himself saw Yin's odes *
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] The emperor loved letters; when he read Yin's hymns *

translations.literal: (the emperor)
translations.idiomatic: (the emperor)

sentences.zh: **[常]*嗟歎之，謂侍中竇憲曰：「卿寧知崔駰乎？

translations.literal: **[often]* sighed in admiration and said to attendant Dou Xian: 'Do you even know Cui Yin?'
translations.idiomatic: **he* often praised them and asked Dou Xian, 'Do you know Cui Yin?'

sentences.zh: 」對曰：「班固數為臣說之，然未見也。

translations.literal: " He answered: 'Ban Gu several times spoke of him to your servant, yet I have not seen him.'
translations.idiomatic: Dou answered that Ban Gu had spoken of him but they had not met.

sentences.zh: 」帝曰：「公愛班固而忽崔駰，此葉公之好龍也。

translations.literal: " The emperor said: 'You love Ban Gu yet neglect Cui Yin—this is Lord Ye's love of dragons.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor said favoring Ban Gu while ignoring Yin was like Ye Gong's love of dragons.

sentences.zh: 試請見之。

translations.literal: Try summoning him to an audience.'
translations.idiomatic: He bade Dou bring Yin in.

sentences.zh: 」[二]駰由此候憲。

translations.literal: " [Note two] Thereupon Yin waited on Xian.
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] Yin therefore called on Dou Xian.

sentences.zh: 憲屣履迎門，[三]笑謂駰曰：「亭伯，吾受詔交公，公何得薄哉？

translations.literal: Xian, wearing slippers, met him at the gate, [note three] smiled and said to Yin: 'Tingbo, I received orders to befriend you—how can you slight me?'
translations.idiomatic: Dou shuffled out in slippers, [note three] smiled, and said Tingbo was ordered to be his friend—why stand aloof?

sentences.zh: 」遂揖入為上客。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he bowed him in as chief guest.
translations.idiomatic: He bowed Yin inside as honored guest.

sentences.zh: 居無幾何，帝幸憲第，時駰適在憲所，帝聞而欲召見之。

translations.literal: Before long the emperor visited Xian's residence; at that time Yin happened to be at Xian's place; the emperor heard of it and wished to summon him to audience.
translations.idiomatic: Soon the emperor visited Dou's house while Yin was there and wanted to see him.

sentences.zh: 憲諫，以為不宜與白衣會。

translations.literal: Xian remonstrated, holding that it was not fitting to meet with a common-robed man.
translations.idiomatic: Dou dissuaded him, saying a sovereign should not receive a commoner informally.

sentences.zh: 帝悟曰：「吾能令駰朝夕在傍，何必於此！

translations.literal: The emperor understood and said: 'I can make Yin attend morning and evening at my side—why must it be here!'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor retorted that he could appoint Yin and keep him at court without a casual meeting.

sentences.zh: 」適欲官之，會帝崩。

translations.literal: " He was about to give him office when the emperor died.
translations.idiomatic: He was on the verge of granting Yin a post when the emperor died.

sentences.zh: 注[一]案：駰集有東、西、南、北四巡頌，流俗本「四」多作「西」者，誤。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Case: Yin's collected works contain odes on the east, west, south, and north tours; vulgar editions often write 'four' as 'west'—an error.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yin's corpus has four directional *xun* odes; vulgar copies wrongly read 'four' as 'west.'

sentences.zh: 注[二]劉向新序曰：「子張見魯哀公，七日，哀公不禮焉而去，曰：『君之好士，有似葉公子高好龍。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Liu Xiang's *Xin xu* says: 'Zizhang had audience of Duke Ai of Lu; seven days passed and Duke Ai did not treat him with courtesy, so he departed, saying: "The lord's love of gentlemen is like Lord Ye Gao's love of dragons.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Liu Xiang's *Xin xu* compares Duke Ai's neglect of Zizhang to Ye Gong's false love of dragons.

sentences.zh: 天龍聞而降之，窺頭於牖，拖尾於堂，葉公見之，失其魂魄，五色無主。

translations.literal: Heaven's dragon heard and descended to him, peeking its head through the window, trailing its tail across the hall; when Lord Ye saw it, he lost his soul; the five colors had no master.
translations.idiomatic: A real dragon answered the call, thrust its head through the window and lashed the hall; Ye fled in terror.

sentences.zh: 是葉公非好龍也，好夫似龍而非龍者。

translations.literal: This shows Lord Ye did not love dragons—he loved what looked like dragons but were not dragons.'
translations.idiomatic: So Ye loved only painted dragons, not living ones.'

sentences.zh: 』」注[三]屣履謂納履曳之而行，言□遽也。

translations.literal: "'" Comment on note three: Xi lu means putting on shoes and dragging them while walking—it speaks of haste.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Xi lu* is slipping into shoes in haste to greet a guest.

sentences.zh: 屣音山爾反。

translations.literal: Xi is read with initial s and final ne in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for *xi*.

sentences.zh: 竇太后臨朝，憲以重戚出內詔命。

translations.literal: When the Empress Dowager Dou held court, Xian, as a weighty in-law, issued edicts from within and without.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dowager Dou governed; Dou Xian, as powerful kin, drafted palace edicts.

sentences.zh: 駰獻書誡之曰：

translations.literal: Yin presented a letter warning him, saying:
translations.idiomatic: Yin sent him a written remonstrance:

sentences.zh: 駰聞交淺而言深者，愚也；

translations.literal: Yin has heard that to speak deeply when acquaintance is shallow is folly;
translations.idiomatic: I have heard that deep talk to a slight acquaintance is folly;

sentences.zh: 在賤而望貴者，惑也；

translations.literal: to hope for favor when base is delusion;
translations.idiomatic: to expect favor from low station is delusion;

sentences.zh: 未信而納忠者，謗也。

translations.literal: to offer loyalty when not yet trusted is slander.
translations.idiomatic: to press loyal counsel before trust is earned looks like slander.

sentences.zh: 三者皆所不宜，而或蹈之者，思□其區區，憤盈而不能已也。

translations.literal: These three are all improper, yet if someone treads them, it is because he thinks his petty earnestness important and, swollen with resentment, cannot stop.
translations.idiomatic: All three are wrong, yet one may fall into them when zeal outruns judgment.

sentences.zh: 竊見足下體淳淑之姿，躬高明之量，意美志厲，有上賢之風。

translations.literal: I have observed that your person embodies pure and goodly bearing and personally carries bright and high capacity; your aims are fine and your will keen—you have the air of a superior worthy.
translations.idiomatic: I see in you pure substance and high capacity, keen will and noble temper—the mark of a true worthy.

sentences.zh: 駰幸得充下館，序後陳，[一]是以竭其拳拳，敢進一言。

translations.literal: Yin has had the luck to fill the lower lodges and take a place in the rear ranks, [note one] therefore I exhaust my earnest loyalty and dare advance one word.
translations.idiomatic: I have been lucky to sit among your clients, [note one] so I speak my whole heart in this one letter.

sentences.zh: 注[一]陳，列也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Chen means to array.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Chen* means to rank or set forth.

sentences.zh: 傳曰：「生而富者驕，生而貴者□。

translations.literal: A tradition says: 'Those born rich are arrogant; those born noble are [proud].'
translations.idiomatic: The adage says the born-rich grow proud and the born-noble grow haughty.

sentences.zh: 」生富貴而能不驕□者，未之有也。

translations.literal: " To be born rich and noble yet not arrogant or [proud]—there has never been such a one.
translations.idiomatic: None have ever combined high birth with freedom from arrogance.

sentences.zh: 今寵祿初隆，百僚觀行，當堯舜之盛世，處光華之顯時，[一]豈可不庶幾夙夜，以永觿譽，弘申伯之美，致周邵之事乎？

translations.literal: Now favor and stipend have just swelled; the hundred officials watch your conduct; you stand in the flourishing age of Yao and Shun, in the brilliant and manifest time—how can you not strive day and night to lengthen your fair name, enlarge the beauty of Shen Bo, and bring about the deeds of the dukes of Zhou and Shao?
translations.idiomatic: Now your honors are new and every eye is on you; in this brilliant age like Yao and Shun's, can you fail to labor night and day for lasting fame, matching Shen Bo and the Zhou dukes?

sentences.zh: [二]語曰：「不患無位，患所以立。

translations.literal: [Note one] A saying says: 'I do not worry about lacking position; I worry about what will establish me in them.'
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] The saying runs: do not fret over office—fret over the merit that earns it.

sentences.zh: 」[三]昔馮野王以外戚居位，稱為賢臣，[四]近陰□尉克己復禮，終受多福。

translations.literal: " [Note two] [Note three] Formerly Feng Yewang, as maternal relative, held office and was called a worthy minister; [note four] recently Commandant Yin [name missing in text] mastered himself and returned to ritual and in the end received great blessing.
translations.idiomatic: " [Note two] [Note three] Feng Yewang once held power as an in-law yet kept a worthy name; [note four] lately Commandant Yin, restraining himself with ritual, won lasting good fortune.

sentences.zh: [五]郯氏之宗，非不尊也；

translations.literal: [Note five] The clan of Tan was not without honor;
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] The house of Tan was mighty enough—

sentences.zh: [六]陽*

translations.literal: [Note six] The Yangping *
translations.idiomatic: [Note six] The Yangping marquess's *

translations.literal: (marquis)
translations.idiomatic: (marquis)

sentences.zh: **[平]*之族，非不盛也。

translations.literal: **[clan]* was not without splendor.
translations.idiomatic: **line* was magnificently powerful nonetheless.

sentences.zh: 重侯累將，建天樞，執斗柄。

translations.literal: Marquis piled on marquis, general on general; they set up Heaven's pivot and grasped the dipper's handle.
translations.idiomatic: They stacked noble titles and commands, seized the axis of power, and held the state's helm.

sentences.zh: [七] 其所以獲譏於時，垂愆於後者，何也？

translations.literal: [Note seven] Why then did they earn the ridicule of their age and leave blame for later generations?
translations.idiomatic: [Note seven] Why then were they mocked in their own day and damned by posterity?

sentences.zh: 蓋在滿而不挹，位有餘而仁不足也。

translations.literal: Surely because they were full yet would not pour off, had surplus rank but lacked humanity.
translations.idiomatic: Because they swelled with pride and would not yield, had more rank than humanity could bear.

sentences.zh: 漢興以後，迄於哀、平，外家二十，保族全身，四人而已。

translations.literal: From Han's rise down through Emperors Ai and Ping there were twenty affinal houses; those that preserved their clan and kept whole persons were only four.
translations.idiomatic: Since Han began until Ai and Ping, twenty maternal clans rose; only four kept their lines and lives intact.

sentences.zh: [八]書曰：「鑒於有殷。」

translations.literal: [Note eight] The *Documents* says: 'Take warning from Yin.'
translations.idiomatic: [Note eight] The *Shang shu* says: 'Look to Shang for a mirror.'

sentences.zh: 可不慎哉！

translations.literal: Can one not be cautious!
translations.idiomatic: Can you afford not to be careful?

sentences.zh: 注[一]尚書大傳曰：「舜時百工相和為卿雲之歌曰：『卿雲爛兮，*

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The *Great Documents Commentary on the Documents* says: 'When Shun's hundred crafts harmonized, they made the Qingyun song, saying: "The Qingyun is bright *
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shangshu dazhuan* records Shun's ministers singing the *Qing yun* ode: 'Bright the auspicious clouds *

translations.literal: (ritual)
translations.idiomatic: (ritual)

sentences.zh: **[愨]*漫漫兮，日月光華，旦復旦兮。』」

translations.literal: **[solemn]* vast and boundless; sun and moon shed radiance; dawn succeeds dawn again.'"'
translations.idiomatic: **solemn* and wide; sun and moon pour their light; each dawn renews the last.'"'

sentences.zh: 注[二]申伯，周宣王之元舅。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Shen Bo was maternal uncle to King Xuan of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Shen Bo was King Xuan of Zhou's chief maternal uncle.

sentences.zh: 周公、邵公皆輔佐周室也。

translations.literal: The dukes of Zhou and Shao both assisted the house of Zhou.
translations.idiomatic: The dukes of Zhou and Shao both steadied the Zhou throne.

sentences.zh: 注[三]論語*

translations.literal: Comment on note three: The *Analects* *
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Analects* *

translations.literal: (says)
translations.idiomatic: (says)

sentences.zh: *孔子之言也。

translations.literal: *it is Confucius's saying.
translations.idiomatic: *gives Confucius's words.

sentences.zh: 言但患立身不處於仁義也。

translations.literal: It means one should only worry whether one's establishment rests in humanity and righteousness.
translations.idiomatic: The sense is: worry only whether you stand on humanity and right.

sentences.zh: 注[四]前書曰，馮野王字君卿，妹為元帝昭儀，野王為左馮翊。

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: Feng Yewang, courtesy name Junqing—his younger sister was Brilliant Companion to Emperor Yuan; Yewang was left governor of eastern Fufeng.
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shu* names Feng Yewang, whose sister was Yuan's favorite; he rose to governor of eastern Fufeng.

sentences.zh: 御史大夫缺，上使尚書選第中二千石，而野王行能第一。

translations.literal: The censor-in-chief's post was vacant; the emperor ordered the secretariat to select among ranked two-thousand-dan officials, and Yewang's conduct and ability ranked first.
translations.idiomatic: When the censor's chair was empty, the throne ranked candidates among two-thousand-dan officials and put Yewang first.

sentences.zh: 注[五]陰□尉，光烈皇后同母弟興也。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Commandant Yin [graph missing] was Xing, full brother of the Brilliant Empress on the mother's side.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Commandant Yin Xing was the empress dowager's uterine brother (name partly damaged in text).

sentences.zh: 以謹□親幸焉。

translations.literal: Because of scrupulous conduct he won favor.
translations.idiomatic: His careful conduct won the sovereign's trust.

sentences.zh: 注[七]王氏九侯五大司馬。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: the Wang clan's nine marquises and five grand marshals.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the Wang line's nine enfeoffments and five grand marshalships.

sentences.zh: 春秋運斗樞曰：「北斗七星，第一名天樞，第二至第四為魁，第五至第七為杓。

translations.literal: The *Spring and Autumn* weft *Pivot of the Dipper* says: 'The Northern Dipper has seven stars; the first is called Heaven's Pivot, the second through fourth form the bowl, the fifth through seventh the handle.'
translations.idiomatic: A weft text maps the Dipper: first star the pivot, stars two to four the bowl, five to seven the handle.

sentences.zh: 杓即柄。

translations.literal: The ladle is the handle.
translations.idiomatic: The 'ladle' is the dipper handle.

sentences.zh: 前書「斗運中央，制臨四海」。

translations.literal: The Former Documents says: "The dipper turns at the center, controlling the four seas."'
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shu* says the Dipper at the hub commands the realm.'

sentences.zh: 注[八]外家，當為後家也。

translations.literal: Comment on note eight: 'Affinal house' should read 'empress's house.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: 'waijia' here means the empress's kin.

sentences.zh: 二十者，謂高帝呂後產、祿謀反誅，惠帝張皇后廢，文帝母薄太后弟昭被殺，孝文帝竇皇后從昆弟子嬰誅，景帝薄皇后、武帝陳皇后並廢，□皇后自殺，昭帝上官皇后家族誅，宣帝祖母史良娣為巫蠱死，宣帝母王夫人弟子商下獄死，霍皇后家破，元帝王皇后弟*

translations.literal: 'Twenty' lists the affinal disasters: under Gaozu, Empress Lü's kin Chan and Lu plotted rebellion and were executed; Emperor Hui's Empress Zhang was deposed; Emperor Wen's mother Empress Dowager Bo's younger brother Zhao was killed; Emperor Wen's Empress Dou's cousin Ying was executed; Empress Bo of Emperor Jing and Empress Chen of Emperor Wu were both deposed; Empress [Wei] committed suicide; under Emperor Zhao, Empress Shangguan's clan was executed; Emperor Xuan's grandmother Lady Shi the Virtuous Consort died in the witchcraft affair; Emperor Xuan's mother Lady Wang's nephew Shang died in prison; Empress Huo's house was ruined; Empress Yuan Wang's younger brother *
translations.idiomatic: The score counts twenty affinal catastrophes from Lü's coup through the Shangguan slaughter, witchcraft deaths, Huo's fall, down to Wang *

translations.literal: (Wang)
translations.idiomatic: (Wang)

sentences.zh: **[子]*莽篡位，成帝許皇后賜死，趙皇后廢自殺，哀帝祖母傅太后家屬徙合浦，平帝母□姬家屬誅，昭帝趙太后憂死是也。

translations.literal: **[Zi]* Mang's usurpation; Emperor Cheng's Empress Xu ordered to die; Empress Zhao deposed and suicide; Emperor Ai's grandmother Empress Dowager Fu's kin banished to Hepu; Emperor Ping's mother's [Wei] Ji kin executed; Empress Dowager Zhao of Emperor Zhao died of grief—these are the cases.'
translations.idiomatic: **Zi* Mang's coup, Xu and Zhao of Cheng, Fu exiled, Ping's Wei in-laws killed, and the Zhao empress dowager's death of grief among them.'

sentences.zh: 四人者，哀帝母丁姬，景帝王皇后，宣帝許皇后、王皇后，其家族並全。

translations.literal: The four are: Emperor Ai's mother Lady Ding, Empress Wang of Emperor Jing, and Empresses Xu and Wang of Emperor Xuan—their clans all remained whole.
translations.idiomatic: Only four lines survived intact: Ding, Jing's Wang, and Xuan's two Xu and Wang consorts.

sentences.zh: 竇氏之興，肇自孝文。

translations.literal: The Dou clan's rise began from Emperor Wen.
translations.idiomatic: Dou power began under Emperor Wen.

sentences.zh: [一]二君以淳淑守道，成名先日；

translations.literal: [Note one] The two lords, by purity and goodness kept the Way and made their names before their day;
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Two early Dou kinsmen won fame through plain virtue before their time;

sentences.zh: [二]安豐以佐命著德，顯自中興。

translations.literal: [Note two] Anfeng earned merit as helper of Heaven's mandate and shone from the Restoration onward.
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] Dou Rong as marquis of Anfeng proved his de in aiding the restoration.

sentences.zh: [三]內以忠誠自固，外以法度自守，卒享祚國，垂祉於今。

translations.literal: [Note three] Inwardly they secured themselves with loyalty; outwardly they guarded with law—thereby they enjoyed the state's blessing and extend good fortune to this day.
translations.idiomatic: [Note three] Loyal within, lawful without, they kept the fief and left blessing to this day.

sentences.zh: 夫謙德之光，周易所美；

translations.literal: The light of humble virtue is what the *Changes* praises;
translations.idiomatic: The *Zhou yi* praises the radiance of modest virtue;

sentences.zh: 滿溢之位，道家所戒。

translations.literal: the position of overflowing fullness is what the Daoists warn against.
translations.idiomatic: Daoist teaching warns against brimming rank.

sentences.zh: [四]故君子福大而愈懼，爵隆而益恭。

translations.literal: [Note four] Therefore the gentleman grows more fearful as his blessing grows greater, more reverent as his title rises higher.
translations.idiomatic: [Note four] So the noble man fears more as fortune mounts and bows lower as his title climbs.

sentences.zh: 遠察近覽，俯仰有則，銘諸幾杖，刻諸盤杅。

translations.literal: Looking far and scanning near, in bowing and rising he has a rule; he inscribes it on armrest and staff, carves it on bowl and basin.
translations.idiomatic: He takes warning from past and present, inscribes maxims on staff and armrest, carves them on bowls.

sentences.zh: [五]矜矜業業，無殆無荒。

translations.literal: [Note five] Trembling, trembling, diligent, diligent—without peril, without neglect.
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] Careful, careful, busy, busy—never rash, never idle.

sentences.zh: 如此，則百福是荷，慶流無窮矣。

translations.literal: If so, then the hundred blessings will be borne and joyous influence will flow without end.
translations.idiomatic: Then every blessing will rest on you and good fortune will never run dry.

sentences.zh: 注[一]前書曰，竇嬰字王孫，孝文皇后從兄子也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The Former Documents says: Dou Ying, courtesy name Wangsun, was a son of Empress Wen's elder cousin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Dou Ying, styled Wangsun, was a nephew of Empress Dou of Wen.

sentences.zh: 孝文時為吳相，孝景時為詹事也。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Wen he was minister of Wu; under Emperor Jing, chamberlain for the palace.
translations.idiomatic: He governed Wu under Wen and served as chamberlain under Jing.

sentences.zh: 注[二]竇太后之弟長君、少君，退讓君子，不敢以富貴驕人，故雲淳淑守道也。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Empress Dowager Dou's younger brothers the elder and younger lords yielded like gentlemen and did not dare pride themselves on wealth and rank—therefore it says they kept purity and the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Her brothers Changjun and Shaojun were modest gentlemen despite rank—hence 'pure and law-abiding.'

sentences.zh: 注[三]竇融封為安豐侯。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Dou Rong was enfeoffed marquis of Anfeng.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Dou Rong held the Anfeng marquisate.

sentences.zh: 注[四]易曰：「謙尊而光，卑而不可踰。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: The *Changes* says: 'Humility gives honor yet radiance; low yet impossible to overstep.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zhou yi* praises humility's shining lowliness.

sentences.zh: 」老子曰：「富貴而驕，自遺其咎。

translations.literal: " Laozi says: 'Wealth and honor with pride bring self-brought blame.'
translations.idiomatic: Laozi warns that wealth and pride fetch their own punishment.

sentences.zh: 功成名遂而身退，天之道也。」

translations.literal: When merit is done and name achieved, retire the body—this is Heaven's Way.'"
translations.idiomatic: When the work is done, step back—that is Heaven's Way.'"

sentences.zh: 注[五]太公金匱曰：「武王曰：『吾欲造起居之誡，隨之以身。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: The Grand Duke's *Metal Coffer* says: 'King Wu said: "I wish to make precepts for rising and resting and follow them with my person.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Jin gui* quotes King Wu wanting daily maxims at hand.

sentences.zh: 』幾之書曰：『安無忘危，存無忘亡，孰惟二者，必後無凶。

translations.literal: "' The writing on the armrest says: "In peace forget not danger; in survival forget not ruin; whoever holds these two will afterward have no misfortune.'
translations.idiomatic: The armrest inscription: in peace remember peril, in life remember ruin—keep both and stay safe.'

sentences.zh: 』杖之書曰：『輔人無苟，扶人無*

translations.literal: "' The staff inscription says: "Help others without laxity, support others without *
translations.idiomatic: The staff says: aid others without slack, uphold others without *

translations.literal: (indulgence)
translations.idiomatic: (indulgence)
**fault.*'"'

sentences.zh: 』」墨子曰：「堯、舜、禹、湯書其事於竹帛，瑑之盤盂。

translations.literal: "'" Mozi says: 'Yao, Shun, Yu, and Tang wrote their deeds on bamboo and silk and carved them on plates and bowls.'
translations.idiomatic: Mozi says the sage-kings engraved their lessons on bamboo, silk, and bronze vessels.'

sentences.zh: 」杅亦盂也。

translations.literal: " Yu also means bowl.
translations.idiomatic: *Yu* here is the same as a ritual bowl.

sentences.zh: 及憲為車騎將軍，辟駰為掾。

translations.literal: When Xian became chariot-and-cavalry general, he summoned Yin as clerk.
translations.idiomatic: When Dou Xian took the chariot generalcy, he appointed Yin to his staff.

sentences.zh: 憲府貴重，掾屬三十人，皆故刺史、二千石，唯駰以處士年少，擢在其閒。

translations.literal: Xian's headquarters were exalted; there were thirty clerks, all former inspectors or two-thousand-dan officials—only Yin, as a young common scholar, was raised to stand among them.
translations.idiomatic: Thirty staffers, all ex-governors—only Yin, young and unsalaried, was pulled into their midst.

sentences.zh: 憲擅權驕恣，駰數諫之。

translations.literal: Xian monopolized power and grew arrogant; Yin several times remonstrated.
translations.idiomatic: Xian abused his power; Yin remonstrated again and again.

sentences.zh: 及出擊匈奴，道路愈多不法，駰為主簿，前後奏記數十，指切長短。

translations.literal: When he marched out to strike the Xiongnu, along the road lawlessness grew; Yin as chief secretary submitted memorials in succession—several tens—pointing out strengths and faults.
translations.idiomatic: On the northern campaign Yin as chief clerk filed dozens of memos exposing abuses.

sentences.zh: 憲不能容，稍□之，因察駰高第，出為長岑長。

translations.literal: Xian could not bear it and gradually [squeezed] him; then, finding Yin high-ranked in the assessment, sent him out as magistrate of Changcen.
translations.idiomatic: Xian could not stomach him, found a pretext, and posted him to distant Changcen.

sentences.zh: [一]駰自以遠去，不得意，遂不之官而歸。
