translations.literal: [Note one] Yin, thinking himself banished far off and frustrated, thereupon did not go to his post but returned home.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Yin took the post as exile, refused the appointment, and went home.

sentences.zh: 永元四年，卒於家。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Yongyuan he died at home.
translations.idiomatic: He died at home in Yongyuan 4 (92 CE).

sentences.zh: 所著詩、賦、銘、頌、書、記、表、七依、婚禮結言、達旨、酒警合二十一篇。

translations.literal: His writings—poems, fu, inscriptions, hymns, letters, records, memorials, *Seven Reliances*, *Wedding Knot Words*, *Penetrating the Intent*, and *Wine Warning*—total twenty-one pieces.
translations.idiomatic: His corpus—verse, rhapsodies, inscriptions, hymns, letters, records, memorials, and shorter pieces—runs to twenty-one titles.

sentences.zh: 中子瑗。

translations.literal: His middle son was Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: His second son was Cui Yuan.

sentences.zh: 注[一]長岑，縣，屬樂浪郡，其地在遼東。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Changcen is a county in Lelang commandery; its territory lay in Liaodong.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Changcen county belonged to Lelang, in Liaodong.

sentences.zh: 瑗字子玉，早孤，銳志好學，盡能傳其父業。

translations.literal: Yuan, courtesy name Ziyu, lost his father early; he was keen-willed and loved learning and fully transmitted his father's occupation.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Yuan, styled Ziyu, was orphaned young but devoured books and mastered his father's learning.

sentences.zh: 年十八，至京師，從侍中賈逵質正大義，逵善待之，瑗因留遊學，遂明天官、歷數、京房易傳、六日七分。

translations.literal: At age eighteen he reached the capital, followed Palace Attendant Jia Kui to query the great meanings, and Kui treated him well; Yuan therefore stayed to wander and study, and thereupon clarified celestial offices, calendrical reckoning, Jing Fang's *Changes* transmission, and the six-day seven-part method.
translations.idiomatic: At eighteen he studied in the capital under Jia Kui, who taught him the great canons; he stayed on to master astronomy, calendrics, and Jing Fang's *Yi* with its sexagenary fractions.

sentences.zh: [一] 諸儒宗之。

translations.literal: [Note one] The ru took him as their authority.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Confucian scholars hailed him as their master.

sentences.zh: 與扶風馬融、南陽張衡特相友好。

translations.literal: With Ma Rong of Fufeng and Zhang Heng of Nanyang he was especially friendly.
translations.idiomatic: He was closest to Ma Rong and Zhang Heng.

sentences.zh: 初，瑗兄章為州人所殺，瑗手刃報仇，因亡命。

translations.literal: Earlier, Yuan's elder brother Zhang had been killed by men of the province; Yuan with his own hand avenged him and then fled as an outlaw.
translations.idiomatic: He had killed a man to avenge his murdered brother Zhang and fled the law.

sentences.zh: 會赦，歸家。

translations.literal: When an amnesty came, he returned home.
translations.idiomatic: An amnesty let him return.

sentences.zh: 家貧，兄弟同居數十年，鄉邑化之。

translations.literal: The family was poor; brothers lived together several decades, and the district was transformed by it.
translations.idiomatic: The brothers kept a poor household together for decades and set the tone for the village.

sentences.zh: 注[一]解見郎顗傳。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Explanation seen in the biography of Lang Yi.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Technical gloss in Lang Yi's biography.

sentences.zh: 年四十餘，始為郡吏。

translations.literal: Past forty he first became a commandery clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Not until after forty did he take a minor county post.

sentences.zh: 以事系東郡發乾獄。

translations.literal: Because of an affair he was bound in the Fagan prison of Dong commandery.
translations.idiomatic: A lawsuit landed him in Fagan jail in Dong commandery.

sentences.zh: [一]獄掾善為禮，瑗閒考訊時，輒問以禮說。

translations.literal: [Note one] The prison clerk was skilled in ritual; whenever Yuan had intervals in examination, he would ask him about ritual doctrine.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Even under interrogation he questioned the jailer on ritual texts.

sentences.zh: 其專心好學，雖顛沛必於是。

translations.literal: His single-minded love of learning—even in extremity he was thus.
translations.idiomatic: His love of learning did not flag in chains.

sentences.zh: 後事釋歸家，為度遼將軍鄧遵所辟。

translations.literal: Later, when the affair was cleared, he returned home and was summoned by the general on the Liaodong frontier, Deng Zun.
translations.idiomatic: Cleared, he was recruited by Deng Zun, the Liaodong general.

sentences.zh: 居無何，遵被誅，瑗免歸。

translations.literal: Before long Zun was executed; Yuan was dismissed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: Soon Deng Zun died at law; Yuan went home dismissed.

sentences.zh: 注[一]發乾縣之獄也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: It means the prison of Fagan county.
translations.idiomatic: Note: the jail at Fagan.

sentences.zh: 注[一]呂後立惠帝后宮子為少帝，周勃廢之也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Empress Lü set the palace son of Emperor Hui as young emperor; Zhou Bo deposed him.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Lü's puppet emperor deposed by Zhou Bo.

sentences.zh: 注[二]元，大也。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Yuan means great.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Yuan* means 'chief' or 'great.'

sentences.zh: 書曰：「元惡大憝。」

translations.literal: The *Documents* says: 'The chief villain, greatly hated.'"
translations.idiomatic: The *Shang shu* line on the arch-villain.'"

sentences.zh: 注[三]史記蔡澤說范睢曰：「君獨不觀夫博者乎？

translations.literal: Comment on note three: The *Records* has Cai Ze persuading Fan Ju: 'Have you alone not watched the dice-player?'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Shiji* quotes Cai Ze's dice metaphor to Fan Ju.

sentences.zh: 或欲大投，或欲分功。

translations.literal: Some wish for a great cast, some wish to divide the gain.
translations.idiomatic: Some players risk all, some play for steady shares.

sentences.zh: 今君相秦，坐制諸侯，使天下皆畏秦，此亦秦分功之時也。」

translations.literal: Now you, minister to Qin, sit controlling the feudal lords and make all the world fear Qin—this too is the time for Qin to divide the gain.'"
translations.idiomatic: You rule Qin and terrify the lords—yet that is also when Qin should share power.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]第，但也。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: Di means only.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Di* means 'merely' or 'only.'

sentences.zh: 司馬相如*[傳]*曰：「第如臨邛。」

translations.literal: Sima Xiangru's *[biography]* says: 'Only as at Linqiong.'"
translations.idiomatic: Sima Xiangru uses *di* in the sense of 'simply.'"

sentences.zh: 久之，大將軍梁商初開莫府，復首辟瑗。

translations.literal: After a long while, when Grand General Liang Shang first opened his headquarters, he again first summoned Yuan.
translations.idiomatic: Years later Liang Shang opened a new staff and called Yuan first.

sentences.zh: 自以再為貴戚吏，不遇被斥，遂以疾固辭。

translations.literal: He himself thought that twice serving as clerk to powerful affines, yet meeting dismissal, he therefore pleaded illness and firmly declined.
translations.idiomatic: Twice burned as an in-law's aide, he pleaded sickness and refused.

sentences.zh: 歲中舉茂才，遷汲令。

translations.literal: Within the year he was nominated flourishing talent and promoted magistrate of Ji.
translations.idiomatic: That year he was nominated *maocai* and made magistrate of Ji.

sentences.zh: [一]在事數言便宜，為人開稻田數百頃。

translations.literal: [Note one] In office he repeatedly proposed practical measures, opening several hundred qing of paddy fields for the people.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] As magistrate he opened hundreds of qing of irrigated fields.

sentences.zh: 視事七年，百姓歌之。

translations.literal: After seven years in office the common people sang his praise.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years in the county won him popular songs.

sentences.zh: 注[一]汲，縣名，屬河內。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Ji is a county name belonging to Henei.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Ji county lay in Henei commandery.

sentences.zh: 漢安初，大司農胡廣、少府竇章共薦瑗宿德大儒，從政有多，不宜久在下位，由此遷濟北相。

translations.literal: In the early Han'an era, Grand Minister of Agriculture Hu Guang and privy treasurer Dou Zhang jointly recommended Yuan as a senior ru of long-tested virtue whose administration showed many gains—he should not long stay in low position—and on that account he was promoted grand minister of Jibei.
translations.idiomatic: In Han'an, Hu Guang and Dou Zhang memorialized him as a veteran scholar and able official; the court named him chancellor of Jibei.

sentences.zh: 時李固為太山太守，美瑗文雅，奉書禮致殷勤。

translations.literal: At that time Li Gu was grand administrator of Taishan; he admired Yuan's refinement and sent letters and gifts with earnest courtesy.
translations.idiomatic: Li Gu, then Taishan governor, courted him with letters and gifts.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，光祿大夫杜喬為八使，徇行郡國，[一]以臧罪奏瑗，征詣廷尉。

translations.literal: After more than a year, Palace Attendant Du Qiao, as one of the eight envoys, toured commanderies and kingdoms, [note one] and on a corruption charge memorialized Yuan; he was summoned to the commandant of justice.
translations.idiomatic: A year later Du Qiao, touring as inspector, impeached him for graft and hauled him to the capital judges.

sentences.zh: 瑗上書自訟，得理出。

translations.literal: Yuan submitted a memorial in his own defense and, the principle being established, was released.
translations.idiomatic: He memorialized in his own defense and won acquittal.

sentences.zh: 會病卒，年六十六。

translations.literal: He happened to fall ill and die, age sixty-six.
translations.idiomatic: He then fell ill and died at sixty-six.

sentences.zh: 臨終，顧命子寔曰：「夫人稟天地之氣以生，及其終也，歸精於天，還骨於地。

translations.literal: Facing death, he gave his son Shi final instructions, saying: 'Man receives the qi of Heaven and Earth to be born; when he ends, he returns his essence to Heaven and his bones to earth.'
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed he told his son Shi: 'We are born of heaven and earth's breath; at death essence flies to heaven and bone sinks to earth.'

sentences.zh: 何地不可臧形骸，勿歸鄉里。

translations.literal: What place cannot house the bodily frame—do not return to the native village.
translations.idiomatic: The body may rest anywhere; do not carry my bones home.

sentences.zh: 其賵贈之物，羊豕之奠，一不得受。

translations.literal: As for condolence gifts and sheep-and-pig offerings, not a single one may be accepted.
translations.idiomatic: Refuse every funeral gift and every sacrificial animal sent in my name.

sentences.zh: 」寔奉遺令，遂留葬洛陽。

translations.literal: "' Shi obeyed the final command and therefore had him buried, staying in Luoyang.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Shi followed his father's will and laid him to rest in Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 注[一]八使見周舉傳。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The eight envoys are seen in the biography of Zhou Ju.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The eight inspectors appear in Zhou Ju's biography.

sentences.zh: 瑗高於文辭，尤善為書、記、箴、銘，所著賦、碑、銘、箴、頌、七蘇、[一] 南陽文學官志、歎辭、移社文、悔祈、草書埶、七言，凡五十七篇。

translations.literal: Yuan excelled at literary phrasing and was especially skilled at letters, records, admonitions, and inscriptions; his writings—fu, stele texts, inscriptions, admonitions, hymns, *Seven Su*, [note one] *Record of the Nanyang Erudite Office*, laments, *Text on Moving the Earth Altar*, *Repentant Prayer*, *Form of Cursive Script*, and heptasyllabic verse—total fifty-seven pieces.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Yuan was a master of belles lettres—memorials, stele inscriptions, and admonitions—and left fifty-seven works including the famous *Nanyang professor's office* memoir.

sentences.zh: 其南陽文學官志稱於後世，諸能為文者皆自以弗及。

translations.literal: His *Record of the Nanyang Erudite Office* was praised by later ages; all who could write felt they could not match it.
translations.idiomatic: Later generations ranked his *Nanyang literary office* piece above their own efforts.

sentences.zh: 瑗愛士，好賓客，盛修餚膳，單極滋味，不問余產。

translations.literal: Yuan loved gentlemen of talent and delighted in guests; he lavishly prepared meats and dishes, exhausting every flavor, and paid no heed to remaining property.
translations.idiomatic: He loved scholars and feasted guests sumptuously, caring nothing for his estate.

sentences.zh: 居常蔬食菜羹而已。

translations.literal: In daily life he ate only vegetables and vegetable broth.
translations.idiomatic: For himself he lived on greens and gruel.

sentences.zh: 家無擔石儲，當世清之。

translations.literal: The household had not a picul of stored grain; contemporaries deemed him pure.
translations.idiomatic: He kept no granary; his peers called him incorruptible.

translations.literal: [Note two]
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] See following gloss.

sentences.zh: 注[一]瑗集載其文，即枚乘七發之流。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Yuan's collected works carry the text—it is of the class of Mei Cheng's *Seven Stimuli*.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Seven Su* in his corpus follows the *Seven Stimuli* genre.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書曰「瑗愛士，好賓客，盛修餚膳。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Hua Qiao's book says: 'Yuan loved gentlemen of talent and delighted in guests; he lavishly prepared meats and dishes.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Hua Qiao records Yuan's lavish hospitality.

sentences.zh: 或言其太奢。

translations.literal: Some said it was too extravagant.
translations.idiomatic: Critics called it excessive.

sentences.zh: 瑗聞之怒，□妻子曰：『吾并日而食，以供賓客，而反以獲譏，士大夫不足養如此。

translations.literal: When Yuan heard, he was angry and [said] to wife and children: 'I eat once every two days to supply guests, yet in return I meet ridicule—gentlemen are not worth nurturing like this.'
translations.idiomatic: Yuan grew angry and told his family he skipped meals to feed guests yet earned mockery.

sentences.zh: 後勿過菜具，無為諸子所蚩也。

translations.literal: Afterward do not exceed vegetable dishes; do not be laughed at by the boys.'
translations.idiomatic: He ordered simpler fare lest his sons ridicule him.'

sentences.zh: 』終不能改，奉祿盡於賓饗」也。

translations.literal: "' In the end he could not change; his salary was exhausted on feasting guests.'"
translations.idiomatic: He never mended his ways and spent his whole stipend on hospitality.'"

sentences.zh: 瑗子寔

translations.literal: Yuan's son was Shi.
translations.idiomatic: His son was Cui Shi.

sentences.zh: 寔字子真，一名台，字符始。

translations.literal: Shi, courtesy name Zizhen, also bore the name Tai, courtesy name Fushi.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Shi, styled Zizhen, was also known as Tai, with the courtesy name Fushi.

sentences.zh: 少沉靜，好典籍。

translations.literal: In youth he was deep and still and loved the canon.
translations.idiomatic: From boyhood he was grave and bookish.

sentences.zh: 父卒，隱居墓側。

translations.literal: When his father died, he dwelt in seclusion beside the tomb.
translations.idiomatic: He mourned at his father's grave.

sentences.zh: 服竟，三公並辟，皆不就。

translations.literal: When mourning was complete, the three dukes together summoned him; he did not go to any.
translations.idiomatic: After the mourning period the three highest offices called him; he refused all.

sentences.zh: 桓帝初，詔公卿郡國舉至孝獨行之士。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Huan's reign, an edict ordered dukes, ministers, commanderies, and kingdoms to nominate men of utmost filial piety and singular conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan ordered nominations for extreme filial piety and outstanding conduct.

sentences.zh: 寔以郡舉，征詣公車，病不對策，除為郎。

translations.literal: Shi, nominated by his commandery, was summoned to the public carriage office; ill, he did not answer the policy questions and was appointed gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: The commandery sent him to the capital; pleading sickness he skipped the examination and was made a court gentleman.

sentences.zh: 明於政體，吏才有餘，論當世便事數十條，名曰政論。

translations.literal: He was clear on the body politic and had surplus administrative talent; he discussed several score practical measures for the age in a work entitled *Discourses on Government*.
translations.idiomatic: He understood statecraft and wrote the *Zheng lun* with dozens of practical reforms.

sentences.zh: 指切時要，言辯而確，[一]當世稱之。

translations.literal: He pointed to the pressing needs of the hour; his words were eloquent yet firm, [note one] and the age praised him.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] His prose was trenchant and the world admired it.

sentences.zh: 仲長統曰：「凡為人主，宜寫一通，置之坐側。

translations.literal: Zhongchang Tong said: 'Every ruler ought to copy out one scroll and place it at his seat.'
translations.idiomatic: Zhongchang Tong said every emperor should keep a copy of the *Zheng lun* at hand.

sentences.zh: 」其辭曰：

translations.literal: " The words thereof say:
translations.idiomatic: It opens:

sentences.zh: 注[一]確，堅正也，音口角反。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Que means firm and correct; it is read with initial k and final kok in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Que* means solid and straight; fanqie is given.

sentences.zh: 自堯舜之帝，湯武之王，皆賴明哲之佐，博物之臣。

translations.literal: From the emperors Yao and Shun, the kings Tang and Wu—all have relied on wise and clear assistants and ministers broad in learning.
translations.idiomatic: From Yao and Shun through Tang and Wu, every sage ruler leaned on wise, learned ministers.

sentences.zh: 故戲陶陳謨而唐虞以興，伊、箕作訓而殷周用隆。

translations.literal: Therefore Gao Yao set forth his counsels and Tang and Yu rose; Yi Yin and Ji Zi made their instructions and Yin and Zhou flourished.
translations.idiomatic: Gao Yao's plans built Yao and Shun; Yi Yin and the Viscount of Ji instructed Shang and Zhou to glory.

sentences.zh: [一]及繼體之君，欲立中興之功者，曷嘗不賴賢哲之謀乎！

translations.literal: [Note one] When succeeding-body lords wish to establish Restoration merit, have they ever not relied on worthy and wise stratagems!
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] No restoration happens without the counsel of the wise.

sentences.zh: 凡天下所以不理者，常由人主承平日久，俗漸敝而不悟，政寖衰而不改，習亂安危，怢不自鶯。

translations.literal: In general, the reason All under Heaven is not well ordered is always that the lord of men, long in unbroken peace, lets custom gradually decay without waking, lets government slowly wane without reforming, grows used to disorder and forgets danger, and is heedless without self-warning.
translations.idiomatic: The realm falls into disorder when a long peace dulls the ruler: custom rots, policy slackens, he mistakes peril for safety and never stirs.

sentences.zh: [二]或荒耽嗜欲，不恤萬機；

translations.literal: [Note two] Some abandon themselves to appetites and do not pity the myriad engines of rule;
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] some wallow in pleasure and ignore the business of state;

sentences.zh: 或耳蔽箴誨，厭偽忽真；

translations.literal: or their ears are blocked to admonition and they tire of the false and neglect the true;
translations.idiomatic: some shut their ears to remonstrance and prefer flattery to truth;

sentences.zh: [三] 或猶豫歧路，莫適所從；

translations.literal: [note three] or they hesitate at a forked road and know not which way to go;
translations.idiomatic: [note three] some dither at the crossroads, unable to choose;

sentences.zh: 或見信之佐，括囊守祿；

translations.literal: or trusted assistants tie the bag shut and guard their stipends;
translations.idiomatic: some trusted aides purse their lips and cling to salary;

sentences.zh: [四]或□遠之臣，言以賤廢。

translations.literal: [note four] or distant ministers, because their words are base, are cast aside.
translations.idiomatic: [note four] and distant advisers are dismissed for low rank.

sentences.zh: 是以王綱縱□於上，智士郁伊于下。

translations.literal: Therefore the royal net is slack above while wise men choke with grief below.
translations.idiomatic: So the bonds of rule fray aloft while talent smothers below.

sentences.zh: [五]悲夫！

translations.literal: [Note five] Alas!
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] Alas!

sentences.zh: 注[一]伊尹作伊訓，箕子作洪範。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Yi Yin made the *Yi xun*; the Viscount of Ji made the *Hong fan*.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Yi Yin's *Yi xun* and the Viscount of Ji's *Hong fan*.

sentences.zh: 注[二]怢音他沒反。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Tu is read with initial t and final mot in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for *tu*.

sentences.zh: 怢，忽忘也。

translations.literal: Tu means to forget heedlessly.
translations.idiomatic: *Tu* means to forget carelessly.

sentences.zh: 注[三]厭飫奸偽，輕忽至真。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Weary of fed-on treachery, they slight the utmost truth.
translations.idiomatic: Note: cloyed on falsehood, they scorn the genuine.

sentences.zh: 注[四]易曰：「恬囊無咎無譽。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: The *Changes* says: 'Tie the bag—no blame, no praise.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Kun* line on tying the mouth of the sack.

sentences.zh: 」括，結也。

translations.literal: " Kuo means to tie.
translations.idiomatic: *Kuo* means to bind or knot.

sentences.zh: 結囊不言，持祿而已。

translations.literal: Tie the bag and do not speak—merely hold the stipend.
translations.idiomatic: They seal their lips and draw pay.

sentences.zh: 注[五]郁伊，不申之貌。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Yu yi is the appearance of not getting through.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Yu yi* means stifled, unable to speak out.

sentences.zh: 楚詞曰「獨郁伊而誰語」也。

translations.literal: The *Songs of Chu* says: 'Alone, stifled—whom shall I tell?'"
translations.idiomatic: *Chuci* has the line on choked solitude.'"

sentences.zh: 自漢興以來，三百五十餘歲矣。

translations.literal: Since Han arose, more than three hundred and fifty years have passed.
translations.idiomatic: Han has stood for over three hundred fifty years.

sentences.zh: 政令垢翫，上下怠懈，[一]風俗雕敝，人庶巧偽，百姓囂然，鹹復思中興之救矣。

translations.literal: Edicts and orders are worn smooth; high and low grow slack and remiss, [note one] custom is carved thin and the people are crafty and false; the hundred surnames clamor—all long again for the saving of a restoration.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Laws grow stale, officials lazy, manners coarse, the people cunning—the crowd cries out for a new restoration.

sentences.zh: 且濟時拯世之術，豈必體堯蹈舜然後乃理哉？

translations.literal: Moreover, the art of aiding the age and saving the world—must one embody Yao and tread Shun only then to bring order?
translations.idiomatic: Must rescuing the times mean aping Yao and Shun?

sentences.zh: 期於補□決壞，枝柱邪傾，[二]隨形裁割，要措斯世於安寧之域而已。

translations.literal: The aim is to patch the breach and break the dam, prop the leaning post, [note two] cut according to the shape, and in sum set this age in the domain of peace—that is all.
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] One need only plug leaks, shore the tilt, trim policy to facts, and settle the realm in peace.

sentences.zh: 故聖人執權，遭時定制，[三]步驟之差，各有雲設。

translations.literal: Therefore the sage holds the scales, meets the time, and fixes institutions, [note three] the steps may differ but each has its cloud-like array of measures.
translations.idiomatic: [Note three] The sage weighs circumstance and frames law; each age sets its own pace.

sentences.zh: 不強人以不能，背急切而慕所聞也。

translations.literal: He does not force on others what they cannot do, nor turn his back on pressing needs to admire what he has merely heard.
translations.idiomatic: He does not demand the impossible nor chase distant models while ignoring the crisis at hand.

sentences.zh: [四]蓋孔子對葉公以來遠，哀公以臨人，景公以節禮，非其不同，所急異務也。

translations.literal: [Note four] Confucius answered Duke Ye on winning the distant, Duke Ai on overseeing the people, Duke Jing on restraining ritual—it was not that they differed, but that the urgencies of their tasks differed.
translations.idiomatic: [Note four] Confucius gave Ye, Ai, and Jing different answers because each state needed something different.

sentences.zh: [五]是以受命之君，每輒創製；

translations.literal: [Note five] Therefore every lord who receives the mandate constantly creates new systems;
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] Founders always innovate;

sentences.zh: 中興之主，亦匡時失。

translations.literal: restoration lords also correct the errors of their time.
translations.idiomatic: restoring sovereigns patch the faults of their day.

sentences.zh: 昔盤庚愍殷，遷都易民；

translations.literal: Formerly Pangeng pitied Yin, moved the capital and changed the people;
translations.idiomatic: Pangeng pitied Yin and moved the people to a new capital;

sentences.zh: [六]周穆有闕，甫侯正刑。

translations.literal: [Note six] King Mu of Zhou had faults; the Marquis of Fu corrected the punishments.
translations.idiomatic: [Note six] King Mu erred until the Marquis of Fu set punishments right.

sentences.zh: [七]俗人拘文牽古，不達權制，奇偉所聞，簡忽所見，烏可與論國家之大事哉！

translations.literal: [Note seven] Vulgar men cling to text and drag antiquity behind them; they do not grasp expedient regulation; they marvel at what they have heard in tales and slight what they have seen—how can one discuss great affairs of state with them!
translations.idiomatic: [Note seven] Pedants hug the classics and ignore practical rule; they swoon at rumor and scorn evidence—unfit to debate governance!

sentences.zh: 故言事者，雖合聖德，輒見掎奪。

translations.literal: Therefore those who speak on affairs, though their words match sage virtue, are always pulled back and thwarted.
translations.idiomatic: So even sage-like counsel gets blocked.

sentences.zh: [八] 何者？

translations.literal: [Note eight] Why?
translations.idiomatic: [Note eight] Why?

sentences.zh: 其頑士闇於時權，安習所見，不知樂成，況可慮始，[九]苟雲率由舊章而已。

translations.literal: The obstinate gentry are blind to the scales of the time, comfortably habituated to what they have seen; they do not know how to rejoice in completion, still less how to plan beginnings, [note nine] and idly say 'only follow the old statutes.'
translations.idiomatic: [Note nine] Obstinate scholars cling to habit, love finished projects, fear new starts, and chant 'keep the old code.'

sentences.zh: 其達者或矜名妒能，恥策非己，舞筆奮辭，以破其義，寡不勝觿，遂見擯□。

translations.literal: The perspicacious may pride themselves on name and envy others' ability, be ashamed that the plan is not their own, flourish brush and raise rhetoric to break its sense—the few cannot overcome the many and so are cast aside.
translations.idiomatic: Clever men jealous of rivals scribble rebuttals until sound counsel is shouted down.

sentences.zh: 雖稷、契復存，猶將困焉。

translations.literal: Though Ji and Xie lived again, they would still be trapped.
translations.idiomatic: Even Ji and Xie could not win in such a hall.

sentences.zh: 斯賈生之所以排於絳、灌，屈子之所以攄其幽憤者也。

translations.literal: This is why Jia Yi was blocked by Zhou and Guan, and why the Quzi poured out his hidden resentment.
translations.idiomatic: That is why Jia Yi fell to Zhou Bo and Guan Ying, and Qu Yuan nursed his *Li sao* grief.

sentences.zh: [一０]夫以文帝之明，賈生之賢，絳、灌之忠，而有此患，況其餘哉！

translations.literal: [Note ten] With Emperor Wen's clarity, Jia Yi's worth, Zhou and Guan's loyalty—yet this misfortune occurred; how much more the rest!
translations.idiomatic: [Note ten] If Wen, Jia Yi, and loyal ministers could not escape it, who can?

sentences.zh: 注[一]垢，惡也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Gou means foul.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Gou* means foul or worn.

sentences.zh: 注[二]□音直莧反，禮記曰：「衣裳□裂紉箴請補綴。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: [Graph missing] is read with initial d and final hwan in fanqie; the *Record of Rites* says: 'When robes and skirts are torn, take needle and thread and ask to mend them.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Gloss on patching torn garments from the *Li ji*.

sentences.zh: 」柱音陟主反。

translations.literal: " Zhu is read with initial tr and final ju in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for *zhu* (prop).

sentences.zh: 注[三]權謂變也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Quan means change.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Quan* means flexible adaptation.

sentences.zh: 遭遇其時而定法制，不循於舊也。

translations.literal: Meeting their time they fix laws and institutions; they do not follow the old.
translations.idiomatic: They set law to fit the moment rather than copying antiquity.

sentences.zh: 注[四]背當時之急切，而慕所聞之事，則非濟時之要。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: To turn one's back on the pressing needs of the present and admire what one has heard of is not the essential for aiding the age.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Chasing reputation while ignoring urgent need is no cure for the times.

sentences.zh: 注[五]韓子曰，葉公問政於仲尼。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Han Feizi says: Duke Ye asked Confucius about government.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Han Feizi cites Duke Ye's question.

sentences.zh: 仲尼曰：「政在悅近而來遠。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'Government lies in gladdening the near and attracting the distant.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius answered: gladden neighbors and draw the distant.

sentences.zh: 」魯哀公問政於仲尼。

translations.literal: " Duke Ai of Lu asked Confucius about government.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Ai asked about government.

sentences.zh: 仲尼曰：「政在選賢。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'Government lies in choosing the worthy.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said: pick the worthy.

sentences.zh: 」齊景公問政於仲尼。

translations.literal: " Duke Jing of Qi asked Confucius about government.
translations.idiomatic: Duke Jing of Qi asked the same.

sentences.zh: 仲尼曰：「政在節財。

translations.literal: Confucius said: 'Government lies in restraining wealth.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said: restrain expense.

sentences.zh: 」此云「臨人」「節禮」，文不同也。

translations.literal: " Here it says 'overseeing the people' and 'restraining ritual'—the wording differs.
translations.idiomatic: The *Zheng lun* varies the wording from the Analects citations.

sentences.zh: 注[六]盤庚，殷王也。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: Pangeng was a king of Yin.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Pangeng, Shang king who moved the capital.

sentences.zh: 自耿遷於亳邑，作書三篇以告之。

translations.literal: He moved from Geng to the Bo settlement and composed three documents to announce it.
translations.idiomatic: He moved from Geng to Bo and issued three proclamations.

sentences.zh: 注[七]甫侯即呂侯也。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: The Marquis of Fu is the Marquis of Lü.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Fu is the Lü marquis of Zhou.

sentences.zh: 為周穆王訓夏禹用刑之法。

translations.literal: He instructed King Mu of Zhou on the punishments used by Yu of Xia.
translations.idiomatic: He taught King Mu the Xia penal code.

sentences.zh: 並見尚書。

translations.literal: Both are seen in the *Documents*.
translations.idiomatic: Both stories are in the *Shang shu*.

sentences.zh: 注[八]掎音居蟻反。

translations.literal: Comment on note eight: Ji is read with initial k and final je in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for *ji* (to pull back).

sentences.zh: 賈逵注國語曰：「從後牽曰掎。」

translations.literal: Jia Kui's commentary on the *Discourses of the States* says: 'To pull from behind is ji.'"
translations.idiomatic: Jia Kui glosses *ji* as hauling from the rear.'"

sentences.zh: 注[九]前書劉歆曰：「夫可與樂成，難與慮始，此乃觿庶所為耳。」

translations.literal: Comment on note nine: The Former Documents records Liu Xin as saying: 'Men may share in rejoicing at completion but are hard to consult at the start—this is the conduct of the common herd.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: Liu Xin on the mob's love of success and fear of beginnings.'"

sentences.zh: 注[一０]孝文帝時，賈誼請更定律，令列侯就國，周勃、灌嬰等毀之。

translations.literal: Comment on note ten: Under Emperor Wen, Jia Yi asked to revise the laws and order the marquises to their fiefs; Zhou Bo and Guan Ying slandered him.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Jia Yi's reforms were destroyed by Zhou Bo and Guan Ying.

sentences.zh: 屈原為楚三閭大夫，上官靳尚妒害其能，憂愁憤懣，遂作離騷經。

translations.literal: Qu Yuan was *sanlü* grand officer of Chu; Shangguan and Jin Shang envied his ability; in sorrow and smoldering rage he therefore composed the *Li sao* classic.
translations.idiomatic: Qu Yuan served Chu until jealous ministers drove him to compose the *Li sao*.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左氏傳曰，息侯伐鄭，「不度德，不量力」。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The *Zuo Tradition* says: when the lord of Xi attacked Zheng, he 'did not measure virtue, did not measure strength.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zuo zhuan* on Xi's rash attack on Zheng.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]八*

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Eight *
translations.idiomatic: Note: Eight *

translations.literal: (generations)
translations.idiomatic: (generations)

sentences.zh: **[代]*謂三皇、五帝也。

translations.literal: **[ages]* means the Three August Ones and Five Thearchs.
translations.idiomatic: **ages* means the high antiquity of the Three August and Five Thearchs.

sentences.zh: 霸政謂齊桓、晉文也。

translations.literal: Hegemon government means Duke Huan of Qi and Duke Wen of Jin.
translations.idiomatic: Hegemon rule means Huan of Qi and Wen of Jin.

sentences.zh: 注[三]密，靜也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Mi means still or quiet.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Mi* means quiet.

sentences.zh: 注[四]墮讀曰隳。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: Duo is read like hui (to destroy).
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Duo* is read as *hui*, 'to ruin.'

sentences.zh: 注[五]左傳，齊桓公伐楚，責以包茅不貢，王祭不供；

translations.literal: Comment on note five: The *Zuo Tradition*: Duke Huan of Qi attacked Chu and reproached them for not presenting the wrapped reeds and for not supplying the king's sacrifices;
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zuo zhuan* on Huan's rebuke of Chu over tribute reeds;

sentences.zh: 晉文公召王盟諸侯於踐土；

translations.literal: Duke Wen of Jin summoned the king and covenanted with the feudal lords at Jiantu;
translations.idiomatic: Wen of Jin summoned the king to Jiantu;

sentences.zh: 管仲相公子糾而射桓公：此並權變之道也。

translations.literal: Guan Zhong served Prince Jiu and shot Duke Huan—these are all paths of expedient change.
translations.idiomatic: Guan Zhong once served a rival and shot Huan—examples of timely flexibility.

sentences.zh: 注[六]楚詞漁父曰「聖人不凝滯於物，而與時推移」也。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: The *Songs of Chu* *Fisherman* says: 'The sage is not stuck fast on things but shifts with the times.'"
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Chuci* has the fisherman tell Qu Yuan to move with the hour.'"

sentences.zh: 注[七]易曰：「上古結繩而化，後世聖人易之以書契。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: The *Changes* says: 'In high antiquity they knotted cords and brought order; later sages replaced them with documents and contracts.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Zhou yi* on replacing knotted cords with writing.

sentences.zh: 」干，盾也。

translations.literal: Gan means shield.
translations.idiomatic: *Gan* is the shield in the ritual dance.

sentences.zh: 戚，鉞也。

translations.literal: Qi means battle-axe.
translations.idiomatic: *Qi* is the axe.

sentences.zh: 尚書曰，苗人逆命，禹乃舞干羽於兩階，七旬有苗格。

translations.literal: The *Documents* says: the Miao people disobeyed the command; Yu therefore danced with shield and plumes on the two staircases, and after seventy days the Miao submitted.
translations.idiomatic: The *Shang shu* tells how Yu won the Miao with ritual dance, not arms.

sentences.zh: 前書，高祖被匈奴圍於平城，用陳平計得解。

translations.literal: The Former Documents records that the Exalted Ancestor was surrounded by the Xiongnu at Pingcheng and by Chen Ping's stratagem got free.
translations.idiomatic: Gaozu's Pingcheng siege was broken by Chen Ping's ruse.

sentences.zh: 言干戚之舞，非平城之所用也。

translations.literal: It means the shield-and-axe dance was not what was used at Pingcheng.
translations.idiomatic: The point: ritual dance is no parallel to Chen Ping's stratagem.

sentences.zh: 夫熊經鳥伸，雖延歷之術，非傷寒之理；

translations.literal: Bear-hang and bird-stretch, though they are arts for lengthening years, are not the principle for treating cold injury;
translations.idiomatic: Daoyin stretches may prolong life but do not cure typhoid cold;

sentences.zh: 呼吸吐納，雖度紀之道，非續骨之膏。

translations.literal: breathing out the old and taking in the new, though it is the way to extend one's span, is not a salve to knit broken bones.
translations.idiomatic: breath exercises may extend years but will not set a shattered bone.

sentences.zh: [一]蓋為國之法，有似理身，平則致養，疾則攻焉。

translations.literal: [Note one] In general the law of governing a state resembles treating a body: in peace one nourishes; when sick one attacks.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Rule the realm as you would a body: nourish in health, strike in fever.

sentences.zh: 夫刑罰者，治亂之藥石也；

translations.literal: Punishments are the mineral drugs for curing disorder;
translations.idiomatic: Penalties are the harsh physic for disorder;

sentences.zh: 注[一]莊子曰：「吹呴呼吸，吐故納新，熊經鳥伸，此導引之士，養形之人也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Zhuangzi says: 'Blow and breathe, expel the old and draw in the new, bear-hang and bird-stretch—these are the practitioners of daoyin, men who nurture the bodily form.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zhuangzi* lists the breathing and stretching arts.

sentences.zh: 」黃帝素問曰：「人傷於寒而轉為熱，何也？

translations.literal: " The *Yellow Emperor's Plain Questions* says: 'When man is injured by cold and it turns to heat, why is that?'
translations.idiomatic: The *Huangdi neijing* asks why cold injury turns to fever.

sentences.zh: 夫寒盛則生於熱也。

translations.literal: When cold is extreme, heat is born from it.
translations.idiomatic: Extreme cold breeds heat within.

sentences.zh: 」度紀猶延年也。

translations.literal: " Du ji means extending the years.
translations.idiomatic: *Du ji* means prolonging life.

sentences.zh: 言鳥伸不可療傷寒，吸氣不能續斷骨也。

translations.literal: It means bird-stretch cannot cure cold injury, and drawing breath cannot splice a broken bone.
translations.idiomatic: Bird-stretch will not cure shanghan; deep breathing will not fuse a fracture.

sentences.zh: 注[二]家語曰：「古者天子以德法為銜勒，以百官為轡策。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: The *Family Sayings* says: 'In antiquity the Son of Heaven took virtue and law as bit and bridle and the hundred officials as reins and whip.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Kongzi jiayu* compares rule to driving a team.

sentences.zh: 善御馬者，正銜勒，齊轡策，鈞馬力，和馬心，故口無聲而極千里。

translations.literal: One who is good at driving horses straightens the bit and bridle, aligns reins and whip, balances the horse's strength, harmonizes the horse's mind—therefore the mouth makes no sound yet reaches a thousand li.
translations.idiomatic: A good charioteer tunes tack and team in silence yet covers a thousand *li*.

sentences.zh: 善御人者，一其德法，正其百官，均齊人物，和安人心，故刑不用而天下化。

translations.literal: One who is good at governing men unifies their virtue and law, aligns the hundred officials, balances and levels the people, harmonizes and calms their hearts—therefore punishments are not used yet All under Heaven is transformed.
translations.idiomatic: A good ruler harmonizes law and hearts so punishments rest unused.

sentences.zh: 」說文曰：「駘，馬銜脫也。

translations.literal: " The *Shuowen* says: 'Dai: the horse's bit slips free.'
translations.idiomatic: *Shuowen* defines *dai* as a slipped bit.

sentences.zh: 」音達來反。

translations.literal: " It is read with initial d and final lai in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie spelling follows.

sentences.zh: 皇路，天路也。

translations.literal: Huang lu means the road of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: *Huang lu* is Heaven's highway.

sentences.zh: 注[三]何休注公羊傳曰：「柑，以木銜其口也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: He Xiu's commentary on the *Gongyang* says: 'Gan means to use wood to muzzle the mouth.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: He Xiu glosses *gan* as a wooden gag.

sentences.zh: 」柑音巨炎反。

translations.literal: " Gan is read with initial g and final yem in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie for *gan*.

sentences.zh: 勒，馬轡。

translations.literal: Le means horse reins.
translations.idiomatic: *Le* is the bridle leather.

sentences.zh: 輈，車轅。

translations.literal: Zhou means cart yoke.
translations.idiomatic: *Zhou* is the shaft yoke.

sentences.zh: 鞬猶束也。

translations.literal: Jian means to bind tight.
translations.idiomatic: *Jian* means to strap fast.

sentences.zh: 說苑曰：「鑾設於鑣，和設於軾，馬動*[則]*鑾鳴，鑾鳴則*[和]*應，行*[之]*節也。」

translations.literal: The *Garden of Persuasions* says: 'Bells are set on the biao, chimes on the crossbar; when the horse moves *then* the bells ring, when the bells ring *then* the chimes answer—this is the rhythm of motion.'"
translations.idiomatic: *Shuo yuan* on bells and chimes keeping the horses in cadence.'"

sentences.zh: 注[四]此以上並見前書刑法志。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: All the above is seen in the Former Documents *Treatise on Punishments*.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The precedents appear in the *Han shu* penal monograph.

sentences.zh: 注[五]式，法也。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: Shi means model.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Shi* means pattern or model.

sentences.zh: 注[六]畝百為夫，九夫為井。

translations.literal: Comment on note six: A hundred mu make a fu; nine fu make a jing.
translations.idiomatic: Note: The well-field reckoning—hundred *mu* per *fu*, nine *fu* per *jing*.

sentences.zh: 注[七]尚書曰：「簫韶九成，鳳皇來儀。

translations.literal: Comment on note seven: The *Documents* says: 'When the shao music reached its ninth repetition, phoenixes came in measured step.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: The *Shang shu* on the ninefold *shao* and the phoenixes.

sentences.zh: 」又「夔曰：『於余擊石拊石，百獸率舞。

translations.literal: " It also says: 'Kui said: "I strike the stones, I pat the stones, and the hundred beasts lead the dance.'"'
translations.idiomatic: Kui's line about stone chimes and the beasts dancing.'"

sentences.zh: 』」其後辟太尉袁湯、大將軍梁冀府，並不應。

translations.literal: "' Thereafter he was summoned to Grand Commandant Yuan Tang's headquarters and to Grand General Liang Ji's headquarters; both times he did not respond.
translations.idiomatic: Later Yuan Tang and Liang Ji called him to staff; he refused both.

sentences.zh: 大司農羊傅、少府何豹上書薦寔才美能高，宜在朝廷。

translations.literal: Grand Minister of Agriculture Yang Fu and privy treasurer He Bao jointly memorialized that Shi's talent was fine and his ability high—he should be at court.
translations.idiomatic: Yang Fu and He Bao urged the throne to employ Cui Shi at the capital.

sentences.zh: 召拜議郎，遷大將軍冀司馬，與邊韶、延篤等著作東觀。

translations.literal: He was summoned and appointed gentleman consultant, promoted to major under Grand General Ji, and with Bian Shao, Yan Du, and others wrote at the Eastern Pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: He became consultant, then Liang Ji's marshal, and edited at the Eastern Library with Bian Shao and Yan Du.

sentences.zh: 出為五原太守。

translations.literal: He went out as grand administrator of Wuyuan.
translations.idiomatic: He was posted to Wuyuan commandery.

sentences.zh: 五原土宜麻枲，而俗不知織績，民冬月無衣，積細草而臥其中，見吏則衣草而出。

translations.literal: Wuyuan's soil suited hemp and ramie, but custom did not know spinning and weaving; in winter the people had no clothes and piled fine grass to sleep in; when they saw officials they wore grass and came out.
translations.idiomatic: The land grew hemp but the people could not weave; in winter they burrowed in hay and met the magistrate in straw.

sentences.zh: 寔至官，斥賣儲峙，為作紡績、織□、綀縕之具以教之，民得以免寒苦。

translations.literal: When Shi reached his post, he sold stored grain and for them made spinning, weaving, and coarse-cloth tools to teach them; the people were thereby spared bitter cold.
translations.idiomatic: He sold official stores to buy looms and taught weaving so they could face winter.

sentences.zh: [一]是時胡虜連入雲中、朔方，殺略吏民，一歲至九奔命。

translations.literal: [Note one] At that time the Hu raiders repeatedly entered Yunzhong and Shuofang, killing and seizing clerks and commoners—within one year there were nine emergency calls to arms.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Barbarians raided Yunzhong and Shuofang nine times in a year.

sentences.zh: 寔整厲士馬，嚴烽候，虜不敢犯，常為邊最。

translations.literal: Shi drilled and sharpened soldiers and horses, made beacon watch strict, and the raiders did not dare attack; he was always first among the border commanderies.
translations.idiomatic: He hardened troops and beacons until the frontier ranked him first.

translations.literal: [Note two]
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] End of border note.

sentences.zh: 注[一]杜預注左傳曰：「織□，織布者。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: Du Yu's commentary on the *Zuo Tradition* says: 'Zhi bu is one who weaves cloth.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: Du Yu glosses the weaver's trade.

sentences.zh: 」孔安國論語注曰：「縕，枲也。」

translations.literal: " Kong Anguo's commentary on the *Analects* says: 'Yun means hemp.'"
translations.idiomatic: Kong Anguo says *yun* is hemp cloth.'"

sentences.zh: 注[二]最為第一。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Zui means ranked first.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Zui* means 'top of the list.'

sentences.zh: 以病徵，拜議郎，復與諸儒博士共雜定五經。

translations.literal: Recalled for illness, he was appointed gentleman consultant and again with the various ru and erudites jointly settled the text of the Five Classics.
translations.idiomatic: Recalled on sick leave as consultant, he helped collate the Five Classics.

sentences.zh: 會梁冀誅，寔以故吏免官，禁錮數年。

translations.literal: When Liang Ji was executed, Shi, as his former subordinate, was dismissed from office and confined for several years.
translations.idiomatic: Liang Ji's fall stripped him of rank for years as an old client.

sentences.zh: 時鮮卑數犯邊，詔三公舉威武謀略之士，司空黃瓊薦寔，拜遼東太守。

translations.literal: At that time the Xianbei repeatedly violated the border; an edict ordered the three dukes to nominate men of martial awesomeness and stratagem; Minister of Works Huang Qiong recommended Shi, and he was appointed grand administrator of Liaodong.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Qiong nominated him for Liaodong when Xianbei pressed the frontier.

sentences.zh: 行道，母劉氏病卒，上疏求歸葬行喪。

translations.literal: On the road his mother Lady Liu died of illness; he submitted a memorial begging to return for burial and mourning.
translations.idiomatic: His mother died en route; he asked leave to bury her and mourn.

sentences.zh: 母有母儀淑德，博覽書傳。

translations.literal: The mother had a mother's deportment and chaste virtue and read widely in documents and traditions.
translations.idiomatic: Lady Liu was a model mother and a reader.

sentences.zh: 初，寔在五原，常訓以臨民之政，寔之善績，母有其助焉。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Shi was in Wuyuan, she often instructed him in the government of the people; Shi's fine record had her help.
translations.idiomatic: She had coached his Wuyuan administration from the women's quarters.

sentences.zh: 服竟，召拜尚書。

translations.literal: When mourning was complete, he was summoned and appointed minister.
translations.idiomatic: After mourning the court named him minister.

sentences.zh: 寔以世方阻亂，稱疾不視事，數月免歸。

translations.literal: Shi, because the age was blocked by disorder, pleaded illness and did not conduct affairs; after several months he was dismissed and returned home.
translations.idiomatic: He pleaded sickness in a chaotic age and soon resigned.

sentences.zh: 初，寔父卒，剽賣田宅，起頤塋，立碑頌。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Shi's father died, he sold off fields and houses, raised a high tomb mound, and set up a stele with praise.
translations.idiomatic: For his father's funeral he sold land to build a grand tomb and stele.

sentences.zh: [一]葬訖，資產竭盡，因窮困，以酤釀販鬻為業。

translations.literal: [Note one] When burial was finished, his assets were exhausted; therefore in distress he took brewing and peddling as his occupation.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] The funeral beggared him, so he sold ale for a living.

sentences.zh: 時人多以*[此]*譏之，寔終不改。

translations.literal: People of the time mostly used *this* to ridicule him; Shi to the end did not change.
translations.idiomatic: Neighbors mocked him; he never changed course.

sentences.zh: 亦取足而已，不致盈餘。

translations.literal: He also only took enough and did not seek surplus.
translations.idiomatic: He earned only enough to get by.

sentences.zh: 及仕官，歷位邊郡，而愈貧薄。

translations.literal: When he held office and passed through posts on the border, he grew only poorer.
translations.idiomatic: Border posts left him thinner in purse than before.

sentences.zh: 建寧中病卒。

translations.literal: In the Jianning era he died of illness.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the Jianning years.

sentences.zh: 家徒四壁立，無以殯斂，光祿勳楊賜、太僕袁逢、少府段熲為備棺槨葬具，大鴻臚袁隗樹碑頌德。

translations.literal: The household was bare to the four walls; there was no means for encoffining; Palace Attendant Yang Ci, grand coachman Yuan Feng, and privy treasurer Duan Jiong provided coffin and burial gear, and grand herald Yuan Kui set up a stele praising his virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Friends paid for the coffin when his house could not; Yuan Kui wrote his stone.

sentences.zh: 所著碑、論、箴、銘、荅、七言、祠、文、表、記、書凡十五篇。

translations.literal: His writings—stele texts, disquisitions, admonitions, inscriptions, replies, heptasyllabic verse, temple texts, essays, memorials, records, and letters—total fifteen pieces.
translations.idiomatic: Fifteen works survive under his name.

sentences.zh: 注[一]廣雅曰：「剽，削也，音匹妙反。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The *Guangya* says: 'Piao means to slice off; it is read with initial p and final mew in fanqie.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Guangya* glosses *piao* as cutting away.

sentences.zh: 」一作「標」。

translations.literal: " One version writes 'biao.'"
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts read *biao* instead.'"

sentences.zh: 寔從兄烈

translations.literal: Shi's elder cousin was Lie.
translations.idiomatic: His cousin was Cui Lie.

sentences.zh: 寔從兄烈，有重名於北州，歷位郡守、九卿。

translations.literal: Shi's elder cousin Lie had heavy reputation in the northern provinces and passed through posts as grand administrator and among the nine ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Lie, famed in the north, rose to governor and minister.

sentences.zh: 靈帝時，開鴻都門榜賣官爵，公卿州郡下至黃綬各有差。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Ling the Hongdu Gate opened for posting sale of offices and noble ranks; from dukes and ministers down to commanderies, kingdoms, and yellow-ribbon posts each had its price.
translations.idiomatic: Lingdi put ranks up for sale at Hongdu from chancellors to county posts.

sentences.zh: 其富者則先入錢，貧者到官而後倍輸，或因常侍、阿保別自通達。

translations.literal: The rich paid money first; the poor, after reaching office, paid double, or else through regular attendants and wet nurses opened separate channels.
translations.idiomatic: Rich buyers prepaid; poor ones paid double after appointment, or bribed eunuchs and nurses.

sentences.zh: [一]是時段熲、樊陵、張溫等雖有功勤名譽，然皆先輸貨財而後登公位。

translations.literal: [Note one] At that time Duan Jiong, Fan Ling, Zhang Wen, and others, though they had merit, toil, and reputation, all first delivered goods and cash and afterward ascended to high office.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Even heroes like Duan Jiong bought their way up.

sentences.zh: 烈時因傅母入錢五百萬，得為司徒。

translations.literal: Lie at that time, through his wet nurse, paid five million cash and obtained the post of minister of education.
translations.idiomatic: Lie paid five million *cash* through his nurse and became minister of education.

sentences.zh: 及拜日，天子臨軒，百僚畢會。

translations.literal: On the day of appointment the Son of Heaven came to the gallery; the hundred officials all assembled.
translations.idiomatic: Investiture day brought the emperor to the hall with the full court.

sentences.zh: 帝顧謂親幸者曰：「悔不小靳，可至千萬。

translations.literal: The emperor looked toward his intimate favorites and said: 'I regret I did not drive a harder bargain—it could have reached ten million.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor muttered he should have asked ten million for the post.

sentences.zh: 」[二]程夫人於傍應曰：「崔公冀州名士，豈肯買官？

translations.literal: " [Note two] Lady Cheng at the side answered: 'Lord Cui is a famed gentleman of Ji Province—how could he buy an office?'
translations.idiomatic: [Note two] Lady Cheng snapped that a Ji worthy like Cui would not buy rank.

sentences.zh: 賴我得是，反不知姝邪！

translations.literal: Thanks to me he got this—do you not even know it is a fine thing!'
translations.idiomatic: She boasted that her pull had won him the seat.'

sentences.zh: 」[三]烈於是聲譽衰減。

translations.literal: " [Note three] Thereupon Lie's reputation and voice declined.
translations.idiomatic: [Note three] Her words ruined his name.

sentences.zh: 久之不自安，從容問其子鈞曰：「吾居三公，於議者何如？

translations.literal: After a long while he was not at ease; calmly he asked his son Jun: 'I occupy one of the three dukes—what do critics say?'
translations.idiomatic: Uneasy, he asked his son what the world said of his ministership.

sentences.zh: 」鈞曰：「大人少有英稱，歷位卿守，論者不謂不當為三公；

translations.literal: " Jun said: 'In his youth the great man had a heroic name and passed through minister and governor posts—critics did not say it was wrong for him to become one of the three dukes;'
translations.idiomatic: Jun said the world had never grudged him high office until now.

sentences.zh: 而今登其位，天下失望。

translations.literal: 'but now that you have ascended to this position, All under Heaven is disappointed.'
translations.idiomatic: Now that he had bought the seat, the empire was disappointed.'

sentences.zh: 」烈曰：「何為然也？」

translations.literal: " Lie said: 'Why is it so?'
translations.idiomatic: Lie asked why.

sentences.zh: 鈞曰：「論者嫌其銅臭。

translations.literal: Jun said: 'The critics find it reeks of copper.'
translations.idiomatic: Jun answered: 'They say you smell of copper coins.'

sentences.zh: 」烈怒，舉杖擊之。

translations.literal: " Lie grew angry and raised his staff to strike him.
translations.idiomatic: Lie swung his cane at him.

sentences.zh: 鈞時為虎賁中郎將，服武弁，戴鶡尾，狼狽而走。

translations.literal: Jun at that time was colonel of the tiger fierce guard; he wore the martial cap with pheasant tail and ran off in confusion.
translations.idiomatic: Jun, in tiger guard uniform with plumed cap, bolted.

sentences.zh: 烈罵曰：「死卒，父檛而走，孝乎？

translations.literal: Lie cursed: 'Dead soldier—when your father strikes you, you run—is that filial?'
translations.idiomatic: Lie shouted after the fleeing soldier-son.

sentences.zh: 」[四]鈞曰：「舜之事父，小杖則受，大杖則走，非不孝也。

translations.literal: " [Note four] Jun said: 'In Shun's serving his father, with a small staff he accepted it, with a great staff he ran—this is not unfilial.'"
translations.idiomatic: [Note four] Jun cited Shun's rule: endure light blows, flee heavy ones.'"

sentences.zh: 」[五]烈籩而止。

translations.literal: " [Note five] Lie thereupon felt shame and stopped.
translations.idiomatic: [Note five] Lie dropped the stick, ashamed.

sentences.zh: 烈後拜太尉。

translations.literal: Lie afterward was appointed grand commandant.
translations.idiomatic: He later rose to grand commandant.

sentences.zh: 注[一]阿保謂傅母也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: A-bao means wet nurse.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Abao* is the imperial nurse.

sentences.zh: 注[二]靳，固惜之也。

translations.literal: Comment on note two: Jin means to grudge sparingly.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Jin* means to regret underselling.

sentences.zh: 靳或作「傿」。

translations.literal: Jin is sometimes written 'jian.'
translations.idiomatic: Some texts write *jian* for *jin*.

sentences.zh: 說文曰：「傿，引為價也。

translations.literal: The *Shuowen* says: 'Jian means to draw out as a price.'
translations.idiomatic: *Shuowen* glosses *jian* as fixing a price.

sentences.zh: 」音一建反。

translations.literal: " It is read with initial y and final jen in fanqie.
translations.idiomatic: Fanqie follows.

sentences.zh: 注[三]姝，美也。

translations.literal: Comment on note three: Shu means beautiful.
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Shu* means lovely.

sentences.zh: 言反不知斯事之美也。

translations.literal: It means on the contrary he did not know this affair was beautiful.
translations.idiomatic: The barb: the emperor missed the 'beauty' of the deal.

sentences.zh: 姝或作「株」。

translations.literal: Shu is sometimes written 'zhu.'
translations.idiomatic: Variant graph *zhu*.

sentences.zh: 株，根本也。

translations.literal: Zhu means root.
translations.idiomatic: *Zhu* means 'root' or 'point.'

sentences.zh: 注[四]以其武官，故罵為卒。

translations.literal: Comment on note four: Because he was a military officer, therefore he cursed him as soldier.
translations.idiomatic: Note: Lie insulted the tiger guard as a common soldier.

sentences.zh: 或作「孔卒」者，誤也。

translations.literal: Some manuscripts write 'kong zu'—that is an error.
translations.idiomatic: The reading 'kong zu' is a scribal mistake.

sentences.zh: 注[五]家語曰：「曾子耘瓜，誤傷其根。

translations.literal: Comment on note five: The *Family Sayings* says: 'Master Zeng was hoeing melons and by mistake injured the root.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Jiayu* on Zengzi and the melon patch.

sentences.zh: 曾□怒，建大杖以擊其首。

translations.literal: Master Zeng [his father] grew angry and took up a great staff to strike his head.
translations.idiomatic: His father Zeng Xi beat him with a heavy staff.

sentences.zh: 曾子仆地不知人，有頃乃蘇。

translations.literal: Master Zeng fell to the ground unconscious; after a moment he revived.
translations.idiomatic: Zengzi collapsed and came to slowly.

sentences.zh: 孔子聞之怒，謂門弟子曰：『參來勿內也。

translations.literal: When Confucius heard of it he was angry and said to his gate disciples: 'When Shen comes, do not admit him.'"
translations.idiomatic: Confucius barred the gate to Zeng Shen for accepting that beating.'"

sentences.zh: 昔瞽叟有子曰舜，瞽叟欲使之，未嘗不往，則欲殺之，未嘗可得。

translations.literal: Formerly Blind Gu had a son named Shun; whenever Blind Gu wished to send him, he never failed to go, yet when Blind Gu wished to kill him, he could never succeed.
translations.idiomatic: The tale of Blind Gu and Shun: summoned, Shun always came; marked for death, he always escaped.

sentences.zh: 小棰則待，大杖則逃，不陷父於不義也。』」

translations.literal: With a small rod he waited; with a great rod he fled—thus he did not trap his father in unrighteousness.'"
translations.idiomatic: He bore light blows but fled heavy ones, so his father was never shamed as a murderer.'"

sentences.zh: 鈞少交結英豪，有名稱，為西河太守。

translations.literal: In youth Jun formed ties with heroes and had a reputation; he became grand administrator of Xihe.
translations.idiomatic: Jun befriended bold spirits and governed Xihe.

sentences.zh: 獻帝初，鈞與袁紹俱起兵山東，董卓以是收烈付郿獄，錮之，鋃鐺鐵鎖。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Emperor Xian's reign, Jun together with Yuan Shao both raised troops in the east of the mountains; on this account Dong Zhuo arrested Lie, handed him to Mei prison, confined him, and shackled him with iron langdang fetters.
translations.idiomatic: When Jun joined Yuan Shao against Dong Zhuo, Zhuo jailed Cui Lie in Mei in chains.

sentences.zh: [一]卓既誅，拜烈城門校尉。

translations.literal: [Note one] After Zhuo was executed, Lie was appointed colonel of the city gates.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] After Zhuo's death Lie became colonel of the city gates.

sentences.zh: 及李傕入長安，為亂兵所殺。

translations.literal: When Li Jue entered Chang'an, he was killed by mutinous troops.
translations.idiomatic: Mutineers slew him when Li Jue took Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 注[一]說文曰：「鋃鐺，鎖也。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The *Shuowen* says: 'Langdang means fetters.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Shuowen* defines *langdang* as chains.

sentences.zh: 」前書曰：「人犯鑄錢，以鐵鎖鋃鐺其頸。

translations.literal: " The Former Documents says: 'When men violated the coinage prohibition, they iron-shackled langdang their necks.'
translations.idiomatic: The *Han shu* describes felons collared with iron *langdang*.

sentences.zh: 」鋃音郎，鐺音當。

translations.literal: " Lang is read like lang; dang is read like dang.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss gives the readings for each syllable.

sentences.zh: 烈有文才，所著詩、書、教、頌等凡四篇。

translations.literal: Lie had literary talent; his writings—poems, letters, instructions, and hymns—total four pieces.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Lie left four literary works.

sentences.zh: 論曰：崔氏世有美才，兼以沉淪典籍，遂為儒家文林。

translations.literal: The disquisition says: The Cui clan for generations had fine talent and moreover sank deep in the canon; therefore they became the forest of letters among Confucian scholars.
translations.idiomatic: The historian writes: generation after generation the Cuis were fine scholars and became a grove of Confucian letters.

sentences.zh: 駰、瑗雖先盡心於貴戚，而能終之以居正，則其歸旨異夫進趣者乎！

translations.literal: Though Yin and Yuan first gave their hearts to powerful affines, they were able to end by holding to the straight—then their ultimate purpose differed from those who pressed for advancement!
translations.idiomatic: Yin and Yuan began in great-clan service but finished upright—unlike mere careerists.

sentences.zh: 李固，高絜之士也，與瑗□郡，奉贄以結好。

translations.literal: Li Gu was a man of lofty purity; he served in the same commandery as Yuan, presented a gift, and formed friendship.
translations.idiomatic: The fastidious Li Gu, posted with Cui Yuan, sent the formal gift and won his friendship.

sentences.zh: [一]由此知杜喬之劾，殆其過矣。

translations.literal: [Note one] From this one knows that Du Qiao's impeachment was probably excessive.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] So Du Qiao's charge against Yuan was likely unjust.

sentences.zh: 寔之政論，言當世理亂，雖□錯之徒不能過也。

translations.literal: Shi's *Discourses on Government* speaks of ordering and disorder in the present age—even a Chao Cuo could not surpass it.
translations.idiomatic: Cui Shi's *Zheng lun* on state and chaos outdoes even Chao Cuo.

sentences.zh: 注[一]儀禮曰：「士相見之禮，贄冬用雉，夏用腒，奉之曰：『某也欲見無由達。

translations.literal: Comment on note one: The *Etiquette and Rites* says: 'In the rite of gentlemen meeting, the gift in winter is a pheasant, in summer dried meat; presenting it they say: "So-and-so wishes an audience but has no means to reach you.'
translations.idiomatic: Note: *Yili* on the guest gift of pheasant or dried meat.

translations.literal: "' Ju is dried cured meat (summer guest gift).
translations.idiomatic: The summer gift *ju* is dried cured meat.

translations.literal: (The gloss repeats the character *ju*.)
translations.idiomatic: (The note repeats the character *ju*.)

translations.literal: **The glossed syllable is read by the fanqie spelling given in the note.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary gives a fanqie reading for the syllable.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：崔為文宗，世禪雕龍。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Cui is patriarch of letters; generation after generation they hand down the carved-dragon craft.
translations.idiomatic: The verse praises the Cuis as masters whose art passes like Zou Shi's 'carved dragon.'

sentences.zh: [一]建新恥潔，摧志求容。

translations.literal: [Note one] Jianxin shamed purity, broke his will to seek acceptance.
translations.idiomatic: [Note one] Zhuan under Wang Mang bent principle to survive.

sentences.zh: 永矣長岑，於遼之陰。

translations.literal: Far lies Changcen, on the north of the Liao.
translations.idiomatic: Banished to distant Changcen beyond the Liao.

sentences.zh: 不有直道，曷取泥沉。

translations.literal: Without the straight Way, how could mud and sinking be chosen?
translations.idiomatic: Without integrity, who would choose the mire?

translations.idiomatic: Cui Yuan scorned salary and avoided false disgrace.

sentences.zh: 子真持論，感起昏俗。

translations.idiomatic: Cui Shi (Zizhen) roused a dull age with his essays.

sentences.zh: 注[一]史記曰：「談天衍，雕龍奭。

sentences.zh: 一七０三頁三行諫刺史無與燕剌王通按：「刺史」之「刺」從朿，「剌王」之「剌」從束，二字音義並異，各本往往斗混。

translations.literal: Page 1703 line 3: remonstrated with the inspector not to associate with the Prince of Yan, Prince Li—Comment: the two graphs for 'inspector' and for the prince's epithet use different radicals and differ in sound and sense, and editions often confuse them.

translations.literal: Page 1703 line 6: Grand Guardian Zhen Feng—Comment: the Jijie cites Huang Shan: in the Former Documents *Wang Mang zhuan* Zhen Han was grand guardian and Feng was grand marshal of the right, not grand guardian; one of the characters *bao* or *feng* must be wrong.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Huang Shan doubts the title pairing Zhen Feng as grand guardian.

translations.literal: Page 1729 line 1: how could there be time to ring bells and harmonize the clear rhythm—Comment: 'clear' was miswritten 'request'; corrected directly from Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: corrected a miswriting of 'request' as 'clear'.

sentences.zh: 一七二九頁四行*[加]*笞與重罪無異據汲本、殿本補，與前志合。

translations.literal: *[add]* beating with the cane is no different from heavy crime—supplemented per Ji and Palace editions, matching the former treatise.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: added wording per Ji and Palace to match the penal treatise.

sentences.zh: 一七二九頁五行不可為*

translations.literal: Page 1729 line 5: cannot be *
translations.idiomatic: Collation fragment on 'cannot be a person'.

translations.literal: (A commoner)
translations.idiomatic: (A commoner)

sentences.zh: **[人]*按：校補案前志本作「不可為人」，此轉改之失。

translations.literal: **[person]*—Comment: collation notes the former treatise reads 'cannot be a person'; this is a transmission error.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: restores 'person' for a miswritten 'commoner' per the former treatise.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: emendation applied.

sentences.zh: 一七二九頁一四行皇路天路也按：汲本「天」作「大」。

translations.literal: Page 1729 line 14: huang lu means the road of Heaven—Comment: Ji edition writes 'great' for 'Heaven.'
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji edition reads 'great' where others read 'Heaven' for *huang lu*.

sentences.zh: 一七三０頁一行馬動*[則]*鑾鳴鑾鳴則*[和]*應據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1730 line 1: when the horse moves *[then]* the bells ring, when the bells ring *[then]* the chimes answer—supplemented per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: supplied conditional particles per Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一七三０頁一行行*[之]*節也據今本說苑補「之」字。

translations.literal: Page 1730 line 1: motion *[its]* rhythm—supplemented the particle from the received *Garden of Persuasions*.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: supplied possessive particle per received *Shuo yuan*.

sentences.zh: 按：汲本、殿本「節也」上無「行」字。

translations.literal: Comment: Ji and Palace editions lack the word 'motion' before 'rhythm.'
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Ji and Palace omit the word 'motion' before 'rhythm.'

sentences.zh: 一七三一頁四行時人多以*[此]*譏之據汲本、殿本補。

translations.literal: Page 1731 line 4: people of the time mostly used *[this]* to ridicule him—supplemented per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: supplied demonstrative 'this' per Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 一七三一頁四行及仕宦汲本、殿本「官」作「宦」，勘誤謂案文「宦」當作「官」。

translations.literal: Page 1731 line 4: when he held office—Ji and Palace editions write 'office' as 'court service'; the errata says by the text 'court service' should read 'office.'
translations.idiomatic: Collation: errata on office versus court-service graph.

sentences.zh: 按：集解引王會汾說，謂古書中言「仕宦」者甚多，「仕官」不成文理，此傳寫互誤，傳及注「宦」字當本作「官」，劉注當本作「官當作宦」。

translations.literal: Comment: Jijie cites Wang Huifen: ancient texts often say 'hold office and rank'; 'office' alone is unidiomatic—this is mutual scribal error; both text and commentary should read 'office,' while Liu's note should read the correction the other way.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: Wang Huifen on mutual error between office and court-service wording.

sentences.zh: 一七三一頁七行一作標按：「標」原斗「摽」，逕改正。

translations.literal: Page 1731 line 7: one version writes 'mark'—Comment: 'mark' was miswritten 'signal'; corrected directly.
translations.idiomatic: Collation: corrected a miswriting of 'signal' as 'mark'.

sentences.zh: 一七三二頁一行父檛而走按：汲本「檛」作「撾」。

translations.literal: Page 1732 line 1: father struck and he ran—Comment: Ji edition writes a different graph for 'strike.'
translations.idiomatic: Collation: variant graph for 'strike' in Ji edition.
